title: 7-foot-9 Olivier Rioux threw out an MLB first pitch and towered over everyone
description: Rioux would definitely have some extension if he became a pitcher.
fullText:
7-foot-9 Olivier Rioux threw out an MLB first pitch and towered over everyone originally appeared on The Sporting News. Add The Sporting News as a Preferred Source by clicking here.

Olivier Rioux is a college basketball national champion from his redshirt season with the Florida Gators.

After one more season at Florida, he transferred this offseason to UC-Irvine.

And with that transfer came a chance to throw out a ceremonial first pitch nearby his new home. The Los Angeles Angels of Anaheim welcomed Rioux out to the mound before their game on Tuesday night.

The important thing to mention: Rioux is 7-foot-9.

So his ceremonial first pitch was astounding:

UC Irvine’s Olivier Rioux is 7’9” 🤯

He threw the first pitch at Angel Stadium last night! pic.twitter.com/OjuZPSTvDG

That's Angels outfielder Wade Meckler with the honor of catching the first pitch, and then posing for a picture with Rioux.

It's even funnier as a visual because Meckler is just 5-foot-9. That's a whole two-foot difference between those two humans.

Rioux is a center, not a pitcher. But can you imagine?

He'd have incredible extension on the mound if he had solid pitching mechanics.

Maybe if the whole basketball thing doesn't work out, someone should see if they can turn Rioux into a pitcher.

MORE: Duke-bound Kager Knueppel is a problem

title: 4 biggest takeaways from Panthers GM Dan Morgan's pre-training camp press conference
description: Let's break down the biggest takeaways from Panthers GM Dan Morgan's pre-training camp chat on Wednesday.
fullText:
With rookies reporting to Charlotte yesterday and veterans back in today, the Carolina Panthers are almost set to begin their 2026 training camp. This summer could serve as another step towards another playoff push for the organization, which is fresh off capturing its first postseason berth since 2017 and their first NFC South title since 2015.

The main architect behind their recent success is president of football operations and general manager Dan Morgan, who hit the lectern for a pre-camp press conference on Wednesday.

So before we get into the on-field action, let's run through the biggest takeaways from Morgan's afternoon chat.

Carolina will start camp without a handful of key players.

Morgan announced that the team has placed left tackle Ikem Ekwonu (knee) and defensive lineman Tershawn Wharton (neck) on the physically unable to perform list. Both are expected to miss time into the regular season.

He also confirmed that cornerback Jaycee Horn (toe) has been put on the non-football injury list and that right tackle Taylor Moton (blood clot) has been put on the non-football illness list. Horn is likely to return soon.

In addition, Morgan revealed two newer moves—one of which will see defensive lineman Bobby Brown join Horn on the non-football injury list with a hamstring issue. Plus, rookie cornerback Jaylin Guilbeau was waived with an injury designation.

Being that both starting offensive tackles in Ekwonu and Moton will not be available at the start of the regular season, the Panthers have had to turn to a pair of recent additions.

As noted by Morgan, fifth-year veteran Rasheed Walker will take first-team reps at left tackle and rookie Monroe Freeling will man right tackle. He spoke about the outlook on Freeling, their 2026 first-round pick.

"I know what Monroe's made of," Morgan stated. "He's a competitor, he's super smart, he's gonna go out there and attack this thing and we have full confidence in him."

While Freeling was primarily a left tackle during his three-year run at the Unviersity of Georgia, he did record 257 snaps on the right side.

The offensive line also got a familiar face back in the fold, as Brady Christensen rejoined the team on a one-year deal this past weekend. Morgan spoke on the importance of Christensen's positional flexibility, especially given the injuries along the trench.

"Brady's versatility—it's huge. He can play all five spots," he said. "Talking to him last season, talking to him throughout the offseason—we did a really good job of staying in touch with him."

Christensen has logged snaps at all five positions on the line as a pro. His experience as a tackle may prove valuable with Ekwonu and Moton sidelined.

Third-string quarterback Will Grier, unexpectedly, announced his retirement last Wednesday. The 31-year-old's departure has left the Panthers with three quarterbacks heading into camp.

Morgan was asked if he plans on adding back a fourth man to the room.

"No plans right now to bring a fourth guy on," he replied. "Obviously, wish Will and his family the best. In terms of his career, just playing as long as he did, and just wish him the best moving forward."

The current group is headed by quarterback Bryce Young, with veteran Kenny Pickett and undrafted rookie Haynes King behind him.

Follow @ThePanthersWire on Twitter/X for more Panthers content.

This article originally appeared on Panthers Wire: 4 biggest takeaways from Panthers GM Dan Morgan's pre-training camp press conference

title: Everton Have Stepped Up For €25m Forward: Why Is It A Gamble?
description: Everton have stepped up their scouting of FC Midtjylland forward Franculino Djú. A raft of English clubs are now keeping tabs on the 21-year-old, with TEAMtalk reporting that Goodison officials are r...
fullText:
Everton have stepped up their scouting of FC Midtjylland forward Franculino Djú. A raft of English clubs are now keeping tabs on the 21-year-old, with TEAMtalk reporting that Goodison officials are right in the thick of the race. Getting him out of Denmark, though, is going to cost a fortune.

Midtjylland will not even entertain discussions unless someone puts forward an offer that eclipses the Danish league’s record transfer fee of €25 million. That is not just posturing. They knocked back a €25 million bid from the Saudi Pro League earlier this summer, so anyone expecting a bargain can forget about it.

The numbers Djú posted during the 2025/26 Superligaen campaign are silly. He finished as the competition’s top scorer, bagging 17 goals in 21 games. Throw in three assists across just 1,341 minutes of action, and you get a clearer picture of his impact. He averaged 1.14 goals per 90 minutes. That ranked him dead first across the entire Danish top flight. He was also tested in the Europa League, which gave him a taste of European football away from domestic duties.

Transfermarkt puts his current value at roughly €13 million. Midtjylland could not care less about that figure. Their absolute refusal to negotiate below €25 million shows how massive the gap is between theoretical market valuations and real-world transfer negotiations.

Everton need a striker. Badly. Nobody is going to argue against that. Whether Djú is actually the right fit at that price, though, is a completely different conversation.

The Superliga is decent. Midtjylland are regular fixtures in European qualifiers, so the level is respectable, but it is hardly the Premier League. Making the jump from Denmark to the top flight in England is tricky. Plenty of players have tried it and struggled with the physical intensity and lack of time on the ball. The drop-off in production can be brutal. What makes the 6ft 1in forward an exciting option is his continental experience. He did not look out of place in Europe this past term. He hit the target with nearly 60% of his shots, which is elite for someone his age.

ROME, ITALY – NOVEMBER 27: Daniele Ghilardi of AS Roma competes for the ball with Dju Franculino of FC Midtjylland during the UEFA Europa League 2025/26 League Phase MD5 match between AS Roma and FC Midtjylland at Stadio Olimpico on November 27, 2025 in Rome, Italy. (Photo by Paolo Bruno/Getty Images)

Shedding €25 million-plus on a guy who has never kicked a ball in a top-five European league carries serious risk. Everton are only just finding their feet after years of living under the shadow of PSR restrictions. Throwing that kind of cash at a single gamble feels reckless.

David Moyes has other fires to fight in this squad. Midfield needs fresh legs. Right-back is a glaring issue. Splashing the majority of the summer budget on a forward from the Danish league while leaving those positions thin makes little sense.

If Moyes can track down a winger or a centre-forward with proven Premier League history for a reasonable fee, that has to take priority. Djú is an exciting talent, but the price tag makes him a luxury Everton simply cannot afford right now.

title: SEC commish throws cold water on 'breakaway' notion, but concerns abound with Senate bill still up for grabs
description: 'If the headline remains that we’re breaking away, that’s uninformed,' Greg Sankey told Yahoo Sports.
fullText:
TAMPA, Fla. – Within this waterfront Marriott hotel along an offshoot of Tampa Bay, college football’s biggest media extravaganza unfolds.

In the main grand hallway, where hundreds of press members gather here for SEC football media days, a giant logo portraying the conference’s three iconic letters — S-E-C — serves as the primary visual backdrop.

But adjacent to that display, behind a guarded curtain, the league’s commissioner slumps into a chair as he scrolls through his cell phone during a rare quiet moment amidst the bustle.

“Mmm,” he mutters as he glares at the screen. “More messages from Washington.”

A topic at this year’s event — even among the head football coaches themselves — is ongoing negotiations over a congressional bill to regulate college athletics and, perhaps even more notable, the SEC’s potential plans, without revised federal legislation, to operate within its own silo: a conference-led governance model that’s caused a stir across the country.

In fact, in a sitdown with Yahoo Sports, Greg Sankey says the frustration within his league over the absence of regulation and enforcement will “continue to simmer” and “if we’re not able to deal with issues, then the boil starts.”

Does that mean the SEC intends to execute a full-fledged breakaway from the NCAA umbrella, to remove itself from the College Football Playoff and NCAA championships, to only play itself in the regular season, hold its own postseason tournament and then, if another league’s champion wants to duel, play them in a neutral-site game deemed as the new national championship?

But it does mean that the SEC is exploring a self-governing model to create and then enforce its own rules related to eligibility, transfers and athlete compensation.

However, he’s made clear in a recommendation to university presidents that it is not his preferred course of action. What is? A congressional bill.

While coaches parade across media day here, lawmakers and staff members in Washington, D.C. are continuing intense negotiations over revisions to the Protect College Sports Act in an effort to gain the support of the SEC and Big Ten. In fact, Big Ten commissioner Tony Petitti, on the Hill this week, has been an active, in-person participant in the negotiations with the bill’s co-authors Sens. Ted Cruz, Maria Cantwell and Eric Schmitt. ACC commissioner Jim Phillips was scheduled to be on the Hill on Wednesday too.

Big 12 commissioner Brett Yormark and Sankey, from the enclave at media days, have participated by phone.

Without a bill, the SEC’s conference-led governance model “takes on more interest,” said Sankey. “But that doesn’t mean something is poised to happen.”

“If the headline remains that we’re breaking away, that’s uninformed,” he told Yahoo Sports.

Those with knowledge of negotiations over the Senate bill have expressed renewed confidence in reaching an agreement with the two conferences over supporting the legislation — a key to the bill moving through the Senate, then the House and being signed by the president.

The latest discussions have centered mostly on changes to the revenue-share cap. Lawmakers are discussing the creation of a separate retention pool of money — $25 million in addition to the $21.3 million cap — that schools can use for the sole purpose of retaining athletes currently on their roster.

Cap adjustments are necessary, Sankey told Yahoo Sports, but he’d prefer to see finalized texts before commenting on the retention pool.

A litany of other issues languish as lawmakers and staff members work to reach a compromise on revisions, both between themselves — the Republican Cruz and Democrat Cantwell — and with leaders of the SEC and Big Ten.

For instance, an anti-expansion provision remains a fluid subject that, as of the latest discussions, would cap conference membership at 19 schools and require programs moving from one power conference to another to operate as an independent for five years. Such a move may expedite another wave of conference expansion as schools jockey to elevate themselves — such as ACC programs moving to the Big Ten or SEC — before the bill passes.

Lawmakers are expected to add to the legislation a provision that prohibits third-party entities, such as private equity firms, from creating a super league by poaching the top 20-30 brands in the country in a move to generate millions 

Asked why he’s so concerned about such entities forming a super league, Sankey told Yahoo Sports, “Because that’s what their proposals contemplate. You can define it however you want to, but my view is, whether it’s 20, 30, 70, 130 [schools], you are really creating a super league. There’s no assurance that once that project is down the road and it can't generate the income it’s promised for 130, that they don’t scale back to something very different.”

Sankey often refers to the SEC as its own “super league.” And many within his conference believe that the SEC should not only govern itself but only compete against itself — a model spearheaded publicly by Georgia president Jere Morehead.

“l think we’d want to play with the schools following the rules,” Morehead told Yahoo Sports earlier this year.

A conference-only governance model is rife with its own problems, let alone the revolutionary action of competitively leaving everyone behind. Sankey holds a background in college compliance and said a league enforcing its own rules would be “very” difficult. It’s why many conferences, including the SEC, abandoned such a structure years ago and instead granted the NCAA control over regulation and enforcement.

When he joined the SEC, Sankey told then-commissioner Mike Slive that he did not want the role of “head of enforcement” because of the difficulties associated with the league penalizing its own member schools.

And what about a competitive breakaway? Starting your own playoff? Playing only yourself?

The ideas have been discussed among administrators as a “Plan B” if federal legislation doesn’t pass. But, Sankey said, the plan may lead to political problems with the same entity, Congress, that officials are lobbying for assistance.

“It doesn’t take much imagination to say we’re going to leave [competitively] and you’re in congressional hearings [answering for it]. I think that’s unwise.”

title: Pacers GM Chad Buchanan address Pacers-LeBron James speculation
description: On the Setting the Pace podcast, Chad Buchanan addressed the Pacers pursuit of LeBron James and adding Kelly Oubre and Larry Nance Jr.
fullText:
In case the crying laughing emojis LeBron James sent Tyrese Haliburton when the Pacers All-Star point guard tried to recruit him weren't enough to make the point, Pacers general manager Chad Buchanan closed to the door on any hope that Indiana might be able to land the NBA's all-time leading scorer.

Buchanan said on the Setting the Pace podcast with Alex Golden and Michael Facci that the Pacers did make a pitch to James' agent Rich Paul -- just as he presumes every team in the NBA did. However, Buchanan says he has no indication that the Pacers are still in competition for James' services.

"If you're expecting the LeBron to Pacers announcement to come, it's not coming," Buchanan said on the podcast. "I don't want to break everybody's hearts. Much respect to LeBron as a player, obviously, and to Rich Paul. I think every team made their pitch to Rich as to their situation, what they could offer, as did we. We also know that he's narrowed it down to some other teams. Hopefully he stays in the West. I don't know where he's going to end up, but I can tell you it won't be in Indiana, unfortunately."

Buchanan's appearance on Setting the Pace represented his first public comments since the NBA calendar flipped over and free agency began. The 30-minute first part of the podcast was posted late Tuesday and outside of the brief discussion on James, it focused on the players the Pacers have brought in or kept so far this summer. Some highlights from the discussion follow.

The Pacers first and biggest signing of the summer was forward Kelly Oubre Jr. to a two-year deal worth approximately $17 million. The Pacers had to use the non-taxpayer mid-level exception to sign Oubre and for that reason they are hard capped at the first luxury tax apron and have little salary cap flexibility beyond that. However, the Pacers viewed quality wing depth as their most important need and invested in Oubre to add it.

The 6-8 Oubre started 150 games for the 76ers over the last three seasons and averaged 14.9 points, 5.4 rebounds, 1.6 assists and 1.3 steals per game. He averaged 14.1 points per game in 2025-26 and posted career-bests in 3-point percentage (.360) and effective field goal percentage (.546). The 30-year-old could push Aaron Nesmith for the starting small forward job but could also add two-way production to the second unit.

"We felt good about our starting five," Buchanan said. "We felt good about a couple of guys on our bench, but we didn't feel as strong about the depth at the wing position. As we went through free agency, we looked at some different trades to see if there was something that made sense. The more we talked through things, talked to other teams, talked to agents, it felt like Kelly was a name we kept coming back to for various reasons. We're a team that's trying to win now. We're trying to compete next year at the highest level. We need win-now players. Kelly's been in the league for a long time. He's established himself as a very solid two-way wing player that's contributed for different teams for different levels."

Oubre has averaged double-figures for each of the last nine seasons and he's also averaged 1.0 steals per game or more for each of the past nine seasons. He hasn't been the most efficient scorer, but he goes hard to the rim and he's a willing shooter, making 1,068 career 3-pointers at a 32.7% clip.

"He impacts the game on the defensive side of the ball as much as he can on the offensive side, which was appealing to us," Buchanan said. "Last year was probably the best shooting year of his career. We think there's more room from that regard for Kelly. I think he'll see more open shots created in our system than he's probably seen in his career. Optimistic that he'll be able to knock down shots for us, but also provide some presence as a defender, being able to guard multiple positions, which is important for us. As we face in the east some of these great bigger scoring guards and scoring wings, Kelly provides us another option besides Aaron and Drew (Andrew Nembhard) specifically and Jarace (Walker) to a certain degree to put on some of these bigger guards."

While the Oubre signing seemed like an obvious fit for the Pacers' roster needs, the logic of signing Larry Nance Jr. was a little less clear, particularly when paired with the waiving of center Micah Potter.

The 28-year-old Potter averaged 9.7 points and 5.0 rebounds per game and shot 51.5% from the floor and 42.3% from 3-point range last season after the Pacers signed him out of the G League on a non-guaranteed contract in late December. The 33-year-old Nance averaged 3.7 points and 2.7 rebounds on 41.9% shooting and 33.3% 3-point shooting, playing just 35 games for Cavaliers. The scoring, rebounding and field goal percentage figures were all career lows for Nance, who is averaging 7.5 points and 5.9 rebounds per game, and shooting 53.5% from the floor and 35.2% from 3-point range for his 11-year career.

The Pacers created about $400,000 in room under the first apron in the move because Potter's deal was non-guaranteed but worth $2.8 million and Nance's veteran minimum contract only counts as about $2.4 million as a cap hit even though he'll be paid nearly $3.9 million. However, Buchanan said there were also basketball reasons for the move.

"Larry brings us some defensive versatility, which I think is important with our bench," Buchanan said. "We've always had, with Rick (Carlisle) and our system, we've had the ability to score. Jay Huff and Larry as our backup five options, Larry can slide over and guard some 4s at times. He's got a lot of experience as well. I think our coaches felt really comfortable with his basketball IQ, his experience, and just his personality felt like a really good fit with our locker room as well. Larry really had targeted us, wanted to be here with the Pacers and I think he's very motivated to have a bounce-back season. I think he would be the first to admit that last year didn't go the way he hoped it would. He's very motivated to come in and fit in and play well for us this season."

Buchanan said there's wide-open competition between Nance and Huff for backup minutes behind starter Ivica Zubac at center and also says that forward Obi Toppin will get minutes at the 5.

"With what we had financially to work with, we were kind of limited in what we could go after at the backup 5," Buchanan said. "As we looked at our option, Larry was our best option that was gettable. We also know he's not surefire. He's gotta come in and earn it. Jay Huff has gotta earn those backup minutes as well. You have Obi as an option to come in and play some backup 5. There's different looks that our coaches could utilize at the backup center spots. There's might be some nights where our coaches will value the defensive versatility in Larry as opposed to the shooting and rim protection that Jay provides. It's probably going to be night-to-night who plays there, but heading into camp it's an open competition at this point."

The Pacers made two decisions to narrow what was a five-man competition for three two-way contract spots down to at least a three-man competition for two spots if not a two-man competition for one.

The Pacers waived two-way contract guard Taelon Peter and re-signed forward Jalen Slawson -- who finished last season on a two-way deal but was a restricted free agent. Forward Kobe Brown and guard Ethan Thompson are also under two-way contracts with Brown having signed one just before the start of NBA Summer League. Second-round pick Braden Smith -- a Purdue All-American and an Indiana Mr. Basketball at Westfield High School -- agreed to a two-way contract when he was drafted but one has not been signed.

The Pacers could technically keep all four players. They have one standard contract still remaining that they could use to sign a player on the rookie minimum. They are about $2.2 million under the first apron which isn't enough to sign a player to the veteran minimum but is enough to sign a player to the rookie minimum.

Buchanan said the Pacers have not yet finalized a decision on those spots.

"We're very, very tight to the first apron where we're hard-capped now," Buchanan said. "We have different options to fill that 15th spot. We're still kind of making that decision, making some choices at the back end of the roster, the 15th spot and the two-way spots. Braden is a guy we valued in the draft for his playmaking, his IQ and his toughness. We haven't decided exactly what we're gonna do now because we have several guys that we like and we want to keep. We're trying to figure out what's the best avenue to keep all those guys in play for us."

Buchanan said the Pacers were thrilled with the growth they saw from Slawson, who they originally brought in on an Exhibit 10 contract last summer and promoted to a two-way in February. In Summer League, he led the Pacers with 20.8 points per game and recorded 16 blocks, most of any player in Summer League. He was productive in his action with the Pacers down the stretch and he earned first-team G League All-Defensive honors for his work with the Noblesville Boom.

"The development and the growth that we saw throughout the season with the Boom was something that we really noticed and really brought to light that this was a player who was really starting to blossom," Buchanan said. "... He got versatility as a defender. I don't know if I've every seen a guy with a knack for chase-down blocks like he has. He might be, next to LeBron, the best in the world. He's got a real knack for that and the rest of his game is piecing together too. His shooting is developing. His decision-making can be up and down, but we like his ability to make things happen."

Buchanan also described the process that led to the Pacers keeping Brown on a two-way contract. Brown was acquired as part of the trade with the Clippers for Zubac. Brown averaged 9.4 points, 4.9 rebounds and 2.0 assists for the Pacers and shot 50.3% from the floor and 43.3% from 3-point range. He hit more 3-pointers with the Pacers in 27 games (39) than he did in 2 1/2 seasons with the Clippers (33).

"We were pleasantly surprised with a lot of the things he was doing," Buchanan said. "We felt like this was a pretty good fit, our system, our coaches' trust in Kobe. As we looked at our roster, No. 1 our roster spots, where we were at in the cap. we knew we were going to have some limitations on what we could probably offer at that position. We talked early on with Kobe's representatives about, 'Hey, we really would like to retain Kobe and have him back with us, but we don't know if we'll have the flexibility to make that a standard guaranteed spot. We'd love to have him on a two-way if that's an option on your end.' ... I think Kobe felt comfortable in his time here with us and wanted to continue on that path. We're excited to have him back."

Dustin Dopirak covers the Pacers all season. Get more coverage on IndyStarTV and with the Pacers Insider newsletter.

This article originally appeared on Indianapolis Star: Pacers GM Chad Buchanan address Pacers-LeBron James speculation

title: Angels reportedly fire self-proclaimed 'Baseball Spy' after he was caught filming Rockies coaches
description: Justin Prinstein was in his first season as a pro scout with the Angels before he was fired.
fullText:
The Los Angeles Angels reportedly fired pro scout Justin “Boomer” Prinstein after he was caught filming Colorado Rockies coaches while he was working in the stands, according to The Athletic.

Prinstein, who referred to himself as “The Baseball Spy,” was fired by the Angels immediately after Major League Baseball informed the team of Prinstein’s alleged actions.

Prinstein reportedly ran afoul of the Rockies — and eventually MLB — after he was spotted filming Rockies coaches with his phone while on an advance scouting trip before the Angels played the Rockies in early June. While scouts filming is acceptable under MLB rules, using technology to steals opponents’ signs is frowned upon by the league.

The Rockies reported the issue to MLB, which contacted the Angels about Prinstein’s alleged actions. Angels general manager Perry Minasian reportedly fired Prinstein immediately and apologized to the Rockies’ front office, per The Athletic. The Angels are not facing punishment from the league, as it’s believed Prinstein acted on his own.

When contacted by The Athletic, the Angels, Rockies and MLB declined comment on the situation. Prinstein, who owns the handle @TheBaseballSpy on social media, did not respond to interview requests made by The Athletic.

Prinstein was in the midst of his first season with the Angels. The previous two seasons, he spent time working for two separate Korean Baseball Organization teams. Prior to that, he worked for the Cincinnati Reds in 2023, per The Athletic.

Using technology to decode signs has been among the biggest issues within the sport in recent years. In the wake of the Houston Astros’ trash-can banging, sign-stealing scandal, the league has attempted to come down hard on MLB teams that use technology to try and steal signs.

The league also reportedly took action against a similar situation around the All-Star break, as it informed teams to stop using artificial intelligence in the dugout to inform strategy. The issues are different, though both pertain to teams using technology in order to gain an edge. In both instances, MLB has made it clear that won’t be tolerated.

Sign stealing is allowed in baseball, but only if done naturally. If a player picks up on a pitcher’s tell during a game or can read signs made by other players or coaches on the field, that’s OK provided the player who decoded the sign did not use technology to gain an edge.

That practice is often kept secret, as teams that gain an edge on opponents don’t want their advantage to be ruined in later matchups. Sometimes, though, spotting players relaying signs to teammates can be obvious during games. This mostly occurs when players are on second base and can see how a pitcher grips a ball in their glove. If the player on second base can tell, that player may perform a specific gesture while on second to let the batter know what pitch is coming next. The New York Yankees were accused of that exact thing late last season.

If Prinstein was using his phone to try and decode signs from Rockies coaches — and it’s unclear whether that’s the case — it had the potential to turn into a much bigger scandal within the sport. The Angels seemingly got ahead of that by firing Prinstein, and that decision may have saved them from being punished by MLB as well.

title: Japan cities hit 40 C, officially marking first "kokushobi"
description: Three cities in central Japan on Tuesday reached 40 C, officially marking the country's first "kokushobi," or "cruelly hot day," since the weather agency introduced the term earlier this year to warn the public of extreme heat.
fullText:
TOKYO - Three cities in central Japan on Tuesday reached 40 C, officially marking the country's first "kokushobi," or "cruelly hot day," since the weather agency introduced the term earlier this year to warn the public of extreme heat.

As a high-pressure system covered much of the Japanese archipelago, Tajimi and Gujo in Gifu Prefecture recorded temperatures of 40.3 C and 40.0 C, respectively, while the mercury in Toyoda in Aichi Prefecture climbed to 40.1 C, according to the Japan Meteorological Agency.

Coined in April, the term "kokushobi" refers to days when temperatures reach 40 C and is intended to more effectively alert the public to dangerous heat.

The agency said it expects temperatures to remain high in many parts of the country from Wednesday onward and urged the public to take precautions against heat-related illness.

The persistent heat has apparently already claimed lives, with two men -- one in his 70s in Odawara, Kanagawa Prefecture, and another in his 40s in Sanjo, Niigata Prefecture -- found collapsed on the street Monday and later confirmed dead at hospital.

On Tuesday, a woman in her 80s died from heatstroke in Mitsuke, Niigata Prefecture, according to local officials. The woman was found collapsed in her home when a staff member from a regional elder care center visited her.

Of the country's 914 observation points, 731 had recorded maximum temperatures of 30 C or higher as of 3 p.m., with 272 qualifying as "moshobi," which literally translates as "extremely hot day," excluding those separately classified as "kokushobi."

According to the agency, the high-pressure system began covering much of the country around Sunday, warming the air at both the surface and in the upper atmosphere and causing temperatures to rise.

Other areas that recorded temperatures close to 40 C included Nagoya, which reached 39.7 C, and Kumagaya in Saitama Prefecture, which recorded 38.6 C, it said.

title: Code mode is all you need: Why agents writing code > calling tools
description: Browserbase is the complete platform to build and deploy agents that browse and interact with the web like humans.
fullText:
TLDR; For a large class of agent tasks, the best “tool” is a small code-execution surface. Let the model write ordinary code against typed capabilities, then enforce security below the model instead of wrapping every action in another bespoke tool call.

We keep giving agents bigger toolboxes, when in reality they just need better tools.

One MCP server exposes 12 tools. The next exposes 30. Add a CLI, a sandbox, a database, a CRM, a browser, and suddenly the model spends a meaningful part of its context learning interfaces instead of doing the job.

Meanwhile, frontier models have spent years getting frighteningly good at one interface already: code.

The first generation of agents needed explicit verbs.

search_customer. get_invoice. click_element. query_logs. download_file.

That made sense; Models were unreliable, context windows were smaller, and every allowed action needed tight guardrails. If you wanted an agent to complete a 10-step workflow, you gave it 10 carefully described tools and hoped it picked the right one in the right order.

“Code mode” changes the shape of that interface.

Instead of teaching the model a new JSON schema for every action, you expose a small set of typed capabilities inside an execution environment. The model writes the glue itself.

It can call 3 services in parallel, filter a 20 MB response before it reaches context, retry one failed branch, write an intermediate result to disk, and compose the whole workflow in a language it already knows by heart.

For this workflow, a rough estimate looks like this:

Roughly 88% less context. Scale the toolbox to 40 always-loaded tools and schemas alone can consume ~12,000 tokens before the agent starts working.

One single code execution can replace a long sequence of tool calls. More importantly, the logic between calls becomes deterministic. JavaScript handles the join, filter, loop, and error path. The model only has to decide what program to write.

Every custom tool asks the model to learn new slang.

The name is completely new, so are the arguments and error shape. The model has to infer whether customer_search, find_customer, and lookup_account are synonyms or subtly different operations. Multiply that across every vendor and every internal service.

Code gives the model a familiar grammar for all of them.

Types provide the vocabulary, the compiler catches malformed calls, runtime errors create a feedback loop, files provide working memory, and standard programming language features provide branching, concurrency, parsing, and transformations without another trip through the model.

An individual tool call is a decision, a program is a decision tree.

This is why code mode feels qualitatively different from adding one more tool. The model can turn a fuzzy task into a temporary, task-specific tool. It writes the exact abstraction it needs, uses it, inspects the result, and can decide to throw it away or saves it as a reusable skill.

At Browserbase, we’re seeing this shift even earlier because we build browser agents.

Every single web page already has a programming interface. The DOM describes its structure, Browser APIs expose state, and JavaScript can query elements, read application data, dispatch events, call page-local functions, inspect perf. entries, and transform results before returning them.

page.evaluate() runs JavaScript in the page context, where window, document, and the application's own runtime are available.

The agent does not need an extract_product_cards tool. It writes the extraction logic (and the extraction logic ONLY) the page requires.

The same pattern works for interaction:

How on earth do you define a universal tool for every interaction on every single website?

You don’t, just let the model’s write code to control the browser in the exact way they need.

There are still limits. The JS inside the page cannot control every piece of browser chrome, bypass origin boundaries, safely handle every credential, or guarantee that synthetic events behave like trusted user input. Visual interfaces, canvases, cross-origin frames, downloads, and native permission prompts (ie. ad popups) still benefit from specialized primitives.

For a huge share of browser work, the most general browser tool is JavaScript.

Code mode improves more than interface familiarity.

A tool-heavy loop often returns every intermediate result to the model. Code can filter, aggregate, and write large values to disk before returning the 10 lines that matter.

A program can control a browser, query a warehouse, normalize a CSV, and update a CRM in one execution. The control flow lives in TypeScript instead of being re-decided one model turn at a time.

A successful run can become a script, test, or skill. The next run starts from working code instead of reconstructing the workflow from a transcript.

Compilers and runtimes produce structured feedback, which the agent uses edit the program and rerun it in a tight loop.

The progression looks something like:

Once an agent discovers a working workflow, paying for fresh reasoning on every run is waste. Save the code then run it deterministically. Bring the model back for repair when the environment changes.

Our internal agent, bb, works across engineering, support, sales, marketing, and operations. It investigates browser sessions, queries internal data, reads files, logs feature requests, and writes code.

We could have given it a giant flat catalog of tools. Instead, its core surface stays small. The most important capability is exec: JS execution against typed service interfaces.

The agent can write code to call a warehouse, CRM, support system, observability stack, or browser. It can parallelize requests, transform results before they enter context, and move large outputs to files. Skills teach it the playbook for a domain, while code handles the execution.

I wrote about the full architecture, including the sandbox, skill system, and credential proxy, here.

This design also makes new integrations cheaper. We define a typed capability once, place it behind the execution boundary, and add a small skill describing when to use it. The agent writes the task-specific composition.

The toolbox grows without making the top-level harness grow at the same time.

If you had security doubts, they are valid. Arb. code execution creates a much larger blast radius than a tool call. “Running it in a sandbox” is necessary, but nowhere near sufficient.

A sandbox limits what code can touch in the execution environment. It doesn’t automatically prevent the agent from exfiltrating a credential, calling a destructive API, sending data to an unapproved domain, or repeating a side effect after a partial failure.

The safety boundary has to sit below the generated program.

Let the model write anything inside the box. Then make the box physically incapable of doing what the run is not authorized to do.

This is where specialized tools belong. Things like a payment action, permission change, or prod deploy should carry a small but explicit contract. Code mode should orchestrate read and transformation surfaces, while high-consequence writes stay tightly governed.

Tool catalogs will keep growing, but the model-facing surface shouldn’t.

SDKs, MCP servers, and CLIs can still provide transport, discovery, authentication, observability, and human access. For capable agents, more of those interfaces will sit underneath a programmable execution layer.

The agent will see types and code, and the runtime will see permissions and policy. The service will see a normal authenticated request.

Each new generation of models writes better code, can debug longer workflows, and uses execution feedback more effectively. We should stop forcing that capability through hundreds of tiny, unfamiliar schemas.

Give the model a language it already speaks, give the language a safe place to run, then put the strict guardrails beneath both.

The best agent harnesses will use both tools and code, pairing a small number of high-consequence tools beside a general execution surface.

All roads lead to Rome, let the Agents write code.
