title: Harry Potter publisher to receive millions in Anthropic copyright settlement
description:
Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their work 
The publisher of Harry Potter has received a multimillion-pound payout as a beneficiary of a $1.5bn (£1.12bn) copyright settlement between the AI startup Anthropic and thousands of authors over the use of their protected work to power chatbots.
Bloomsbury, which is home to the bestselling novelists Sarah J Maas and Susanna Clarke as well as JK Rowling, said it had 14,087 titles listed within the settlement, with a proposed compensation of about $3,000 a title.
 Continue reading...
fullText:
Bloomsbury has 14,087 titles listed in agreement between AI startup and authors over use of their work

The publisher of Harry Potter has received a multimillion-pound payout as a beneficiary of a $1.5bn (£1.12bn) copyright settlement between the AI startup Anthropic and thousands of authors over the use of their protected work to power chatbots.

Bloomsbury, which is home to the bestselling novelists Sarah J Maas and Susanna Clarke as well as JK Rowling, said it had 14,087 titles listed within the settlement, with a proposed compensation of about $3,000 a title.

The London-based company expects to receive the cash from the settlement in instalments, potentially starting in the second half of this fiscal year, with the proceeds to be split with authors.

After a deduction of about 10% for attorney fees and other expenses, Bloomsbury and the group of affected authors can expect to receive about $19m (£14m).

US-based AI companies have argued that using such work is allowed under the legal doctrine of fair use, which allows use of copyrighted work without the owner’s permission in certain circumstances. However, creative professionals and artists around the world have been demanding that AI companies seek permission first – or at least pay them for using their work.

The US district judge Araceli Martínez-Olguín said on Monday that the settlement provided “meaningful relief” to affected authors and publishers. The case began when the novelist Andrea Bartz and two other authors filed the lawsuit in 2024. About 91% of the 482,000 works covered in the suit have been claimed.

The authors’ lead lawyer, Justin Nelson, described it as “the largest known copyright recovery in history”.

It is the first major settlement out of dozens of AI copyright lawsuits being filed in the US on behalf of authors and news outlets, which are still going through the courts.

Anthropic’s deputy general counsel, Aparna Sridhar, said in a written statement after the ruling: “We are pleased that more than 91% of authors and publishers covered by the settlement have claimed their share of the payment, and we’re looking forward to bringing this matter to a close.”

Bloomsbury announced an AI licensing deal last year which allows it to sell academic works to train up generative AI programmes.

The firm said recently that more subject areas were now being considered for AI training. Authors have been given the opportunity to “opt in” to the scheme and will be paid royalties if they decide to let their work be used.

title: Why are OpenAI and Anthropic cheering on regulation in Australia? The answer has global reach
description: The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in...
fullText:
The companies hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June

Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast

Top US AI developers Anthropic and OpenAI cheered when Australia announced it would set new AI rules.

Big tech celebrating limits on their Silicon Valley VC-funded free-for-all might seem counterintuitive but there’s a much broader play than just what happens in one relatively small market.

Anthropic and OpenAI aren’t even on sharemarkets yet but this week lost billions in predicted value after being shown up by a Chinese startup.

Moonshot AI on Friday launched a new model, Kimi K3, advertising it as the first open-source model of its size, allowing more user modification than Anthropic’s Claude and OpenAI’s ChatGPT.

Analysts are judging K3 is competitive with both the leading US models, which are also less freely customisable. Moonshot had to temporarily pause new subscriptions after demand for the new model strained capacity.

Implied values for Anthropic’s sharemarket debut has slumped US$232bn to $1.56tn from Friday to Tuesday on IG’s trading platform. OpenAI dropped US$160bn to $1.16tn.

The two companies are still privately owned and valued at less than US$1tn each, so IG’s numbers are market bets, not true values.

But they hope to follow in the footsteps of SpaceX, which raised $86bn and soared to a $2.1tn valuation after it listed on public markets in June.

Anthropic has already begun registering to list on US sharemarkets, which would allow it to raise billions from new investors. The better the story it can tell, the more money it can raise.

Chinese competition does not make for a good story. Nor do surprise lawsuits from unhappy creatives, such as the one Anthropic had to settle for US$1.5bn on Monday (Tuesday AEST).

The company will pay authors about US$3,000 for each of an estimated 500,000 books covered by the settlement.

One of the authors whose work Anthropic scraped was Andrew Charlton, Australia’s assistant technology minister. Charlton says the new regulation will mean Australian creatives can choose whether AI models are trained on their work and big tech companies will have to pay them.

Yet those new rules could be a boon to big tech, Charlton says.

“If we are upfront with what we expect in this Australian standard, we can give clarity to investors that in some ways enhances the attractiveness of Australia,” he told Sky News on Monday.

Malik Ahmed Khan, an equity analyst at Morningstar Equity, says technology companies would find it much easier to invest in Australia as they learn where regulation is heading.

The companies will also have an easier job convincing investors to buy in at high prices if they can show they’re focusing on building AI and managing the risk of running afoul of governments or local laws, Khan says.

While Australia is one of few countries to now be regulating AI directly, with little direct impact on companies based overseas, Anthony Albanese hopes to set the global tone. In his speech announcing the regulations, he cited Australia’s leadership on social media bans for under-16s as proof.

Khan says more countries following Australia’s lead on AI could boost to companies’ prospects and market values.

“A positive deal in Australia could enable them to pursue similar deals across the globe,” he said.

Regulation would also give companies the prospect of favourable terms.

Albanese signalled he would consult “closely” with industry and other countries to legislate regulations that would aim to enable AI’s arrival, not restrict it. OpenAI and Anthropic were quick to position themselves as partners ready to help shape the new approach.

Dario Amodei, Anthropic’s chief executive and a former OpenAI vice-president, has already given an indication of what big tech thinks it should look like.

In a June essay, he called for a coalition of democracies to dominate in AI and lock out China.

While there are real national security concerns in this area, there’s also a healthy dose of self-interest in blocking out competitors.

With the launch of Kimi’s K3, analysts believes it is increasingly likely that the US will block businesses from exclusively using Chinese-based models over national security fears.

Amodei also called for domestic regulation, asking governments to test, evaluate and even block AI models if they presented “unacceptable risks” – but with protections against local “political favoritism or arbitrary decisions”.

Anthropic’s feud with the Trump administration has left it on a US supply chain blacklist. Days after Amodei published his essay, the US government forced Anthropic to suspend access to its Mythos 5 models for foreign nationals.

Anthropic’s critics have accused it of self-interest as it nears its initial public offering (IPO) on sharemarkets. The Pentagon’s Kirsten Davies said in June: “Some things are simply more important than revenue cycles, clickbait and pre-IPO valuation.”

Yet Amodei’s warnings about security concerns have been echoed by Albanese and by Five Eyes intelligence agencies.

Aruna Sathanapally, the CEO of Australia’s Grattan Institute, met the Anthropic chief when he visited Canberra in April. She said Amodei’s concerns seemed sincere.

“He was seeing things within his own company that were giving him great concern about what Anthropic, as well as their competitors, may be capable of developing,” Sathanapally said.

“Just because they have vested interests in the particular shape of regulation, it doesn’t mean that the calls for regulation themselves are not well founded. It just means … you need to keep alive to competitive neutrality in how you design it.”

title: How Pokémon Go encouraged me to find wonder in the world
description:
These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals
• Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? Sign up here
Believe it or not, Pokémon Go is 10 years old this month. The wildly successful smartphone game, which allows you to track down lovable creatures in the real world using GPS and augmented-reality technologies, has reportedly been downloaded 800m times across 150 countries and regions. A trillion Pokémon have been caught so far – and I have been responsible for a modest percentage of that figure.
When the game was first released, my sons and I spent many happy hours wandering the streets and parks of Frome searching for Jigglypuffs and Charizards, and the game’s social element, which lets you take on gym battles with other players in your vicinity, offered my autistic son a route to communicate with other children in a way he’d never been able to before.
fullText:
These days, I ‘collect’ birdsong and wild flowers, but it all started when my sons and I first set out to find Pikachu and pals

Don’t get Pushing Buttons delivered to your inbox? 
Believe it or not, Pokémon Go is 10 years old this month. The wildly successful smartphone game, which allows you to track down lovable creatures in the real world using GPS and augmented-reality technologies, has reportedly been downloaded 800m times across 150 countries and regions. A trillion Pokémon have been caught so far – and I have been responsible for a modest percentage of that figure.

When the game was first released, my sons and I spent many happy hours wandering the streets and parks of Frome searching for Jigglypuffs and Charizards, and the game’s social element, which lets you take on gym battles with other players in your vicinity, offered my autistic son a route to communicate with other children in a way he’d never been able to before.

My boys have grown up now, but I enjoy the fact that the game is still seducing new players into searching their local shopping centre for Machops. There’s a part of me that never really let go of this compulsion, although it’s now evolved. I have a folder of apps on my phone that I have ready whenever I go out for a walk, or visit an unfamiliar town. Seek is a free app that uses image recognition to help you identify plants, trees and animals, and it lets you collect species badges to celebrate new discoveries. The accuracy can be a little variable, but it has given me a new interest in wild flowers and trees, and, being a nerd, I like to imagine which particular species would win in a Pokémon-style battle. How would red valerian fare against field bindweed? What would be the special attack of the common dog violet?

Another favourite is the Merlin Bird ID app, developed by the Cornell Lab of Ornithology. It lets you identify birdsong wherever you are in the world, and provides a “bird of the day” to look out for. My wife and I use it competitively, sending each other screenshots of unusual birds we’ve heard on our travels. I can now fairly confidently identify at least 10 bird songs, and I have to admit, I do associate certain birds with specific Pokémon. Pikachu? The yellow hammer, of course. Corviknight is the raven. Pidgeots are self-explanatory.

Alongside these, I also love Flightradar24 and MarineTraffic, which track airplane and ship movement respectively. I like to know where things are going and why, and to play Top Trumps with the vehicle data, pitting nearby cargo ships against each other in imagined naval battles based on their size and carrying capacity.

I’m not sure I’d be using any of these apps if its wasn’t for Pokémon Go. It opened up in me an understanding of the natural world as a vast playable space, filled with beautiful little signs and systems. When you explore a new game world, everything you encounter is potentially interesting. You have no filter of expectation – in terms of executive function, it’s like being a toddler again. As you get older, you lose that fascination with the minutiae of the world around you – you go for dramatic vistas, rather than the patches of weeds along roadsides; the dawn chorus over the solitary songbird in your garden. Sometimes you need a little prod to bring that back.

During the summer of 2016, and for a few seasons after, every walk we took became an adventure. Mundane places we knew well suddenly bristled with possibilities, boring landmarks became battlegrounds, strangers became allies. I just took all that and applied it to the real things around us – the birds in the woods near where we live, the weeds in our overgrown garden, the planes overhead. I’m not alone in this. Inspired by Pokémon Go, Brisbane student Kris Norris has created a social discovery game based on leaving messages on payphones around the city. Hydrologists in Colorado used a Pokémon Go-style app to discover and track small streams.

I’ve also discovered other location-based experiences along the way, that I might not have thought about otherwise. The fitness game Zombies, Run! was better for me as a running tool than Couch to 5k; Pikmin Bloom is a delightful floral walking game, and Geocaching, where you track down real-life caches hidden by other players, has tricked me into a few long hikes.

What I have learned, or at least had confirmed, since playing Pokémon Go is that all video games have the potential to open new intellectual doorways – new ways to envisage the world and how it works. No time spent in play is wasted. Wonder is a transferable skill.

Mystery games are having a real moment, but brothers Tom and Adam Vian have been quietly perfecting their Detective Grimoire series for more than a decade. I’ve eagerly awaited The Mermaid Mask, in which the titular detective and his smarter sarcastic sidekick Sally try to solve a locked-room murder aboard a submarine, and it does not disappoint.

I always recommend playing these games with a loved one – not because they’re difficult (the puzzles are about as taxing as you’d want for a cosy evening) but because the fully voice-acted, laugh-out-loud script makes them just as fun to watch (and backseat drive) as to actually play. Jordan Erica Webber

Available on: PC, Switch/Switch 2, PS5
Estimated playtime: 10 hours, best enjoyed over a few evenings

Last year, Paramount signed up to make a Call of Duty movie. Now, the studio has confirmed that it will take place in the Modern Warfare universe – which means contemporary weapons, hi-tech espionage and geopolitical slaughter. But who will play moustached SAS hero Captain Price? There are rumours Pedro Pascal is up for the role, but surely it’s got to be Henry Cavill?!

So now it’s all over, it turns out EA Sports FC correctly predicted the World Cup winner for the fifth year in a row. The development team ran a simulation of every group and knock-out match in the competition, where Spain emerged as the victor. I wonder if it also predicted that England v France would be the most video game-like match of the tournament?

On Saturday, GamesRadar ran a feature interview with senior figures at Xbox, who confirmed the importance of exclusive games to the console. “There has to be a reason for people to buy an Xbox,” said chief content officer Matt Booty, accurately. Gears of War: E-Day and Clockwork Revolution will be the first titles to be affected by the withdrawal from the multiplatform approach that saw titles such as Forza Horizon 6 and Sea of Thieves head to PS5.

Bub – living an artist’s dreams and dreads in New York

Apple Crumble: a tasty Knives Out-inspired mystery that lets you snoop on your own family

Castlevania: Belmont’s Curse – Konami classic rises again from Paris sewers and Joan of Arc is a boss

D&D players raise millions in real-life campaign against ‘corporate elite’

Denshattack! – time to get on board with kickflipping trains | ★★★★☆

Splatoon Raiders – Nintendo’s inky shooter fades to grey | ★★☆☆☆

This week, a philosophical question from Richard:

“What genuine life lessons have you learned from games? I often think of Verso’s advice to Lune in Clair Obscur: Expedition 33, that the only voice that should be in your head is your own. Bonus hard mode: not just life lessons from dialogue or other ‘narrative’ parts of a game, but from the gaming action itself. The hours I spent trying to beat the last level of Super Mario Odyssey taught me a lot about my flaws: panicking in the face of new situations, and stumbling over bits I had done umpteen times before because I contemptuously thought I’d mastered them.”

One lesson I took from platform games such as Impossible Mission and Jet Set Willy and later from stealth games, was to stop and observe any complex situation before leaping in. That has saved me a lot of trouble. I’ve also picked up little snippets of practical information that I’ll never get to use in real-life – I discovered the Scandinavian flick playing World Rally Championship (it’s a driving technique, not a haircut) and the Coriolis effect while playing the famed assassination mission in Call of Duty 4: Modern Warfare. Finally, as a novelist, I learned plenty of things about comedy writing from The Secret of Monkey Island – specifically the importance of understatement and how to set up a gag and then keep it running. The line “That’s the second-biggest monkey head I’ve ever seen” will always stick in my head in relation to both of those skills. I should have it crocheted and framed on my wall.

If you have a question for Question Block – or anything else to say about the newsletter – email us at pushingbuttons@theguardian.com.

title: Red Kiss – guiding vampires through a blood-soaked cold war Berlin
description:
Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades
‘You look at the news and you’re like, ‘Another pointless war, another pointless bloodshed, another pointless genocide.’ And that can make you a bit hopeless.”
Roy van der Schilden, narrative designer and business director at Wispfire, speaks of a very human feeling. But the protagonist of its upcoming game Red Kiss can relate – despite being a vampire.
 Continue reading...
fullText:
Inspired by interactive theatre and fuelled by the motion capture tech used by VTubers, the vampire spies in Red Kiss have personality in spades

‘You look at the news and you’re like, ‘Another pointless war, another pointless bloodshed, another pointless genocide.’ And that can make you a bit hopeless.”

Roy van der Schilden, narrative designer and business director at Wispfire, speaks of a very human feeling. But the protagonist of its upcoming game Red Kiss can relate – despite being a vampire.

You play as an (initially human) inhabitant of cold war Berlin who has tried to no avail to endure the conflict outside the window: through partying, journalling, activism … Still, you feel empty. When offered a new life of power and purpose, you don’t need much convincing to offer up your exposed neck.

“I think a lot of people who create vampire games feel the anguish and the melancholy of the genre,” says Van der Schilden. “Like, how to deal with your feeling of justice in an unjust world.”

In this tactical narrative RPG, you plunge into a fictional 80s Berlin by dispatching vampire agents to its fractured streets. They’re your eyes and ears, looking for clues, building reputation with city factions, and of course quenching their bloodthirst. As in last year’s superhero caper Dispatch (from AdHoc Studio), you’ll come across opportunities around the map, each with its own requirements, making use of a skillcheck system tied to six character skills. It’s up to you to pick the best fangs for the job, all while maintaining their energy, money and blood supply.

The vampires under your command are the beating (undead) heart of the experience. You progressively unveil their backstories by undertaking personal missions, during which you learn about their individual pasts and struggles. It’s in these moments that you begin to enact change and share some of your newfound purpose with others as guidance, deciding how their stories are ultimately resolved.

Wispfire emphasises the importance of the cast by infusing hand-drawn portraits with voice acting and motion-capture (mocap) animation. Notably, for the latter the team is using Live2D, a relatively inexpensive software tool commonly used by VTubers to animate their virtual avatars during a livestream.

A good iPhone was enough to start experimenting, but the team had to develop tools to adapt the software to suit their needs, and the tech isn’t without limitations. “With a mocap character for 3D, you usually can move your entire head,” says art director and animator Aïda de Ridder. “There’s something stuck to your face and it moves with you, right? We have a static phone, so we have to make their performance more centred. There is a limit to the range of movements.”

In order to make models feel “animated”, the team found that a performer needs to exaggerate their facial features to convey the desired result. But Wispfire is well acquainted with this, thanks to a shared background in interactive theatre where, as Van der Schilden says, “It’s harder to see for people in the back if you do a muted performance.”

Vampires shine the most when they have strong personalities and distinct looks. In Red Kiss, many elements intersect with one another to give characters power and purpose. And this is all being funnelled through a queer and dark lens – like the best vampire stories are.

“We like queer media, “says Van der Schilden. “But we, as a team, always felt that a lot of the media was very pink rainbows, happy-go-lucky. We wanted to make a really dark, queer, diverse game. And I think Red Kiss is that.”

Red Kiss is currently in development for PC, with a release date to be announced.

title: Samsung's New Galaxy Smart Glasses Aim for Style and Google Integration
description: Samsung and Google are making a serious play for people's faces.
fullText:
At its Unpacked event in London this morning, Samsung closed its presentation with a surprise, dropping some details on its soon-to-be-released line of smart glasses. Designed in partnership with Warby Parker, Gentle Monster, and Google, Samsung's "Galaxy Glasses" (if that's what they wind up being called) are audio-, AI-, and camera-focused.

Like Meta's Ray-Ban and Oakley glasses, these glasses will not have a display (although Samsung display glasses are reportedly in development), but they will be integrated into the Google ecosystem. The Galaxy Glasses (or whatever) will launch this fall in a variety of fashion-forward designs from Gentle Monster and Warby Parker.

All models of Samsung's upcoming smart glasses will all feature the same tech under the hood.

Operating system: Runs on Google's Android XR platform.

AI agent: Integrated with Gemini Live AI.

Processor: Powered by Qualcomm's Snapdragon AR 1 Gen 1 chip.

Controls: Wear OS integration allows control from Galaxy Watches

Battery life: Up to nine hours of battery life per charge, plus seven full charges from the carrying case.

Camera and audio: Front-facing camera with outward privacy LED indicator and with dual open-ear speakers.

Samsung highlighted several practical use cases for the eyewear during the Unpacked presentation, including looking at a whiteboard to auto-save information into Samsung Notes, making restaurant reservations and coffee orders on the fly, and taking pictures and video by touching the frame.

Here are some of the "missing pieces" about these glasses.

The price: No information has been released about what they'll cost.

Release date: While they are scheduled for "this fall," the exact release date has yet to be revealed.

Camera features: Megapixel count, resolution, and field-of-view specs have yet to be specified.

Offline capability: It's not clear how much AI processing will happen through the onboard Snapdragon chip, and how much will rely on a connection to the cloud/your phone.

Samsung's entry into the display-free smart glasses space share a lot of features with Meta's line of smart glasses—both include onboard cameras, open-air speakers, and a heavy focus on AI—but there are some distinct differences as well. Here are some areas where Samsung aims to set itself apart from its primary competitor:

The ecosystem: If you're already rocking a Samsung phone and Galaxy Watch, these glasses will lock right into your device setup. But the big selling point may turn out to be their utility with Google: Glasses that fully integrate with Google products means you'll be able to get instant Google Maps navigation, create events in your Google Calendar, use Google's live translation, use Google's Workplace apps, and more.

The AI: While Meta's glasses are tied to Meta AI, Samsung's will use Google's Gemini Live. Whether that's a plus or a minus is a matter of personal preference, of course.

Design: For most of us, finding a pair of glasses that look good is at least as important as which AI agent they connect to. Samsung partnering with both a "trendy" eyewear company like Gentle Monster and a more everyday company like Warby Parker promises to give you more choices, so you can find a pair that's flattering instead of making do with the clunky default option.

title: IDF says Palestinian soccer players it targeted served Hamas and Jihad terror groups
description: The IDF claims Palestinian soccer players killed during the war allegedly served as terrorists for Hamas and Islamic Jihad in operational roles.
fullText:
EXCLUSIVE: The Israeli Defense Forces (IDF) has told Fox News Digital that soccer players representing the Palestinian Authority it targeted with military strikes were allegedly also working as terrorists for Hamas and Jihadist groups.

"In recent weeks, false claims have been circulated alleging that the IDF deliberately targeted innocent Palestinian soccer players throughout the war," the IDF said in a statement to Fox News Digital.

"In light of these claims, the IDF is now revealing the identities of Palestinian soccer players, referees, and coaches who were presented as athletes and innocent civilians, but who in fact served as terrorists in the military wings of the Hamas and Islamic Jihad terrorist organizations in the Gaza Strip, and were struck and eliminated by the IDF during the war.

Idf Claims To Have Taken Out Hamas Commander Who Participated In Oct 7

"Following an intelligence assessment, the IDF can now disclose these terrorists' organizational affiliations and operational roles. In many cases, they were portrayed internationally only as players, with no mention of their affiliation with terrorist organizations."

The IDF identified the players as Mohammed Mohammed Hassan Barakat, Mahmoud Kamal Mohammad Ali Al-Rifi, Mohammed Mansour, Ahmed Mohammed Hassan Abu Al-Atta, Abdullah Riyadh Abdullah Khattab, Ali Mahmoud Ahmed Al-Kurd, Mohammad Emad Ata Hussouna, Tariq Ziad Hussein Al-Hour, Mohammed Ziad Hussein Al-Hour, Mahmoud Osama Al-Jazzar and Mohammad Nidal Mohammed Al-Hawajri.

According to the IDF, three of the players were allegedly involved in the Oct. 7, 2023 massacre, and others allegedly served in other terror operations and leadership roles.

"The false campaign portraying the IDF as deliberately targeting members of the soccer community, which was created as part of a broader effort promoted by various actors against the State of Israel, accompanies other false narratives advanced throughout the war," the IDF added.

"In those campaigns, Palestinian terrorists struck and eliminated by the IDF were presented as journalists, humanitarian aid workers, and other civilian professionals, while their involvement in terrorist activity was deliberately concealed in order to promote false claims that they had been targeted because of their civilian professions."

Fox News Digital has reached out to the Permanent Observer Mission of the State of Palestine to the United Nations for a response to the IDF's claims.

Iran Allegedly Used Soccer Clubs To Spy On Citizens, Opposition Group Urges Fifa Ban Regime Before World Cup

The official Palestinian Football Association (PFA) reported earlier this month that about 1,009 members of its athletic community had been killed by IDF forces since the war began in October 2023, according to Al Jazeera.

The IDF's exclusive disclosures come against the backdrop of a fierce diplomatic and public relations battle on the global sports stage.

Click Here For More Sports Coverage On Foxnews.com

For over two years, the Palestinian Football Association, headed by Jibril Rajoub, has aggressively lobbied the International Olympic Committee (IOC) and FIFA to suspend Israel from international competition. Rajoub and the PFA have repeatedly cited the mounting death toll of Palestinian athletes and the destruction of sports infrastructure in Gaza as grounds for an immediate, indefinite ban.

The campaign reached a boiling point at recent FIFA Congresses. However, following extensive legal reviews, FIFA's Disciplinary Committee ultimately rejected the PFA's demands to suspend Israel entirely.

Instead, FIFA recently opted to impose a fine on the Israel Football Association (IFA) for unrelated disciplinary breaches and ordered the Israeli team to display a "Football Unites the World – No to Discrimination" banner during upcoming matches.

However, FIFA did not let the IFA walk away completely untouched. In a compromise measure, FIFA's Disciplinary Committee opted to penalize the Israeli association for unrelated disciplinary breaches.

The IFA was fined 150,000 Swiss francs (approximately $190,700) for multiple breaches of anti-discrimination obligations. Additionally, FIFA ordered the IFA to implement a mandatory anti-discrimination prevention plan and required the Israeli team to prominently display "Football Unites the World – No to Discrimination" banners during upcoming matches.

title: The Out-of-Touch Adults' Guide to Kid Culture: What Does 'Quiet on the Creek' Mean?
description: It's time to decode this week’s internet kid culture, slang, and viral memes.
fullText:
The Out-of-Touch Adults' Guide is your roadmap to what the younger people are up to. This week, discussions of what "quiet on the creek" means, why kids are calling each other "Tylenol babies," and what "corny on the bob, off the bob" means take us into some surprisingly choppy water. Because just like all of us, young people are wrestling with cultural appropriation, questionable health advisories, and issues of race and identity. But they're doing it with memes.

Kids are exhorting one another to be "quiet on the creek" this week, usually in a stage whisper. The phrase's definition should be familiar to any angler: You have to be quiet on the creek so you don't scare the fish away. Extend that a bit and you have a colorful way of saying "keep it on the down low" or "be cool."

But there's a second more more charged meaning of "quiet on the creek." When it originally took off online, clueless people asked "what does it mean?" and the response from many was to not explain it, to gatekeep the definition, first because it was regarded as AAVE and people are understandably tired of AAVE being co-opted, but also because when slang is explained, the fizz disappears and you end up with the verbal equivalent of flat champagne.

Among those exhorting people to not talk about quiet on the creek is the rapper who popularized the phrase, Saucy Santana. Saucy posted this video exhorting others to be more quiet on the creek about quiet on the creek:

All respect to Santana, but it's not hard to suss out the definition of "quiet on the creek" if your dad ever took you fishing. I understand the impulse to protect slang from being watered down, but the internet is relentless: Once a phrase goes viral, no matter where it came from, the original context is stripped away, and people are going to say what they say, regardless of the politics behind it. Anyway, you should check out Santana's song and support their career because they are good at this shizz.

Controversy aside, the phrase has become popular enough that it's being used in a ton of online videos of people in movies and TV being quiet on the creek, like this:

Since we're on the subject of over-exposure watering down Black people's slang and culture online, let's talk about the strange case of "That's Just the Way It Is." Early in the week, the hashtag thatsjustthewayitis was combined with the chorus of Tupac's "Changes" and used to comment on racism in videos like these:

Soon after "that's just the way it is" took off, people predicted how it would be coopted, posting videos like these that are basically parodies of how white people would make the meme about them, substituting legit concerns for mistaken Starbucks orders and whatnot:

Depressingly, this kind of co-optation happens all the time, but in this case, I'm having trouble finding many actual examples of videos of the phrase/meme being co-opted by white people. There are a few out there, but they seem to be getting way fewer views than the commentary on them.

I have no doubt this phrase would have been more widely misunderstood and misused as it became more popular, but there doesn't seem to have been enough time. So the "that's just the way it is" meme has become about the predictable adoption of Black culture, while skipping "the middleman" of the actual co-opting, almost like a preemptive defense against cultural gentrification. And that's without even mentioning that Tupac's song is built around the chorus to Bruce Hornsby's "The Way it is," which is one of whitest songs ever.

In September 2025, President Trump and Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. held a press conference announcing a scientifically dubious link between the use of acetaminophen (the active ingredient in Tylenol) by pregnant women and autism. Kids immediately weaponized "Tylenol baby" and have used it ever since to roast their pals.

Middle schoolers tend to care more about finding new ways to insult each other than they do about FDA research, so the phrase generally isn't meant to suggest anyone actually has autism. It's a way for people who don't know any better (yet, hopefully) to use the societal stigma of autism to say, "you're a dope" or "you're acting foolish."

The final slang phrase in this week's dive into youth and race relations is "corny on the bob," it means "corn on the cob," and has not been co-opted or stolen from AAVE, nor does it insult anyone with a disability. Instead, it's a catchphrase of Patty Miller, an unexpected online star whose cooking and eating videos regularly rack up millions of views based on her unique personal presentation and her colorful way of pronouncing words. In this video, she shows off the "liver and engines" she made and her "corny on the bob, off the bob."

Look, I wouldn't eat that slop with your mouth, but that doesn't mean I don't have a place in my heart for the internet's new meemaw.

title: New York City landlords warn American dream is slipping away under socialist rent policies
description: New York landlords push back against Democratic Socialists of America rent freeze calls, saying property taxes, insurance and mortgages erode profits.
fullText:
Democratic Socialists' calls to freeze rents and reject double-digit investment returns for landlords are fueling fears among some New York City property owners, who say years of rising costs and restrictive housing policies are pushing family-run businesses to the brink.

"It shows how flawed the system is in its entirety and how inefficient everything is here," New York landlord Jose Tur told Fox News Tuesday.

Tur, whose late grandfather spent decades building the family's New York real estate business after emigrating from Cuba, said the debate over landlord profits overlooks the hardships facing many small property owners.

Nyc Rent Board Dissenter Warns Mamdani-backed Freeze Could Hurt Affordable Housing Over Time: 'Slow Burn'

He recalled how his grandfather, who purchased his first apartment building in 1979, spent decades growing the family business before he was reportedly mugged at gunpoint by a tenant who had not paid rent in five years. Tur said his grandfather suffered a stroke and died two weeks later at age 99.

Fellow New York landlord Natalia Bonanno also relayed her family's struggle amid what she characterized as growing attacks from the far left: mounting operating costs that have made it increasingly difficult to keep rent-stabilized buildings financially viable.

She told "America's Newsroom" that her father came to the U.S. as an immigrant with a dream. He became the first in his family to earn a college degree and later became an engineer before deciding he wanted to own property.

"He started to invest also in the '70s and buying properties in Brooklyn and then passed them down to my sister and I," Bonanno recalled.

Today, Bonanno said that dream is becoming increasingly difficult to sustain. She disputed claims that landlords are routinely earning double-digit returns, arguing such figures ignore the rising costs of mortgages, insurance, taxes and the expense of maintaining aging rent-stabilized buildings, many of which were constructed in the early 20th century.

Steve Forbes: Mamdani's Socialist Rent-control Puts New York On The Road To Housing Ruin

"I don't know where they're getting 12% from," Bonanno said, responding to remarks made Friday by New York City Democratic Socialists of America co-Chair Gustavo Gordillo.

"I know a lot of people refer to the NOI figure that came out recently, and that doesn't take into account our mortgage and insurance," she added.

Gordillo, speaking to "The Story" host Martha MacCallum last week, argued that landlords should not expect double-digit investment returns and dismissed claims that rent freezes would drive property owners out of business.

Tur also disputed Gordillo's comments, arguing they were not made in reference to rent-stabilized properties, which he and Bonanno both own.

When asked whether they planned to remain in the business, Tur said he hopes to continue the work his grandfather spent decades building, describing it as a responsibility to both his family and the tenants he has known for years.

"I think it's the right thing to do on behalf of my family and what my grandfather built and also on behalf of the tenants," Tur said.

"These are people that we've known for several years. It's not like we're a huge company that's not present. We're boots on the ground."

Bonanno, however, said her family's future in New York is far less certain.

"We're selling most of our rent-stabilized buildings because we just don't see a future here for having profit," she said.

"We hope we could stay in New York City and maybe invest in non-rent-stabilized properties, but if not, we'll have to just sell and purchase properties in other states."

Original article

title: How to Preorder the Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8, Z Fold 8 Ultra, or Z Flip 8
description: Samsung's latest foldables are now available to preorder.
fullText:
During its July Unpacked event, Samsung officially unveiled its next-generation of foldable Galaxy devices. There's the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra, which, despite the name, is really the successor to the Galaxy Z Fold 7. The Galaxy Z Fold 8, instead, is actually a new device, introducing a lighter design (and a slightly smaller price tag) than the Z Fold 8 Ultra. Finally, there's the Z Flip 8, which actually doesn't change much from the Z Flip 7.

If you're in the market for a new foldable smartphone, these new devices might be on your radar. Even the Z Flip 8's minor adjustments might prove a worthwhile upgrade if your Flip device is more than a few years old. While you can't buy one today, you can preorder any of them right now.

The Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra is Samsung's premium foldable for 2026. Out of Samsung's newest products, it has the biggest and best cameras (200MP Wide, 50MP Ultra Wide, and 10MP Tele), the largest displays (6.5-inch outer display, 8-inch inner display), the biggest battery (5,000 mAh), and, unfortunately, comes with the biggest price tag. If you're interested, you can preorder from the following links:

Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra (256 GB): $2,099.99 at Samsung

Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra (512 GB): $2,299.99 at Samsung

Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra (1TB): $2,699.99 at Samsung

A lot of people are going to think the Z Fold 8 is the upgraded version of the Z Fold 7, but it's not. This is a lighter take on Samsung's foldable, which might appeal to users who want a phablet that takes up a little less space in their pocket. It comes with a 5.5-inch outer display, a 7.6-inch inner display, three rear cameras (50MP Wide, 50MP Ultra Wide, and 2x Optical Zoom), and the same Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 chip as the rest of the "Z 8" line. With that lighter framework comes a slightly smaller MSRP (emphasis on slightly), as the Z Fold 8 starts at $1,899.99. Here are the preorder links from Samsung:

Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 (256 GB): $1,899.99 at Samsung

Samsung Galaxy Z Fold 8 (512 GB): $2,099.99 at Samsung

Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (1TB): $2,499.99 at Samsung

The Z Flip 8 isn't much changed from the Z Flip 7. It comes with a 4.1-inch outer display and a 6.9-inch inner display (though the peak brightness increases to 2600 nits from 1600 nits), 12GB of RAM, and similar cameras to the Z Flip 7, including a 50MP Wide, 12MP Ultra Wide, and 10X Digital Zoom. (That said, it does have a new 2x optical zoom.) It also upgrades the SoC from the Exynos 2500

As such, this isn't the device you upgrade to if you bought Samsung's flip phone last year. Still, it's the company's flagship Z Flip, so if you're looking to upgrade, or thinking about picking up a flip phone for the first time, it's one to consider. Here's where to preorder:

Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (256 GB): $1,199.99 at Samsung

Samsung Galaxy Z Flip 8 (512GB): $1,399.99 at Samsung

title: If You Loved 'House of the Dragon,' There's One Movie You Need to Watch Next
description: Looking for more dragon battles and epic personality clashes?
fullText:
House of the Dragon is getting so good, I'm flirting with calling it a more satisfying watch than Game of Thrones. After two seasons of thrilling political maneuvering, betrayals, and the occasional dragonfire massacre, the story has really clicked into place, and I've found myself deeply invested in the travails of the battling Targaryen dynasties, even though I technically know how everything turns out. (It is a prequel, after all.)

If each new episode leaves you craving more sharp writing and spectacular dragon battles, you’ve got a lot of other series to keep you fed going forward. But if you want a more cinematic experience that offers similar pleasures, there’s one obvious choice: Reign of Fire, the 2002 sci-fantasy epic starring two obscure actors named Matthew McConaughey and Christian Bale.

Reign of Fire is set in a world where a tunneling project in modern-day London accidentally awakens hibernating dragons—real, fire-breathing, flying lizards just like the ones in fantasy novels. The dragons quickly breed and decimate the world’s militaries, and in the chaotic aftermath, nuclear weapons are unleashed, leaving the human race teetering on the edge of extinction—and the dragons starving. When the few surviving humans realize that the dragons have a huge weakness that could be exploited to end their dominance, a desperate plan is hatched to save humanity.

While that plot doesn't sound similar to the high fantasy trappings of House of the Dragon, there's an obvious throughline: It's the dragon warfare, silly. House of the Dragon is filled with absorbing characters (team Daemon all day) and fascinating, twisty plots as the Targaryen family rips itself apart in a struggle for the Iron Throne, but if you say you’re not here for the dragon fights, you’re lying. Reign of Fire has dragon fights. Lots of dragon fights. Dragon fights that involve a tank and a helicopter. The effects hold up more than 20 years after the film’s release, too.

But the film isn’t just dragon fights—there’s a proper story too. At a castle in England, a man who witnessed the initial dragon awakening, Quinn (Christian Bale), is leading a rag-tag group of desperate folks trying to survive until the dragons die off from lack of food. Things are grim, and Quinn struggles to hold his community together. When a group of Americans called the Kentucky Irregulars, led by Denton Van Zan (Matthew McConaughey, looking badass with a shaved head and crazy eyes), arrives with a tank and a functioning helicopter, Quinn can’t stop some of his people from signing up to help them fight dragons.

Not only do McConaughey and Bale lay down terrific performances as desperate men with opposing visions of victory, the people following them are also portrayed as a complex group with various dynamics. People do unexpected things, turning what could have been a straightforward action film into an absorbing sci-fi story. If you enjoy the twists and turns of House of the Dragon, you’ll find a lot to love in Reign of Fire, and you can rent it on Prime Video.

Reign of Fire is an hour and forty minutes of dragony goodness, but if it leaves you wanting more, there are a few more movies you can check out—including Christopher Nolan’s currently-in-theaters epic The Odyssey, which offers a universe of monsters, epic battles, and vengeful gods that looks like a perfect fit for fans of House of the Dragon. Here are a few other movies you can watch right now on your screen of choice:

In one sense, Valhalla Rising couldn’t be more different from House of the Dragon. Where the show is often loud and action-packed, Valhalla Rising can be disturbingly still and quiet—on purpose. Set in 12th-century Scotland, Mads Mikkelsen plays an unnamed slave with one eye who possesses incredible strength. Forced to fight other slaves to the death, One-Eye sees visions that help him escape, launching him on a violent journey to his destiny. It’s a moody, bloody story that shares a sense of Hell on Earth with the show, depicting beautiful, brutal landscapes where death is always lurking. Stream Valhalla Rising on Kanopy.

If you want more epic battles and doomed autocrats desperately trying to hang onto power (and their lives), Kingdom of Heaven is a great choice. Set during the Crusades era in the 12th century, the film follows a newly-knighted noble, Balian (Orlando Bloom), who travels to the Crusader Kingdom of Jerusalem in search of salvation. Instead, he finds a delicate, complicated political situation as the ailing Leper King, Baldwin IV, struggles to maintain a fragile peace for his unstable kingdom. If you want more of the Blacks/Greens political skulduggery and more huge battles between armies, stream this (sorry, no dragons though). Rent Kingdom of Heaven on Prime Video.

Want more dragons? J.R.R. Tolkien’s Smaug is one of the most entertaining and terrifying dragons ever conceived—and when he’s voiced by Benedict Cumberbatch, it’s a pure delight. Yes, Smaug the dragon talks, and isn’t a vicious aerial killing machine like Vhagar, but his smoothly malevolent evil permeates the film with a sense of danger and dread. Plus, Middle-earth is the heavyweight of epic fantasy lore and world-building, so if the deep history and complexity of Westeros is what you crave, any of Peter Jackson’s films set in Tolkien’s universe are a great choice. Rent The Hobbit: The Desolation of Smaug on Prime Video.

Lawrence of Arabia is a clear visual influence on House of the Dragon, and the actors have namechecked David Lean’s 1962 epic as inspiration for their performances. Based on the true story of T. E. Lawrence and adapted from his book Seven Pillars of Wisdom, the film depicts the brilliant life and career of Lawrence, who was instrumental in organizing the Arab tribes against the Ottoman Empire in World War I. It’s as widescreen an epic as old Hollywood ever made, and Peter O’Toole makes Lawrence into the conflicted antihero of Daemon Targaryen’s dreams. Stream Lawrence of Arabia on Philo or rent it on Prime Video.

If you love the politics and strategy on display in House of the Dragon (without sacrificing the bloody battles), and like the realistic, mud- and blood-splattered depiction of medieval life in the show, check out The King. Loosely adapted from William Shakespeare’s plays, a young Timothée Chalamet portrays King Henry V as he rises from a lazy prince to one of the most brilliant warrior kings in English history—the king who claimed the crown of France, if only for a moment. It’s a viscerally exciting film, and one of its great pleasures is the battle of wills and tactics (and insults) between Henry and the heir to the French throne, Louis, Duke of Guyenne (Robert Pattinson). Stream The King on Netflix.

title: Here's What Actually Counts As ‘Moderate Exercise’
description: Your’re supposed to get 150 minutes of it per week, but how do you know when you're doing it?
fullText:
Exercise is a healthy and, one might argue, necessary part of our lives. The guidelines from public health organizations tell you to aim for at least 150 minutes of “moderate” exercise each week, and that each minute of “vigorous” exercise counts double. That raises the question: What the hell is “moderate” exercise? How do you know when you’re doing it?

Moderate exercise is sometimes conflated with "zone 2" exercise. These two things aren't exactly the same, but they do overlap a bit. I'll get into the difference below. Moderate exercise includes activities like walking and casual bicycling, and it counts whether you think of it as a workout or not. Getting enough moderate exercise has benefits for your heart, metabolism, mental health, and more—so let's dig in to what it is and how you can know when you're doing it.

If you use a fitness tracker like the Fitbit Air, it may try to tell you that "moderate" exercise occurs when you reach a certain heart rate. This isn't actually the case. The scientists who came up with the exercise guidelines didn't use heart rate as their guide. Instead, they used charts that measured how many "METs," or metabolic equivalents, each type of exercise requires.

I'll explain more about METs below, but the important thing to know is that the type and intensity of exercise is what tells you whether it's moderate, not your heart rate while you're doing it. You can look up exercises on a chart like this one, but here's the basic idea:

Walking is moderate exercise; jogging or running is vigorous.

Bicycling is moderate if it’s under about 12 miles per hour on the flat.

Indoor cycling is moderate if it’s up to about 100 watts of power.

You don’t have to overthink it. The guidelines that mention “moderate” and “vigorous” activities aren’t asking you to monitor your heart rate or any other numeric metric. They want you thinking in terms of generalities: walking versus running, leisurely bike commuting versus sweating your heart out in a spin studio.

And honestly, if it’s easier to watch your heart rate than to worry about the above, that’s fine. For most of us, 150 minutes of Zone 2 is going to be at least 150 minutes of moderate exercise. So follow that guideline, and you’ll be an overachiever.

So why does your watch want to steer you toward a certain heart rate during exercise? Mostly because that's easy to measure. Moderate exercise sorta-kinda matches up to "zone 2" cardio, which I’ve previously explained. (Zone 2 is is the second-lowest intensity in a five-zone system, and the easiest way to know which zone you're in is to glance at your fitness watch.)

While you can use heart rate zones as a rule of thumb to find moderate exercise, this idea falls short in two ways: (1) different gadgets and systems use different cutoffs to decide which numbers count as "zone 2", and (2) for most of us, zone 2 includes most moderate activities but also some vigorous activities. If you’re relatively fit, you can jog at a 12 minute-per-mile pace while keeping your heart rate in zone 2. That’s a “vigorous” activity in terms of METs, though.

The other reason heart rate isn’t accurate for this task is that your heart rate changes for all kinds of reasons. The hotter it is when you're working out, the higher your heart rate tends to be. Same goes for when you’re nervous or stressed. And as you get fitter, you’ll be able to do the same activities at a lower heart rate. Those activities might feel easier than they used to, but they’re not any less work.

One MET is the energy expended when you’re at rest—the amount of oxygen, calories, etc that it takes to keep you alive and breathing. (We use oxygen in the process of burning calories, so officially a MET is 3.2 milliliters of oxygen per kilogram of body weight per minute.)

Researchers can then put a mask on a person and measure how much oxygen they use while running, walking, playing guitar, etc. If an activity takes twice as much oxygen as sitting still, they say it takes two METs. Here are a few examples (taken from this scientific paper):

2 MET: washing dishes, or playing croquet

3 MET: walking at 3 miles per hour (a pretty typical walking pace)

4 MET: table tennis, ice skating

5 MET: modern dance, fast-paced ballroom dance

6 MET: volleyball, singles tennis

The numbers go up from there. Speed skating clocks in at 15 MET. To be clear, you will not be measuring METs directly when you exercise. The MET studies are done in labs so that we can use the information to get a sense of what MET values each common type of exercise tends to have.

The physical activity guidelines define “moderate” exercise as at least 3 MET, but less than 6. Vigorous is 6 MET or more. Because METs are specific to the activity, not to how fit you are, it makes the most sense to look at METs in terms of the pace you run or the settings you use on your treadmill or other cardio machine. Here are paces and activities that have been clocked as between 3 and 6 METs:

Walking at 3-4 mph (a 15-minute to 20-minute mile)

Cycling, between 50 to 100 watts

Taking a low-impact aerobics class

And these are vigorous (6 or more MET):

Jogging (a 12-minute mile is 8.0 MET; the faster you go, the higher the MET)

Bicycling at 12 miles per hour or faster

Playing a game of basketball, soccer, or hockey

So when you're trying to get your 150 minutes (or more) of moderate exercise each week, you can count all your walking, your easy bike riding, and your low-impact aerobics class. Give yourself credit for vigorous exercise for all the time you spend jogging (at any heart rate), lap swimming, or rucking. You may find you've been doing more exercise than you think.

title: Preorder the New Samsung Galaxy Z Phones and Get up to $350 in Amazon Gift Cards
description: The Galaxy Z Flip 8, Galaxy Z Fold 8, and Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra phones are all up for grabs with Amazon gift cards and trade-in credits.
fullText:
Samsung announced five new products during its Galaxy Unpacked event. Three of those are the new flagship smartphones: the Galaxy Z Fold 8, Z Fold 8 Ultra, and Z Flip 8. Samsung already has them up for pre-order, but Amazon launched its own pre-order deals, which are objectively better. You can get an Amazon gift card up to $350, while Samsung is offering a max of $200 in Samsung credits. And if you're looking to switch to T-Mobile, you can get a Galaxy Z Fold 8 on them. You can read more details on that deal here.

If you were expecting a shiny new toy from the Z Flip 8, you might be disappointed—it's not that different from the Z Flip 7. It has a 4.1-inch outer display and a 6.9-inch inner display, 12GB of RAM, and similar cameras to the Z Flip 7, including a 50MP Wide, 12MP Ultra Wide, and 10X Digital Zoom. Upgrades over the previous model include peak brightness that's 1000 nits brighter, now reaching 2600 nits; there is a new 2x optical zoom; and the processor upgrades the SoC from the Exynos 2500.

As Senior Tech Editor Jake Peterson says, the Z Fold 8 is not the upgraded version of the Z Fold 7: It's a brand new device which might appeal to users who want a phablet that takes up a little less space in their pocket. You can expect a 5.5-inch outer display, a 7.6-inch inner display, three rear cameras (50MP Wide, 50MP Ultra Wide, and 2x Optical Zoom), and the same Snapdragon 8 Elite Gen 5 chip as the rest of the "Z 8" line.

The flagship foldable Samsung phone for 2026 is the Galaxy Z Fold 8 Ultra. It has the biggest and best cameras (200MP Wide, 50MP Ultra Wide, and 10MP Tele), the largest displays (6.5-inch outer display, 8-inch inner display), and the biggest battery (5,000 mAh).

title: WhatsApp Just Announced Four New Features, Including a Huge Upgrade for CarPlay and Android Auto
description: WhatsApp now works as it should when you're in the car.
fullText:
Samsung might have just announced a bunch of new products during its July Unpacked event, but that's not stopping WhatsApp from trying to steal headlines. As Samsung was unveiling the latest Galaxy Z devices, WhatsApp announced four new features that users on both Android and iOS can take advantage of right now. Here's what's new:

The goal of CarPlay and Android Auto is to make smartphone apps safe to use while on the road. You shouldn't text and drive, but if you can hear messages and respond to them with your voice while keeping your attention where it should be, that's a win-win. Unfortunately, that wasn't really the case with WhatsApp's previous experience in the car: You could send new messages and place WhatsApp calls, but that's it, and that made WhatsApp a much more limiting experience than other car chat apps.

According to WhatsApp, that's no longer the case. The latest update overhauls the experience, finally letting you listen to and respond to your messages. In addition to placing calls, you can see your call history, so you can check whether you missed a call from somebody. You can also access your list of favorite contacts, which should make it easier to start quick calls and messages to the friends and family you talk with most often.

With this update, you can now open PDFs directly in WhatsApp on the web or in the desktop app without downloading them first. What's more, you can edit those PDFs after opening them, including highlighting and annotating, while remaining in the app. WhatsApp says you can view and edit these PDFs via Adobe Acrobat, putting WhatsApp in the running against other lightweight and free PDF apps on the market.

If you're listening to a song you really like on Apple Music or Spotify, you can share it across many different social media apps, and that now includes WhatsApp, too. The company says you can share tracks from these music apps to your WhatsApp Status, which, presumably, will let your friends and family check out the song themselves in Apple Music or Spotify.

It took a while, but WhatsApp finally got an iPad app in May of 2025. Now, over a year later, WhatsApp is letting users create an account directly from WhatsApp on iPad, without needing to tie the account to a phone number. If you or someone you know uses an iPad as their main device, this should be some welcome news.

title: Canada reportedly scraps joint bridge celebration with US after Trump renews tariff threat
description: Canada canceled a joint celebration for the Gordie Howe International Bridge, connecting Detroit to Windsor, Ontario, after President Donald Trump renewed tariff threats.
fullText:
Canada on Tuesday called off a joint celebration with the United States to mark the opening of the Gordie Howe International Bridge, following renewed tariff threats from President Donald Trump.

The bridge — which connects Detroit and Windsor, Ontario — was named after legendary Canadian hockey player Gordie Howe who played for the Detroit Red Wings, symbolizing the longstanding relationship between the two countries.

Trump on Monday announced plans to impose a 50% tariff on certain Canadian imports, citing what officials called trade discrimination against American companies in the dairy, auto and alcohol industries. The White House said Canada's policies gave foreign competitors an unfair advantage.

"In light of trade action threatened by the United States earlier this week, it would be inappropriate to proceed with a celebratory event ​between the two countries," Jenna Ghassabeh, a spokesperson for Canadian ​Infrastructure Minister Gregor Robertson, told Reuters in a statement.

Trump Says He Will Block Us-canada Bridge Unless Canada Negotiates On Trade

The bridge was scheduled to open to traffic next Monday, and American and Canadian officials were expected to attend a ribbon-cutting ceremony on Friday, Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens previously told local media, according to Reuters.

Ghassabeh confirmed to Reuters that the bridge will still open next week, but the celebration will be held only "among Canadians."

Canadian Pm Carney Fires Back At Trump Over Claim That 'Canada Lives Because Of The United States'

The multibillion-bridge project has faced multiple setbacks amid strained relations between the United States and Canada.

In February, Trump threatened to block the project after accusing Canada of unfair trade practices and criticizing its relationship with China. He cited Ontario's removal of U.S. alcohol products from its liquor store shelves as an example of what he described as unfair treatment.

The bridge opening was later cleared to proceed after the U.S. and Canada reached a deal on how tolls would be charged to drivers earlier this month.

The new bridge is expected to ease traffic on the privately owned Ambassador Bridge, the largest commercial border crossing between the two countries, which also connects Detroit and Windsor.

The port of entry handled $126 billion in trade carried by commercial trucks in 2023, Reuters reported.

Original article

title: Preorder the Samsung Galaxy Watch 9 Now and Get a $50 Amazon Gift Card
description: The deal effectively brings the $379.99 Bluetooth model down to $329.99.
fullText:
Samsung just announced a ton of new products at its July Unpacked event, and among them is the highly anticipated Galaxy Watch 9. It’s now available for preorder, and Amazon is sweetening the launch with a free $50 gift card on both the 40mm and 44mm models. With the bonus credit, the 40mm Samsung Galaxy Watch 9 is down to $329.99 (originally $379.99) while the 44mm version is down to $409.99 (originally $459.99), making this a solid deal for those who would find an Amazon gift card useful.

This is not a direct discount, but it’s a great preorder perk for anyone already planning to buy Samsung’s newest everyday smartwatch. The Galaxy Watch 9 has a slimmer 8.6mm case and an AMOLED screen that reaches a peak brightness of 3,000 nits. It’s powered by the updated Qualcomm SW6100 chip for better CPU and GPU performance, with 2GB of RAM and 32GB of storage.  It has an IP68 rating and can withstand depths of up to 50 meters of water, plus military durability testing that makes it more rugged than before.

The Watch 9 adds new AI-powered wellness features, including Vitals, Fitness Index, and an updated Running Coach, as well as the ability to start listening for instructions as soon as you raise your wrist to talk, no wake word required. Updates also include a fitness feature that claims to measure flexibility and added durability for water sports like scuba diving. Battery life is improved, too: The 40mm model has a 390mAh battery and is rated for up to 30 hours with the always-on display enabled, while the 44mm model has a 445mAh battery. That is an increase from the Galaxy Watch 8’s 325mAh and 435mAh batteries, respectively.

The Samsung Galaxy Watch 9 is scheduled to arrive on August 7, but Amazon’s $50 gift-card offer could disappear before then. Because this is a preorder promotion rather than a cash sale, it only makes sense if you were already considering the Watch 9 and can put the gift card to use. While it is not a must-have upgrade for Galaxy Watch 8 owners, its slimmer design, brighter display, newer processor, larger batteries, and expanded health and fitness tools are all noteworthy improvements.

title: Eccles and Salmon given Team Wales honour in Glasgow
description: Reigning Commonwealth Games champions Rosie Eccles and Daniel Salmon are named as Team Wales' flag and baton bearers for the opening ceremony in Glasgow.
fullText:
Reigning Commonwealth Games champions Rosie Eccles and Daniel Salmon have been chosen as Team Wales' flag and baton bearers for the opening ceremony in Glasgow.

Boxer Eccles and bowler Salmon were surprised with the news by Team Wales chef de mission Gethin Jones.

Eccles will celebrate her 30th birthday by carrying the flag and leading Wales out at the Hydro on Thursday night.

The Caldicot boxer is competing at her third and final Games having won silver on the Gold Coast in 2018 and gold in Birmingham.

"I don't think I've stopped smiling, I was speechless and it's not often that happens," said Eccles.

"It is unreal, a huge honour. I feel so fiercely proud to represent Wales again anyway and have my shot at gold for the team.

"But then to carry the flag and lead some amazing athletes out, it doesn't get much better than that."

Eccles recalls watching Olympic cycling champion Geraint Thomas carry out the flag at Birmingham four years ago.

"I remember thinking how proud he must feel and never did I think that could be me in the following Games," said Eccles.

"I'm taken aback that they've picked me."

Team Wales named for Glasgow Commonwealth Games

Can Wales find new Commonwealth legends in Glasgow?

Baton bearer Salmon, who turned 32 yesterday, was emotional when he was informed of the honour.

Salmon, from Cardiff, will compete in the men's pairs bowls alongside Ross Owen, and is hoping for a third Games gold medal by defending the titles he won in 2018 and 2022.

"I just play bowls I guess that's what is in my head, just going quietly about my business and trying to win medals for Wales," said Salmon.

"That's why I never thought this would have happened, but it's quite amazing."

Team Wales had already announced Paralympic athlete Olivia Breen will be the team captain for the Games before giving Eccles and Salmon the ceremonial honour.

"They're brilliant ambassadors for their country," said Jones.

"They're very proud Welsh people and are the right choice.

"I can not wait to see them carry the flag and baton tomorrow night."

title: Zelle must face New York attorney general's lawsuit over fraud, judge rules
description: A judge rejected Zelle's bid to dismiss a lawsuit by New York Attorney General Letitia James, who said the electronic payment platform's refusal to
fullText:
NEW YORK, July 22 (Reuters) - A judge rejected Zelle's bid to dismiss a lawsuit by New York Attorney General Letitia James, who said the electronic payment ‌platform's refusal to adopt critical safety features enabled fraudsters to steal more than $1 billion ‌from consumers.

In a decision on Tuesday, Justice Phaedra Perry-Bond of a New York state court in Manhattan said James sufficiently alleged ​that Zelle's parent Early Warning Services "prioritized accessibility, convenience, consumer adoption, and market dominance at the expense of consumer safety" in rushing the platform to market, despite objections from its banking partners.

What role did banking partners play in Zelle's launch?

How much money did fraudsters steal through Zelle?

What safety features did Zelle allegedly fail to implement?

Why did the judge reject Zelle's dismissal bid?

Early Warning Services is owned by seven large U.S. banks: Bank of America, Capital One, JPMorgan Chase, PNC, Truist, US Bank ‌and Wells Fargo.

Perry-Bond said Zelle also conceded ⁠it is still collecting and retaining fees from fraudulent transactions, raising the question of whether it implicitly or expressly approved what the fraudsters were doing.

James also ⁠objected to how Zelle was marketed to consumers, including claims that the platform offered "peace-of-mind" and that Zelle was "backed by the banks, so you know it's secure."

"Reports of fraud and scams committed by bad ​actors ​against Zelle users have always been exceptionally low," spokesperson ​Eric Blankenbaker said. "The attorney general is targeting ‌our company for political gain by recycling claims that courts across the country have rejected as meritless. The attorney general's claims are not supported by either the facts or the law."

The attorney general's office was not immediately available for comment.

In seeking a dismissal, Zelle had argued it was not misleading to advertise the platform as safe and secure, and there was no liability for ‌what it called "passive nonfeasance" in allegedly creating an atmosphere conducive ​to fraud.

Zelle was launched in 2017, and competes with ​apps such as PayPal's Venmo and Block's ​Cash App.

James said typical scams involved hacking into users' accounts and making unauthorized ‌transfers, convincing users to send money for ​nonexistent goods and services, and ​impersonating banks, government offices and utilities.

She said it wasn't until 2023, after the U.S. Consumer Financial Protection Bureau and several members of Congress began probes, that Zelle adopted "basic" safeguards it ​had proposed four years earlier.

James sued ‌after the CFPB dropped a similar case in March 2025. That agency ended most enforcement ​activity soon after U.S. President Donald Trump began his second White House term.

Barbecue warning after 'preventable' beach fire
description: Firefighters have "strongly advised" people not to use barbecues outside designated areas.
fullText:
Firefighters have issued further warnings about disposable barbecues after a stretch of coastline was badly damaged in a blaze that was "started deliberately".

Lincolnshire Fire and Rescue Service made the plea after 2.7 acres (11,000 sq m) of land at Skegness was left badly scorched on 17 July.

David Lynch, area manager for community prevention and protection, said: "We strongly advise against the use of disposable BBQs outside of strictly designated areas, and this beach fire is another strong example of why."

Officers from Lincolnshire Police said the incident would not be treated as arson as there was no criminal intention to start the fire.

Businesses in the area described their relief after they watched the wind blow the fire away from their kiosks.

"There was a lot of smoke and the flames were just everywhere," said Luke Speed who was working at the Stack Street Food hut on Tower Esplanade.

"We were quite worried it was going to blow in this direction and cause issues over here, but it didn't. It looked quite serious."

John Nuttall, whose family has been operating donkey rides in the seaside town for more than 100 years, had left the beach for the day when the fire started.

"Even from a mile away at home we could see the smoke in the air. There were fire engines everywhere," he said.

"After the hot weather we've been having it's so easy for fires to go up but luckily it blew out towards the sea."

Mandy Whitehead often walks her dog in Skegness and said she was shocked by what had happened.

She said: "It's terrible. The smell and all the greenery and wildlife which might have been affected.

"It's a bit daft if people are having barbecues when it's so dry."

Tracey Firbank was visiting the beach on a day trip and described the scene as "awful".

"I just brought the dog on the beach and could smell the fire. It's awful. There's no need for it," she added.

East Lindsey District Council said it would be working to develop a nature recovery programme for the area which is a Site of Special Scientific Interest.

The authority also said it had issued guidelines for the use of barbecues including a reminder for people to make sure all fires are put out before they leave.

Councillor Martin Foster, the portfolio holder for operational services, said: "I would like to remind visitors of the impact that lighting fires or failing to fully extinguish disposable barbecues can have, particularly on local wildlife.

"I would like to thank the emergency services for their quick response and for working to bring the blaze under control as quickly as possible."

Lincolnshire Fire and Rescue Service said it had faced "non-stop demand" after the recent hot, dry weather conditions had increased the risks of wildfires.

Lynch added: "Already this summer we have seen hundreds of acres of nature destroyed by fires from people incorrectly disposing of cigarettes, lighting BBQs, burning waste, and littering, and all these causes are entirely preventable.

"We obviously want people to enjoy the outdoors this summer, but please do help us keep our communities safer and keep fire safety in mind."

Listen to highlights from Lincolnshire on BBC Sounds, watch the latest episode of Look North.
