title: Philippine, Chinese foreign ministers trade protests over South China Sea encounter
description: MANILA/BEIJING, July 22 (Reuters) - The foreign ministers of the Philippines and China met in Manila on Wednesday and both made "strong" protests over an encounter between the two countries' vessels
fullText:
MANILA/BEIJING, July 22 (Reuters) - The foreign ministers of the Philippines and China met in Manila on Wednesday and both ‌made "strong" protests over an encounter between the two countries' vessels ‌this week in contested South China Sea waters.

The Philippine foreign affairs secretary, Maria Theresa ​Lazaro, said in an X post that she reiterated the protest over what she called "unacceptable actions against Filipino personnel", while Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi was quoted by an official readout as lodging a protest ‌over "the Philippine personnel's egregious ⁠act of ramming Chinese law-enforcement vessels".

Bilateral relations have been tense in recent years amid repeated maritime confrontations ⁠in the South China Sea, which China claims almost entirely as its own despite a 2016 arbitral ruling that invalidated those claims.

The incident this ​week took ​place on Monday in the ​Second Thomas Shoal. The Philippines ‌said Chinese Coast Guard personnel struck one of the Philippine navy staff on the head with a wooden baton, and China said the Philippine personnel were the first to attack and accused Philippine vessels of ramming a Chinese patrol boat.

In the meeting, Wang told Lazaro that ‌China-Philippines relations stood at a crossroads, ​and that the Philippines needed to make ​a "correct and rational" choice regarding ​its future direction, according to a readout released ‌by China's foreign ministry.

Wang also accused "certain ​forces" within the ​Philippine military and police of deliberate provocations, undermining dialogue and "carrying out the orders of external forces".

Lazaro said that the two ​also "discussed the importance of ‌keeping communication lines open, managing tensions, and pursuing practical ways ​forward".

title: Republican Rep María Elvira Salazar declares, 'After Cuba, Nicaragua is next'
description: Criticizing Nicaragua's Daniel Ortega, U.S. Rep. María Elvira Salazar, R-Fla., said in a post on X that "After Cuba, Nicaragua is next.
fullText:
Republican Rep. María Elvira Salazar of Florida stated in a Monday social media post that "After Cuba, Nicaragua is next."

The Central American nation's president, Daniel Ortega, said on Sunday, "There won't be any more elections here for them to try to seize the government and seize power," according to Reuters. "The days when parties backed by the Yankees (United States) and the Somocistas would return to power are over - never again," he said, according to the outlet.

In her Monday post on X, Salazar said, "Daniel Ortega just announced that Nicaragua will no longer hold elections. When a communist dictator knows the people would vote him out, he takes away their vote. Repression, fear, and force become the only way to stay in power. The Ortega-Murillo regime has declared war on the Nicaraguan people's right to choose their own future."

Ortega's wife Rosario Murillo is co-president, reports indicate.

"The United States must stand unequivocally with the people of Nicaragua, increase the pressure on this despicable regime, and ensure that those responsible for its crimes are held accountable. After Cuba, Nicaragua is next," Salazar added in the post.

Nicaragua's Ortega Says Country Will 'Never Again' Hold Elections, Shutting Out Opposition

The U.S. snatched Venezuela's Nicolás Maduro and his wife out of Venezuela and transported them back to the U.S. earlier this year.

The U.S. later launched a war against the Islamic Republic of Iran in conjunction with Israel.

And then, during a speech in March, President Donald Trump said, "Cuba's next."

Us Military Hits Iran In Push To Degrade Regime's Ability To Target Ships In Strait Of Hormuz

Fox News Digital reached out to Salazar's office on Tuesday to ask more about her comment suggesting that "After Cuba, Nicaragua is next."

The congresswoman said in a Tuesday post on X, "One by one, the dictators of the last era are answering for their crimes. Maduro sleeps in a cell in New York. The Ayatollah is dead. Raúl Castro is under indictment. Daniel Ortega, whose regime killed more than 300 peaceful protesters in 2018, jailed opposition candidates before elections, exiled priests, stripped hundreds of Nicaraguans of their citizenship, and has now abolished elections altogether, still sits in Managua. Communist dictators don't fear losing elections. They fear having them. His day will come."

Secretary of State Marco Rubio said in a Tuesday statement, "Daniel Ortega's declaration that under his family's dictatorship Nicaragua will never again hold elections lays bare their true authoritarian nature. Daniel Ortega and Rosario Murillo have abandoned even the pretense of popular consent.

Cuba Built Covert Network Inside America Through Spies, Activists And Universities, State Dept Alleges

"The Nicaraguan people have the right to choose their own leaders through democratic elections. The United States calls on the international community to join forces to demonstrate to the Murillo-Ortega dictatorship that it cannot expect to maintain business as usual with other nations when it is thwarting the basic tenets of our democratic hemisphere," Rubio asserted.

Original article

title: Woman attacked by brown bear while hiking with friend and 3 dogs near Alaska state park
description: A woman was injured after a brown bear attacked her on an overgrown trail area near Chugach State Park before rescuers safely evacuated her, police said.
fullText:
A woman was attacked by a brown bear Saturday while hiking with a friend and three dogs near a state park in Alaska, authorities said.

The woman sustained non-life-threatening injuries and is expected to recover, the Anchorage Police Department (APD) said Sunday.

The attack happened late afternoon near the Bear Valley residential neighborhood, adjacent to Chugach State Park in south Anchorage, the Alaska Department of Fish and Game (ADF&G) said in a separate statement on Monday.

Terrified Hikers Face Nightmare Survival Scenario After Heart-pounding Grizzly Bear Encounter

Officials said the hikers were on an unofficial, user-made trail that climbs a steep hillside in a zigzag pattern. The area was heavily overgrown with dense vegetation, which created poor visibility and increased the risk of surprising wildlife, ADF&G said.

"The bear came out of the dense alders, made brief contact with one of the hikers, and left the scene," ADF&G said.

One of the victim's dogs was also off leash during the encounter and became separated from the group, according to the authorities. Another group of hikers later found the "uninjured dog and brought it back to safety, ultimately returning it to its home," ADF&G said.

Hidden Danger At Popular Us National Park Forces Trail Closures As Bear Sightings Climb

According to wildlife officials, bears are known to cache and defend food sources this time of year and noted reports that at least one moose had recently been killed nearby.

"High winds and inclement weather conditions at the time of the encounter may have increased the chance that the bear was surprised by the hikers and their dogs, even if they were making noise," ADF&G said.

Bear Attack In Yellowstone National Park Leaves 2 Hikers Injured

"There are many private and public trails throughout Anchorage and the surrounding area that are overgrown at this time of year with tall grass and leafed out shrubs, which can greatly increase the chances of surprise encounters with wildlife."

First responders from multiple agencies, including the Anchorage Fire Department, Alaska Wildlife Troopers and Alaska State Troopers, responded.

Upon arrival, police officers reportedly hiked up the trail to locate the injured adult.

Firefighters then carried the injured woman down the trail before transporting her to a hospital.

During the rescue, APD officers deployed a drone and located a brown bear nearby. Officers continued monitoring the bear throughout the rescue, allowing first responders to safely treat and evacuate the injured hiker.

Original article

title: Seaside parking fines increase for second year
description: Bournemouth, Christchurch and Poole (BCP) Council is allowed to increase parking fines again.
fullText:
Visitors to a seaside resort will again face higher fines for illegal parking this summer.

Bournemouth, Christchurch and Poole (BCP) Council more than doubled its Penalty Charge Notices (PCNs) in a trial last year and has now been allowed to do the same this summer.

Earlier this month the Department for Transport approved the second trial, which will run from 4 until 31 August.

Councillor Richard Herrett said the "tougher fines work" after the council said it issued 7% fewer fines in 2025.

Herrett added: "The 2025 results showed these powers work and there was still capacity across our car parks, including those close to beaches and town centres.

"Crucially, higher fines did not deter people from visiting. The BCP area remained as popular as ever, with returning visitors more than doubling year-on-year and millions of trips made to our area every month."

Parking in the car park closest to Bournemouth seafront - Bath Road South - currently costs £28, with a cost of a fine £35 if paid within 14 days.

The fines will increase to £160, reducing to £80 if settled within 14 days and will be charged along 543 coastal roads from Sandbanks to Southbourne.

Other tariffs include a £100 wheel clamp charge and £280 to release a vehicle from a pound - charges comparable to those seen in London.

The increased PCN fines will be applicable to illegally parked vehicles in both on-street and off-street areas.

Trial shows parking fines should rise, resort says

Cars towed as record beach parking fines issued

Thousands get bank holiday parking fines at resort

Parking charge rises planned at seaside resorts

title: A year in a foxhole: Ukrainian soldier survives beneath the front line
description: Serhii Krynitskyi watched the life seep out of the only man who had shared his living hell.
fullText:
KRAMATORSK, Ukraine, July 22 (Reuters) - Serhii Krynitskyi watched the life seep out of the only man who had shared his living hell.

He and his comrade had survived nearly a year together in a foxhole on the front lines. They'd killed Russian soldiers and scavenged for food among the dead. They'd argued and laughed, amidst the horror.

The Ukrainian soldier aimed his phone camera at his companion's lifeless body, wedged inside the dugout. One blood-stained leg was exposed. An artery sliced open by shrapnel from a bomb dropped by ‌a drone. He bled and bled.

"In the morning, you're joking together," the 42-year-old told the camera numbly, his face smudged and worn. "And by the evening, it's the gates of heaven calling."

The men's isolated position is a consequence of the way drone warfare is revolutionizing the battlefield. ‌The technology has forced both sides to abandon the age-old practice of massing troops, artillery and armoured vehicles in a relatively narrow corridor along the long front line. Such visible targets are easy prey for the cheap unmanned aerial vehicles that both sides have flooded the skies with.

Instead, both armies now have tiny teams, sometimes of just two or three soldiers, scattered in small dugouts hollowed into the ground ​across a battlefield some 30 km (20 miles) wide, known as the "kill zone" because of the constant drone threat. These groups seek to evade detection and infiltrate gaps in enemy defences, often taking huge risks for minimal territorial gains. They are typically stuck for months in their positions because it's so dangerous to rotate troops in and out.

Krynitskyi was deployed near the town of Chasiv Yar in eastern Ukraine from April 20, 2025 to March 31, 2026, he and his brigade said. He filmed a series of video diaries with a cellphone, powered at times by batteries collected from downed Russian drones. He documented, in expletive-laden recordings, the inhumanities and absurdities of life in a cramped redoubt, about 3 by 4 metres, carved beneath the front lines of a grinding war.

He endured terrifying cycles of violence: Every couple of days, Russian soldiers would advance on foot in ⁠small groups of typically two or three. They sometimes came as close as 20 metres before the Ukrainians fired at them.

He said he'd killed 23 enemies at close range during his deployment. He most clearly recalled one of his first: a soldier injured by a grenade. The Russian had faked surrendering before taking aim at him with a rifle.

"Well, I reacted," Krynitskyi said. "He stopped aiming at me."

In a clip from November 24, more than 200 days into his deployment, Krynitskyi trained his camera on the bodies of ⁠two enemy soldiers in the eerie calm after repelling an assault. "Fools," he said. "Crawled up where they don't belong."

Krynitskyi's unit, the 24th Mechanised Brigade, confirmed that he had reported 23 enemy kills. Reuters couldn't verify all aspects of the infantryman's account.

Russia's defence ministry didn't respond to a request for comment about the changing nature of the battlefield and the experiences of soldiers on both sides.

Krynitskyi was not a military man.

He had worked in farming and construction until an injury left him in a month-long coma. After that, he scratched out a living as a rabbit breeder.

In late 2024, he was rounded up by draft officers in his village of Bilohir'ya in western Ukraine. They put him on a bus, brought him to the city ​of ​Rivne and trained him quickly.

He was assigned to the 24th Mechanised Brigade and sent to the front in the east, where Russia's invading army, significantly outnumbering Ukraine's, was attacking the so-called "fortress belt" ​of strategic cities in the Donetsk region.

He and two other privates dug in near Chasiv Yar in April 2025. There, they ‌excavated a foxhole, and Krynitskyi would subsequently spend almost a year living under the ground as enemy forces closed in. The men knew the deeper they dug, the better their chance of survival. Krynitskyi joked he didn't know whether to call the hole a shelter or a grave.

When their refuge was hit by drones, they had to excavate the area anew. One comrade was killed early in the deployment by an attack that left Krynitskyi concussed and temporarily without hearing. Reuters withheld the names of the two dead soldiers at the request of their brigade.

Krynitskyi and his remaining comrade also fought the elements, plugging holes to block frigid drafts and capping lookout shifts at two hours. Any longer was unbearable.

Sometimes, three or four days passed before a re-supply drone could make it through enemy fire. To survive, Krynitskyi picked food and sundries from fallen Russians.

The infantryman, who has two teenage children, started filming the diaries in August 2025, to show people when he got back home what he went through and to remind himself. Reuters verified the date of all the videos using the original file metadata.

In one video, in February this year, he surveyed the cigarettes he'd gathered from dead enemy troops: Camels, Winstons, Chesterfields and others. He obsessed over the packets, "like some nerd collects stamps."

In another video days later, he showcased mud-caked "trophy" rifles he'd ‌seized.

"This is my small arsenal," Krynitskyi said, proudly counting off the 13 firearms, arranged in a row.

DESPERATE ATTEMPT TO SAVE HIS COMRADE

Russia's full-scale invasion of its neighbour in 2022 unleashed staggering ​carnage. While neither side discloses death tolls, the Center for Strategic & International Studies estimates Russian battlefield casualties at 1.4 million, including killed, wounded and missing, and Ukrainian forces have suffered between 525,000 and ​625,000 casualties.

Krynitskyi dug his foxhole on one of the deadliest stretches of the kill zone. On March 11, he heard an explosion outside.

He rushed out into ​the brisk spring air and found his comrade on the ground, hit in the leg by a drone attack. Krynitskyi dragged him back into the hole and frantically tore through his thick cotton trousers to find the wound.

He applied a tourniquet. It was too ‌late to save him.

Upon radioing command, Krynitskyi was ordered to abandon the position as the Russians encroached. That night he ​crawled out of the dugout for the last time to join a few fellow infantrymen ​holed up nearby for almost three more weeks. Then came a perilous march to safety 25 km away made more difficult by his weakened state after months underground.

He left behind the body of his friend - a man he had come to know deeply through countless conversations between enemy assaults.

In May, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy presented Krynitskyi with the Hero of Ukraine medal for personal bravery.

Ukraine's army is full of men like him, who have endured the unimaginable, said Andriy Derun, a military manager at leading Ukrainian charity Come Back Alive, which provides equipment and other support for the armed forces.

"It can completely ​destroy a person," said Derun, who frequently speaks with frontline troops.

Back in Kramatorsk, the eastern city where he returned after his ‌time at the front, Krynitskyi described the mental toll exacted by the marathon stints on the front: "Many guys have gone out of their mind". Nearly two months since he left the front line, the scars inside show on the surface. He fights back tears. His hands shake.

During ​recent visits Krynitskyi made to schools near his home to speak to pupils about his experience, he began to understand better how others may benefit from his sacrifices.

"I realized there, in those schools, that it wasn't in vain that I defended our land and these children – ​so they could study, see the clear sky."

title: ‘A politically impossible situation’: New poll shows Trump voters losing patience with Iran war
description: The share of MAGA voters who think the war is worth the economic cost has fallen to roughly over a third, down from 50 percent in May, the POLITICO Poll found.
fullText:
Half of President Donald Trump's MAGA base thought the Iran war was worth its economic costs in May. Now, just a little more than a third do.

That precipitous, 13-point drop in just two months, according to a new POLITICO Poll, reveals how the president's most die-hard supporters are starting to agree with the majority of Americans who have long opposed the U.S.' involvement in the war.

Additionally, 37 percent of self-identified MAGA Trump voters say the U.S. should continue its involvement only if it doesn't increase costs, up from 29 percent who said the same in May, according to the survey, conducted by independent polling firm Public First. Nearly 1 in 5 MAGA voters believe that the U.S. should end its war in Iran, regardless of the costs.

That flagging support among the president's most loyal supporters is the clearest sign that Americans' patience is wearing thin as the economy continues to dominate as voters' top concern heading into a critical midterm cycle.

But Trump on Monday gave little indication that he was looking for a quick end to the war. The U.S. military has pounded Iran for the last two weeks, and speaking to reporters on Tuesday the president claimed that Iranian officials "want to desperately meet" but that "until they are ready to meet in a meaningful way we have no interest in even talking."

The White House is "at a maximum level of frustration right now, and I was told by very senior people that the president is now fully aware that the only way to the victory that he wants is completely politically impossible. The American people just will not support the kind of escalation it would take at this point," said one person close to the White House, granted anonymity to discuss private conversations. "We've got more dead Americans and a politically impossible situation."

This souring of the president's base comes amid renewed hostilities between the U.S. and Iran, which have been conducting tit-for-tat strikes after the president earlier this month declared a tentative peace agreement between the two countries "over." Two U.S. servicemembers were killed in Jordan this weekend and gas prices have climbed above $4 per gallon, underscoring the war's impact at home.

The mood among White House staff and their outside allies is grim as the president grapples with finding a path out of an unpopular and expensive war three months from a midterm election expected to turn on cost of living issues.

The president was expected to spend the year on a victory tour on affordability issues, touting his popular tax cuts, Trump accounts for newborns and lower drug prices. Instead, a domestic travel schedule has been pocked by visits to Dover Air Force Base to oversee the dignified transfer of the remains of more than a dozen dead soldiers, and he has during his campaign-style rallies been forced to time and again argue to the American people that the price of war — most salient to higher prices at the pump — was worth ending Iran's nuclear program.

White House aides contend that price bumps are temporary and will quickly return to pre-war levels either when the war ends or when the U.S. has degraded Iran's military capabilities to the point that it can no longer stem the flow of oil tankers through the Strait of Hormuz.

"What matters most to the American people is having a Commander in Chief who takes bold action to keep them safe, which is exactly what President Trump is doing," said White House spokesperson Olivia Wales. "The President does not make national security decisions based on fluid opinion polls, but on the best interests of the American people."

Still, the latest polling shows that non-MAGA Trump voters are even more down on the war, with only 21 percent believing it is worth higher costs, while 28 percent say that the U.S. shouldn't continue the war regardless of costs.

And the results show the president's voters are broadly comfortable blaming the war for higher prices. When asked why gas prices have increased since the president took office, the majority of respondents blamed the conflict, including 57 percent of MAGA voters and 63 percent of non-MAGA Trump voters.

No other cause — from seasonal demand to environmental regulations limiting drilling — crested 25 percent for the core MAGA group. Those who voted for former Vice President Kamala Harris, on the other hand, also blamed the president's poor handling of the economy and his tariffs, at 55 percent and 52 percent, respectively, for the rise in costs.

Trump's 2024 supporters also are split on whether gas prices would have gone up more, less or the same under a Democratic president compared with now — another indication that Americans are more willing to assign blame to Trump for the state of the economy. Equal shares of Trump voters say that prices would've gone up more under a Democrat (34 percent) and that prices would have gone up the same (35 percent). A notable 20 percent believe prices have gone up more under Trump.

Prominent Republicans, including strategists in key races, have spent the last several months warning about the impact of backlash to the Iran war on their chances of holding the House and the Senate, with frustrations around the war playing out in competitive races across the country.

"This shows that as unpopular as the first round of war was from February to early summer, this second round is even more ridiculous," said Curt Mills, executive director of the American Conservative magazine. "The American people quite simply do not get it, do not understand why we are in this war, and, yes, President Trump is king of the Republican Party. Yes, the president can do a lot and has a lot of latitude with his own supporters, but kings are not invulnerable from political reality."

But Trump on Tuesday said he wasn't concerned.

"They won't have any impact on me. I'm just here to do the right thing. I don't view it as, gee, we're having an election in a period of time. You know, everyone says, oh, I'm doing something before — no, the election — I can't think about that having to do with this," Trump said.

title: Top US general says Ukraine's F-16s recently shot down their first Russian fighter jet, showing how far its air defense has come
description: Gen. Dan Caine said Ukraine scored its "first air-to-air kill where a Ukrainian F-16 shot down a Russian fighter," a mark of its progress.
fullText:
A top US general said a Ukrainian F-16 scored the country's first air-to-air kill.

He said it showed how far Ukraine's layered defenses have developed.

The general did not specify what kind of Russian fighter jet was shot down in the engagement.

Ukraine's F-16 fighter jets scored their first air-to-air kill by shooting down a Russian fighter jet, a top US general said, calling the engagement a sign of Ukraine's improving air defenses.

US Air Force Gen. Dan Caine, the chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said during a congressional hearing on Tuesday that "recently we had the first air-to-air kill where a Ukrainian F-16 shot down a Russian fighter."

He described it as evidence of Ukraine's growing air-defense capability.

"I think their ability to scale their layered defense has come a long way over the last couple years with the help and assistance of many," he said at a Senate Appropriations Committee hearing.

An effective layered defense employs different sensors and weapons, positioned at varying distances and altitudes, to counter varied threats. It requires not only having enough sensors and weapons fielded, but also the ability to connect them, share information, and assign the right weapon to each threat.

Ukraine has expanded those defenses during Russia's full-scale invasion, combining domestically developed technology and Western weapons with Soviet-era systems already in service.

Caine did not provide details about the engagement, including where it occurred or what type of Russian aircraft was shot down. Some observers have speculated that he may have been referring to a July 8 Ukrainian Air Force claim that its forces downed a Russian Su-35. That, however, remains unclear.

The general cited the growth of Ukraine's defense industry and said he wanted to "highlight the incredible work that the Ukraine industrial base is doing as they scale their own capabilities. It ranges from their surface-to-air capabilities to their air-to-air capabilities." He was speaking in response to a question about what impact a potential US halting of air defense funding to Ukraine for three years would have.

Though it has significantly improved its defenses since the start of the war, including through domestic developments such as interceptor drones, Ukraine continues to ask for any and all air defense support it can get as Russian drone and missile bombardments worsen and continue to threaten both military and civilian infrastructure.

Ukraine received its first batch of F-16s in summer 2024 after a long debate among Western militaries about whether the provision of these fighter jets could be perceived as unnecessarily escalatory and how quickly Ukraine could integrate them and build the training, maintenance, logistics, and operational systems needed to use them effectively.

Ukraine has managed to integrate these assets into its air force, using them to intercept Russian drones and missiles, conduct strikes on ground targets, and now engage hostile crewed aircraft.

And Western partner nations are now studying how Ukraine combines cheap sensors, interceptor drones, dispersed operations, and older flying techniques to strengthen its air defenses and operations.

Partner nations are providing other advanced Western jets to Ukraine, including Swedish Gripens and French Mirages, framing it as both the result of Ukraine's competency and allied desire to integrate Ukraine into the future of Western defense, not just to have it survive this war.

If you enjoyed this story, be sure to follow Business Insider on Yahoo.

title: Fetterman calls out Trump over 2020 election claims: 'You're the president'
description: U.S. Sen. John Fetterman criticized President Donald Trump after Trump's comments about alleged election interference during a primetime address.
fullText:
Following President Donald Trump's remarks during a primetime address to the nation accusing China of election interference, U.S. Sen. John Fetterman, D-Pennsylvania, lambasted the president for his focus on unsubstantiated claims of election fraud.

Here's what Fetterman said as he rebuked Trump's recent election fraud claims.

Fetterman chided Trump for his continued insistence that extensive voter fraud swung the 2020 presidential election.

"I don't know why the president is dragging up all of this stuff," Fetterman said on CNN's July 19 "State of the Union." "You're the president. Do something positive, and you are talking about 2020."

Fetterman's criticism comes after Trump flip-flopped on criticisms toward China, initially alleging that the country had interfered in the 2018 midterms and the 2020 presidential election, but later saying he believed China had changed.

Fetterman further called out the president's fixation on 2020 election fraud during his appearance on "State of the Union."

"That's been settled over six years ago," Fetterman said. "It's deeply unhelpful, and that distracts important things, especially like the Iranian war right now."

During the discussion, Fetterman emphasized that Pennsylvania, where Fetterman was serving as lieutenant governor in 2020, elected former President Joe Biden and that the election was "absolutely secure, fair and accurate."

"That was the voice of Pennsylvania, and they picked Joe Biden in 2020," Fetterman said. "That's a fact."

Fetterman also said if he saw evidence that more than 250,000 migrants were registered to vote, "that would make me reconsider some of these things."

"But there's no evidence, and I don't expect that we will see any of that," Fetterman said. "And I also would remind people, in Pennsylvania, out of 67 counties, (the) vast, vast, vast majority of them are deep red counties, and they have never encountered any kinds of fraud."

For nearly six years, Trump has repeatedly claimed election fraud took place during the 2020 election when Biden won despite the Department of Justice finding no evidence of widespread voter fraud.

Most recently, Trump called the election system "broken and so vulnerable that no one can possibly defend it" during a primetime address to the nation on July 16. Referencing declassified intelligence documents, he also alleged that China had attempted to influence the results of the 2018 midterm elections and had tried to stop him from getting elected in 2020, when, he said, the country compromised voter data and stole 220 million voter files from states across the U.S. The effort, Trump claimed, was carried out by Beijing to favor Biden in "the largest compromise of election data in history."

"The cover-up of this colossal security breach is even more disturbing in light of the additional information showing that China engaged in other election-related activities to undermine my first administration and our 2020 campaign," Trump said. "They did not want, they just didn't want it, (and) they fought like hell not to have it, Donald Trump to win, and for good reason."

But just days later, Trump downplayed China's alleged actions, saying he believed the country has changed since Biden's election and that the U.S. also targets China, though he wasn't specific about what U.S. actions he was referencing.

"I think China's maybe a little bit different today than it was then," Trump said. "But look, they do things. And we do things to them. I'll be honest. We do things to them, too. It's not a one-way street. But we'll be talking to them."

Finch Walker is the Pittsburgh Connect Reporter for the USA TODAY Network. Contact Walker at FWalker@usatodayco.com. Instagram: @finchwalker_. X: @_finchwalker.

This article originally appeared on USA TODAY: Fetterman calls out Trump over 2020 election claims: 'You're the president'

title: Ukraine recaptured 700 sq km of territory this year, outgoing top general says
description: July 22 () - Ukraine has recaptured 700 sq km (270 sq miles) of its territory this year, ‌outgoing Commander in Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi said ‌on Wednesday, confirming he was leaving the top military j...
fullText:
July 22 (Reuters) - Ukraine has recaptured 700 sq km (270 sq miles) of its territory this year, ‌outgoing Commander in Chief Oleksandr Syrskyi said ‌on Wednesday, confirming he was leaving the top military job.

"I am ​handing over to my successor an army that is not only holding the defence but is also on the offensive," Syrskyi said in a statement on Telegram.

President ‌Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on ⁠Tuesday that a young major general, Mykhailo Drapatyi, would replace Syrskyi after days of ⁠street protests.

The move followed the ousting in a wider government reshuffle last week of tech-savvy reformist defence minister ​Mykhailo Fedorov, ​which brought out into ​the open the deep ‌divisions in the Ukrainian defence leadership.

Syrskyi has held the commander in chief position since early 2024, after playing key roles in Kyiv's defence in the early days of the war and in a lightning counteroffensive ‌to retake swathes of the ​northeastern Kharkiv region in 2022.

But he ​has faced harsh ​criticism for a rigid command style which ‌some service members have said ​resulted in ​unjustifiably high troop losses.

In his statement he highlighted his track record, including stopping a Russian offensive ​in the Kharkiv ‌region, and an operation in Russia's Kursk region ​in 2024.

title: Cardinals roster: Training Camp TE Outlook
description: What is behind Pro Bowler Trey McBride?
fullText:
The Arizona Cardinals have a lot of work to do in this year’s training camp. Very few NFL clubs are installing a new offense this year, but the Cardinals are. So, a lot of time and effort will be geared toward getting the scheme installed where everyone is on the same page.

This is one thing that training camp is for. Plus, new veterans and rookies that haven’t been to the desert yet and need to become acclimated to the area, its climate, and the task at hand.

Now that training camp is on us, let’s look at the tight end room.

This year’s group can’t just be Trey McBride and nothing else. There should be a lot of improvement with this group even though some members became injured, which is not their fault. But other players must step up and be productive and not just a jersey nameplate.

There are seven tight ends in camp. New this year are Teagan Quitoriano from the Atlanta Falcons, former San Francisco 49er Kenny Yeboah, and undrafted rookie Jameson Geers.

Starting tight end: Trey McBride

TE4: Kenny Yeboah, Teagan Quitoriano, Rivaldo Fairweather, Jameson Geers

McBride (6’-4”, 246 pounds) is a beast. The entire league knows what his skill set is. This new offense is predicated on the short passing game and running the ball. That falls right into McBride’s wheelhouse. He has gone from 104 targets to 111 and 169 last season.

Marvin Harrison, Sr. has the record for the most targets in a single season with 205. If Vegas is putting a line on McBride breaking that plateau, take the over. 126 catches last year; take that a notch up to over 150 on his way to a First Team All-Pro nod. Truly, this Rams/49ers offense elevates the tight end position. Who is the highest-paid tight end? Hint: Plays for the Niners.

Now comes the tricky part. This new offense needs a minimum of three good tight ends. That is mandatory. Tip Reiman (6’-5”, 271 pounds) hasn’t been used to catching the ball in college, but he is actually a very good receiver. In past offenses, he wasn’t thrown the ball, but in this new offense he can expect to gain 500-600 yards. And since he is just shy of being an offensive tackle as far as body size, he is an exceptional blocker.

So, who comes next? Who takes the fourth and final roster spot for tight ends? There are five candidates, and training camp will reveal which two.

This group can shine if the correct quarterback is found early enough.

McBride is pretty durable, so that shouldn’t become an issue. If his backup and TE3 become injured, this offense will spit and sputter.

Reiman had a horrible injury last year. Elijah Higgins (6’-3”, 245 pounds) is really a good play on his own. He could very well be paired with McBride with Reiman the third guy. But Higgins will need more than the 37 targets he received last year. Throw him the ball. He has good hands, but the knock on him is pretty huge. He doesn’t get separation from his linebacker or safety.

The worst-case scenario this year is what happened last season: it was McBride, and basically nothing else with this group. This offense will live and die with the tight end room.

This group will become the shining star of this new offense. Cardinals’ fans will soon know every tight end on the roster.

A steady flow of short passes will become the tight end’s resume. This new offense doesn’t throw deep much, but the short passing game along with a steady run game will move the sticks into the Red Zone. Yards for stats won’t be generated in large chunks but in steady increments, which fits in the tight end wheelhouse tremendously.

Higgins can be another huge receiving target in this offense. He is not huge like Reiman, but he has a large catch radius and can be a mismatch with a safety. His route running is sparse, and yes, he lacks top-end speed, but he knows how to get to the high point of the throw and has decent YAC numbers.

Nobody is asking Higgins to be McBride 2.0, but he will surprise a lot of folks in this offense with Reiman a tremendous effort in blocking. Higgins paired with McBride will provide an explosiveness that can stretch the defense down the seam. He is just waiting to be unleashed.

As far as the others, Kenny Yeboah (6’-4”, 250 pounds) is the only one with NFL experience, having played for the New York Jets for four years. He wasn’t thrown the ball much either.

The roster will rely heavily on McBride and Higgins on the receiving end, with Reiman as an in-line and downfield blocker. The only questions will be who will be the last player who makes the final roster, and how many will they keep on the practice squad.

And how will they all be used in the game plan?

title: FIFA World Cup breaks 32-year Fox and Telemundo record
description: Soccer is perhaps the most popular sport in the world, even if it is not the biggest in the United States of America. The NFL, NBA, etc, are generally more popular than ‘football’, but the 2026 World Cup meant a lot more eyes were on soccer across America this summer.
fullText:
Soccer is perhaps the most popular sport in the world, even if it is not the biggest in the United States of America.

The NFL, NBA, etc, are generally more popular than ‘football’, but the 2026 World Cup meant a lot more eyes were on soccer across America this summer.

In turn, it is perhaps not surprising to see the tournament breaking viewership records on Fox and Telemundo.

The USMNT were knocked out of the 2026 World Cup in the Round of 16 after losing 4-1 to Belgium.

Despite the early exit, it seems like many were still heavily invested in the competition all the way up to the final.

This was made clear after Fox’s Michael Mulvihill tweeted that the final between Spain and Argentina set viewership records on the network.

He stated: “The World Cup Final averaged 66.4 million viewers in the U.S. on FOX and Telemundo combined. 42.5 million on FOX. 23.9 million on Telemundo.

“Other than Super Bowls, this is the largest sports audience since the 1994 Winter Olympics.”

Soccer in America still has some way to go if it is to reach the heights of the Super Bowl, however.

The 2026 showpiece is reported to have had over 125 million viewers on Fox earlier this year, around double that of the World Cup.

Of course, that may have been different had the USMNT reached the final, but that was always going to be an unlikely scenario.

Still, it may lead to more people inside the USA starting to take soccer a bit more seriously, given there is clearly a decent audience to capitalise on.

Soccer fans react as MLS tickets on sale for a fraction of FIFA World Cup tickets

Argentina to take legal action over ‘misinformation campaign’ aimed at Lionel Messi and other stars

The White House react to Ferran Torres wearing ‘Make Spain Great Again’ cap at World Cup celebrations

title: Are the Orioles capable of playing winning baseball?
description: The seven-game win streak showed that the Orioles can win games, but the team revealed its flaws on Monday in Boston.
fullText:
The Orioles had won seven straight before traveling to Boston to face the only team in the league touting a better win streak. The Birds were set to play in their first series in over a year that carried a heightened sense of meaning. Playing the Red Sox—a division opponent in possession of a playoff spot—marked an opportunity for the club to measure itself against a contender.

How did the Orioles respond? They committed four errors and lost a one-run game.

It’s always tempting to over analyze after a bad loss, and yesterday’s rain out only helps the cause. We all know that every outcome counts as one win or loss in the standings, but that game was rich with symbolism. Baltimore pulled Shane Baz (6 IP, 2 ER) after 77 pitches, and both relievers allowed a run. The team left the tying and go-ahead runs in scoring position. Craig Albernaz pinch hit for Christian Encarnacion-Strand despite his strong offensive night, and Sam Huff finished 0-for-3 with three strikeouts for the second consecutive game.

At the same time, Tyler O’Neill squared up a ball that could have kept the Birds alive. The Orioles scored in four different innings despite missing Adley Rutschman and Samuel Basallo. They got another solid start from the rotation, and the Red Sox are obviously a good team too. Winning teams lose all the time, and you shouldn’t make a mountain out of a one-run molehill. But those four errors… Those are the type of plays that make you wonder if this team is capable of playing winning baseball.

So what exactly is winning baseball? Did the Orioles play winning baseball when they won seven consecutive games? It’s kind of like that thing the Supreme Court spent some time discussing in the 1960s. You know it when you see it.

The Orioles have been a bad defensive team all season, but they made some winning plays over that recent stretch. O’Neill extended the game with an impressive catch and throw in Houston. The outfielders made several running grabs, and Brandon Young’s kick save was named MLB’s play of the week.

This team is capable of making the necessary plays, but defense will never be considered a true strength. One or two defensive miscues may be inevitable, but those four errors felt like something more. It felt like the Orioles crumbled in their first big game of the season.

The American League is bad enough that a flawed team is going to make the playoffs. The Orioles could be that team. The question is whether a team like that can do any damage once they get there. Flawed teams can go on a run if they—and stop me if you’re over it by now—play winning baseball.

Jim Palmer has spent enough time explaining the value of hitting the cutoff man, but those things make a difference. Gunnar Henderson is capable of making the “wow” type of plays, but can he remain engaged over nine innings? Baltimore’s shortstop made his 12th error of the season on Monday and could have been credited with another when he failed to catch an 82 MPH line drive in third inning.

The rotation has provided some real reason for hope, and the bullpen has been better than it should be on paper. At a minimum, it feels like the Orioles could hang around with any AL team in a three-game series. Unfortunately, Baltimore is 8-15 in one-run games. Their eight wins are the lowest number of one-run victories in all of baseball.

The Orioles can’t go back and win any of those 15 games, but they can learn from the outcome. The front office, if they plan on buying, must determine what this team really needs. The club probably doesn’t want to pay the premium for Tarik Skubal or Joe Ryan. If acquiring a true ace is off the table, the team doesn’t need another mid-rotation starter.

The bullpen is a different story. The Birds need to flip the script in one-run games, and a shutdown reliever or two would go a long way. Ryan Helsley will be out until at least September, and it’s far too early to expect anything from Félix Bautista.

Will the team address the defense? A steady third baseman that hits righties would work wonders for this team, but nobody is replacing Henderson at short. It feels like the Orioles have been “learning how to win” since 2022, but the sentiment still rings true. The team had an extra day to flush Monday’s loss and can take a big step in the right direction by playing 18 innings of winning baseball today.

title: Previewing 2nd half of Arizona football’s 2026 schedule
description: Arizona football is right around the corner and with training camp soon to commence, we are taking an early look at the opponents the Wildcats will be facing this season. Compared to last season, the competition level has increased so trying to get to the Big 12 championship will be as hard as it can […]
fullText:
Arizona football is right around the corner and with training camp soon to commence, we are taking an early look at the opponents the Wildcats will be facing this season. Compared to last season, the competition level has increased so trying to get to the Big 12 championship will be as hard as it can be.

Noah Fifita is set to return for one last ride in Tucson and Brent Brennan is hoping to build off of the success of last season. Arizona returns almost all of its coaching staff so continuity in the program is as strong as it has ever been.

On Monday we took a look at the first half of opponents the Wildcats will face this fall. Here is a look at the opponents Arizona will face in the second half of the 2026 season, which starts after a Week 7 bye:

Opponent’s 2025 record: 8-4, 5-4 Big 12

Last meeting: 2024 (Iowa State 39, Arizona 14)

Arizona holds the all-time series lead over the Cyclones with a 4-2-1 record. It’s new all around for Iowa State this season after former head coach Matt Campbell left for the job at Penn State. Starting quarterback Rocco Becht, along with many other players, followed Campbell to Happy Valley.

The Cyclones brought in Jimmy Rogers to lead the program and he will try to piece together a season starting from scratch. Connor Moberly saw little to no action last season but does return for Iowa State. Zane Flores transferred in from Oklahoma State and comes in with eight starts under his belt.

For its rushing attack, Iowa State brought in three transfer running backs. Tulane transfer Arnold Banes III rushed for 217 yards on 51 carries, Oregon State transfer Salahadin Allah ran for 71 yards on 19 carries, and Bowling Green transfer Cameron Pettaway was the MAC Freshman of the year with 365 yards on 72 carries.

At receiver, the Cyclones have a mix of young talent and veteran experience. The room is highlighted by Tarleton State transfer Cody Jackson who had 53 receptions for 799 yards and six touchdowns, and Tulane transfer Omari Hayes who had 39 receptions for 515 yards and a touchdown.

Some names to look for on defense are Washington State transfer Duhron Goodman, Arkansas transfer cornerback Jaheim Singletary, and a trio of Washington State defensive tackle transfers in Isaac Terrell, Max Baloun, and Bryson Lamb.

Opponent’s 2025 record: 12-2, 8-1 Big 12

Last meeting: 2024 (Texas Tech 28, Arizona 22)

Arizona is set to take on the reigning Big 12 champs and if all goes right for both schools, this should be a matchup with major postseason implications. The Red Raiders have dominated the all-time series with a 27-5-2 record.

Will Hammond will likely be the starting quarterback and saw a good amount of action last season. He had 680 passing yards and threw seven touchdowns while rushing for 299 yards and five touchdowns.

Cameron Dickey returns as the leading rusher for Texas Tech with 1,124 rushing yards and 14 touchdowns. J’Koby Williams follows behind him with 868 rushing yards and six touchdowns.

Texas Tech’s leading returner in receiving yards is Coy Eakin who had 637 yards and caught six touchdowns. Pittsburgh transfer Kenny Johnson had 109 receptions and 1,354 yards in his two seasons with the Panthers.

Ben Roberts comes back to Lubbock as the leading returner in tackles with 90 and he also had four tackles for loss along with four interceptions. As a whole, the Red Raiders defense forced 17 interceptions and 35 fumbles.

Texas Tech averaged 39.43 points per game and had a 49.55% third down conversion rate. It’s easy to see why it dominated its way to the Big 12 championship and why it is the favorite to be repeat champions.

Kickoff/TV info: 8:15 p.m. MT, ESPN

Opponent’s 2025 record: 9-4, 5-4 Big 12

Last meeting: 2024 (TCU 49, Arizona 28)

The Wildcats will be looking to tie the all-time series against the Horned Frogs in a 2-1 series record. TCU has won the last two games with the most recent being in Brennan’s inaugural season.

Last season’s starting quarterback Josh Hoover transferred to Indiana so TCU went into the portal and got Harvard transfer Jaden Craig. He had school records for single season passing completions (208), touchdown passes (25), and passing yards (2,869).

TCU’s leading rusher returns in Jeremy Payne who had 623 rushing yards and five touchdowns. Jordan Dwyer is the leading returner in receiving yards with 730 yards and seven touchdowns.

Safety Jamel Johnson returns as the leading tackler with 96 and he also had three tackles for loss and five interceptions. The Horned Frogs forced 15 interceptions and fumbles each.

TCU averaged 30.69 points per game and had a 47.4% third down conversion rate.

Opponent’s 2025 record: 11-2, 7-2 Big 12

Last meeting: 2024 (Arizona 23, Utah 10)

While Arizona may have taken the last two games against Utah, it is the Utes who own the all-time record standing at 26-21-2. In the last meeting, the Wildcats went to Salt Lake City and left with an early season upset road win.

Former Utah head coach Kyle Whittingham left for the Michigan job and defensive coordinator Morgan Scalley was promoted to be the head man of the program. He was able to retain starting quarterback Devon Dampier to lead the offense.

Dampier had 2,490 passing yards and 24 touchdowns. He was also the second leading rusher with 835 yards and had ten touchdowns. Wayshawn Parker led the Utes in rushing yards with 981 and ran for six touchdowns.

Larry Simmons had 280 receiving yards and six touchdowns. He will be leading the receiver room more than likely but the Utes also brought in San Jose State receiver Kyri Shoels who had 59 catches for 768 yards and two touchdowns.

The defense will be led by Johnathan Hall who led the team in tackles with 67 and had six tackles for loss. Jackson Bennee will be leading the secondary and last season he had 64 tackles with four interceptions.

Utah averaged 41.31 points per game and had a 52.57% third down conversion rate. The defense forced 15 interceptions and eight fumbles. The transition from the Pac-12 to the Big 12 has not stopped the Utes from producing successful seasons and they will look to keep that trend going.

Opponent’s 2025 record: 6-6, 5-4 Big 12

Last meeting: 2025 (Arizona 23, Kansas State 17)

The battle of the Wildcats has belonged in favor of the ones from Tucson. Arizona leads the all-time series with a 6-2-1 record. This season, UA will travel to Manhattan for the second time in three years.

Kansas State has a new head coach in Collin Klein, who used to play for the Wildcats. Avery Johnson returns as starting quarterback and he had 2,154 passing yards with 16 touchdowns. He also had 460 rushing yards and six touchdowns.

Joe Jackson is the leading returner in rushing yards with 842 yards and ran for eight touchdowns. Jaron Tibbs will more than likely be Johnson’s leading target, as he had 552 receiving yards and two touchdowns last season. Garrett Oakley had the most receiving touchdowns with six.

Zashon RIch had 51 tackles last season which makes him the leading returner in tackles and he also had 3.5 tackles for loss. The Wildcats forced 11 interceptions and 14 fumbles last season on defense.

Averaging 28.27 points per game, and a 37.67% third down conversion rate, Kansas State will be looking to improve on those numbers this season.

Opponent’s 2025 record: 8-5, 6-3 Big 12

Last meeting: 2025 (Arizona 23, ASU 7)

The game of all games within the state of Arizona, the Territorial Cup will be celebrating its 100th anniversary in Tucson. Arizona leads the all-time rivalry series 52–47-1 and is coming off a win in Tempe last season.

Sam Leavitt, the Sun Devils’ starting quarterback last season, transferred to LSU so they will be looking for a new offensive leader. It will more than likely come down to either Kentucky transfer Cutter Boley, or UCF transfer Mikey Keene.

ASU also lost its leading rushing to Raleek Brown to Texas. Jason Brown Jr. saw some playing time last season and had 151 yards and a touchdown.

Whoever ends up being the Sun Devils’ quarterback will have a big time target to throw to in Colorado transfer Omarion Miller. Last season, Miller had 45 receptions for 808 yards and eight touchdowns.

Safety Adrian Wilson comes back to Tempe as the leader in tackles with 57. He also had an interception, forced fumble, and fumble recovery. ASU forced eight interceptions and fumbles each.

ASU averaged 25.77 points per game, and had a 33.3% third down conversion rate. Those numbers are going to have to improve if the Sun Devils want to be in the conversation for the Big 12 championship.

Last year’s matchup had major postseason implications, and if all goes right for both Arizona schools, then this might be another matchup with postseason destinations on the line.

title: Cowboys Oxnard training camp roundtable discussion
description: Here is our latest roundtable with a panel of BTB writers giving us their answers to this week’s questions
fullText:
Every so often, we gather to discuss the latest news about the Dallas Cowboys and seek our writer’s perspective on each headline. Welcome back to the roundtable. This week we have Jess Haynie, Sean Martin, Brian Martin, and for his first Roundtable appearance we have Jorge Guajardo.

Mike: Jaishawn Barham is the rookie I’m most excited to watch because linebacker was one of Dallas’ biggest weaknesses last season and desperately needed an upgrade. Barham is has the size to handle traffic between the tackles, but also the athleticism to chase plays outside and create pressure when sent after the quarterback. Training camp will show how quickly he diagnoses offenses and earns the coaches’ trust, but the opportunity is there for him.

Jess: If we’re all being honest, the answer is Caleb Downs. So I’ll go with my next guy, Jaishawn Barham. We’re desperate for stability at inside linebacker, let alone a playmaker. Even if DeMarvion Overshown explodes this year, and his knee doesn’t, we still need one more guy to help make this defense competitive again. Comparisons between Barham and the Eagles’ Zack Baun, given the Christian Parker connection, are also tantalizing.

Jorge: The answer is Caleb Downs, but I really want to see Michael Trigg. TE is one of the deepest position groups on this team, and if Brevyn Spann-Ford makes a leap, the path to the 53-man roster gets really tough for everybody behind Spann-Ford, setting up what could be a desperate attempt to make the roster, which, I think, always leads to entertaining and even good football. Trigg is a physical specimen, so seeing him fight and claw for a roster spot could be epic, and could light a fire under the other TEs on the roster as well.

Brian: As much as I’m looking forward to seeing what Caleb Downs can do in the ever important nickel/safety role in Christian Parker’s scheme, I’m going to go with the Cowboys other first-round pick, Malachi Lawrence. Dallas’ pass rush was pretty much nonexistent a season ago, but will hopefully be much improved this year with the new, versatile 3-4 scheme and the additions of Rashan Gary and Lawrence. The rookie could play a big role as a pass rush specialist or maybe even earn a starting role.

Sean: Kind of an “old man sitting on the porch” answer from me, which believe me doesn’t feel too great on a personal level, but I’ve covered enough Cowboys camps at this point to know that it is a yearly tradition like no other to absolutely lose our minds over a fringe wide receiver that makes some intriguing plays. It is the one position where a huge swarm of fans always have a “pet cat” they swear has to be a roster lock, very few ever are, but the fun and enthusiasm still goes on year after year.

For these reasons I’m going with seventh-round pick Anthony Smith, who has the speed to make plenty of social media highlights potentially getting behind a Christian Parker secondary that won’t take too well to giving up big plays, making the back and forth that much more intense. Let’s see what Anthony Smith can do.

Mike: Brevyn Spann-Ford is my underrated player to watch in Oxnard. He was already trending upward last season despite sitting third on the tight end depth chart, and he has a genuine opportunity to overtake Luke Schoonmaker for the number two role. Spann-Ford’s size and catch radius make him an intriguing target.

Jess: Training camp seems to always be when CB Caelen Carson shines. The problem is that we haven’t seen it carry into the regular season, either due to injuries or just general inexperience. Now in his third season, and playing under a DB guru in Parker, I’m intrigued to see if many have been too quick to dismiss Carson as a contender this summer.

Jorge: For the same reasons Jess believes in Caelen Carson, I’m going to go with Shavon Revel. He has all the tools to be a solid contributor at the NFL level. He finally has a full, injury-free offseason to work on his craft and be mentored by Christian Parker, which I think will do wonders for him.

Brian: It’s going to be difficult for certain players to shine in training camp due to the lack of physicality (tackling/hitting) in practices, so I’m going to have to go with a skill player here. I think second-year WR Traeshon Holdon could pick up where he left off last year in training camp where he turned some heads. He has another year in Brian Schottenheimer system and has been working with Dak Prescott. I think we’ll be hearing quite a bit from No. 80.

Sean: I’m going to go with Shemar James, just because there is so much uncertainty around what he can bring to the defense, but a lot of potential as well. There’s been a laundry list of things to call a wash from last year’s defense and hope for better under Parker, and the overall communication on the backend of the defense under Eberflus was high on that list. James got “lost in the sauce” in this overall bad defense probably one too many times to have a lot of fans excited about him in Oxnard, but don’t forget how much legitimate smoke there was about the Cowboys drafting a traditional linebacker in the first round. That is still a roster need that’s been a little under-addressed, but if James starts turning the corner, it would go a long way.

Mike: Shavon Revel against George Pickens. Pickens is a physical receiver who can challenge a corner at every level, making him the perfect daily test for measuring how far Revel has developed. Revel is also another year removed from his ACL injury, so he should look more confidence. There will inevitably be difficult reps against a receiver of Pickens’ quality, but that is exactly how Revel can accelerate his development. If he holds his own consistently, it would be an encouraging sign that he is ready to take a major step in the Cowboys’ secondary.

Jess: The battle that has the most chance to decide wins or losses is at backup QB. Since 2020, Dak Prescott has missed five or more games every other season. He played a full 17 last year, so that’s daunting if the pattern holds. Sam Howell is now here to give Joe Milton real competition for the job. During some of Prescott’s absences, Cooper Rush reminded us how having a QB2 who can play competently can keep your season afloat. We need to see more of Howell and Milton to build our confidence that they can at least do that.

Jorge: It’s the running back battle behind Javonte Williams for me. Each back, whether it’s Jaydon Blue, Phil Mafah, or Malik Davis, brings a different skill set to the table. The Dallas rushing attack was solid last year, and if one of the backs emerges behind Williams this offense could take another leap forward and make them even more lethal than last year’s unit.

Brian: I’m going to cheat and go with two position battles we should all be watching closely, linebacker and left tackle. From a starting standpoint and a backup one, both positions remain unsettled right now heading into training camp. Both positions also carry some significant injury concerns for projected starters like Tyler Guyton and DeMarvion Overshown. Staying healthy and showing consistency at both positions will be key for the Cowboys success this year.

Sean: I really like Jorge’s answer about the running backs, but I’m going to go with cornerback myself. We know Christian Parker will be working very closely and hands-on with this position group, so through the cornerback battle we’ll also learn about the new defensive coordinator. This is also a position group where the Cowboys had almost no choice but to keep some retreads from the Eberflus defense, although several were here even before that, and this could be the camp where we find out if players like DaRon Bland are still dependable, just how good the Cobie Durant acquisition was, and if Caelen Carson and/or Shavon Revel can assert themselves.

Mike: The one takeaway from training camp this year would be clear evidence that the defense has genuinely improved, not just changed names. Dallas needs to look far more connected than it did last season, especially at linebacker and in the secondary. If the communication is cleaner and making life difficult for Dak Prescott, that would be the strongest sign that this team has actually raised its floor heading into the season.

Jess: I’ll take the low-hanging fruit on this one: stay healthy! I’m so tired of key players starting the year on IR, or spending most of the year nursing injuries that limit their effectiveness. I want to see everything Overshown has to offer. I want Bland back to full form. I want Ferguson taking full advantage of an explosive offense. I know this is part of the game and we’re not going to get out of August unscathed, but it just feels like lately we’ve had a pretty bad run with injuries to key players.

Jorge: Establish standards early and often on the defensive side. It was gut-wrenching at times to watch the 2025 Dallas Cowboys on defense. Missed gap assignments, blown coverages, and just straight-up bad football. Matt Eberflus paid for it with his job, but many of the guys who were on that team are still here, and new DC Christian Parker has to flush the bad habits and approaches that partly led to that disaster. If he can set the foundation of standards and non-negotiables, a culture of success will follow.

Brian: I’m going to have to go with leadership/accountability. We’ve already heard some good things about certain players stepping into leadership roles in OTAs and hopefully that will continue in training camp/preseason all the way through the season. With better leadership will hopefully mean more accountability, specifically cutting down the penalties. Dallas is continually one of the most penalized teams in the league, but hopefully no more.

Sean: I’m going to have my ear very close to the ground for the details of how this offense will actually be better in 2026. It’s been a long offseason of repeating the same talking points about continuity on the depth chart, and continuity in coaching with Brian Schottenheimer and Klayton Adams. That much continuity should be tangible on the field when it comes to Dak Prescott getting the offense in the right look always, protections being picked up, receivers playing in different spots (which has been teased already), and overall just a level of command that should feel different all summer. After all, we all sort of know it will be the offense that can be this team’s best defense during some of their most recent winning seasons.

title: Pre-Snap Reads 7/22: Which Seahawks contract yields the most value?

fullText:
