title: How weird was Tuesday’s game?
description: I think it was pretty weird, and this isn’t a post about pitching management (not weird when botched by the Braves)
fullText:
The Braves lost to the Padres on Tuesday night. That in and of itself isn’t weird, especially considering that it came at home. Did you know that the Braves haven’t won a home series from the Padres since 2018? That’s a lot of really good years (including 2022 and 2023, where the team won 100-plus games), but… the Padres in Atlanta have been an issue. (The Braves have also lost a bunch of series to the Padres going back to mid-2024, including a playoff series… but they did actually win a road series against them in 2024.)

Anyway, here’s some random weird stuff about last night:

The Braves set a season-high in walk rate at 20.5 percent. They tied their season high in walks with eight. In the other three games they had eight walks, they scored eight, 10, and 17 runs. Yet, they scored just three runs.

Despite all the walks, the team had its second-worst quality of contact of the season (.224 xwOBACON). It was shut out in its only game with a worse xwOBACON on the season. In essence, the Braves did nothing on swings, but did a lot when not swinging.

Two of their runs scored on a not-that-well struck ball that perfectly split the defense and looked awesome. Jim Jarvis’ triple had a .163 xBA and .214 xwOBA, and came off the bat at 94 mph. Then, Jarvis had a nearly-perfect bloop single off Mason Miller of all people. Speaking of…

The Padres used Mason Miller in a five-run game for some reason. Talk about a bazooka to kill a fly. They had a five-run lead. Miller ended up having to throw a lot of pitches because of Jarvis’ bloop and a walk to Drake Baldwin, not that he had any real issues in the inning. Imagine using the best reliever in baseball in a situation where the other team could’ve been justified in pitching Elieser Hernandez in the top half of the inning.

The Braves scored a run on a balk. I don’t even have anything to add. I don’t know the last time the Braves scored a run on a balk. To add to the weirdness: when the home plate umpire spoke to the crowd about the balk, his words said that the batter at the time (Austin Riley) was to take first base, maybe because the pitch-on-which-there-was-a-balk was taken outside the zone. (The words were, “run scores, second and first” or something along those lines.) Yet, per the rules, Riley did not take first base, and actually struck out instead of making more of a game of it. So the umpire called a balk, and then goofed the verbal (not applied) explanation of what was to happen.

Bonus: the Phillies didn’t gain a game in the standings because of an Eli White-level baserunning mistake. In case you missed it, they had the tying run on third with one out in the ninth, and the go-ahead run on second in the form of JT Realmuto. Brandon Marsh hit a sliced dribbler to third. The runner on third was cut down in a rundown, prolonged enough to allow Realmuto to reach third. But, for some reason, Realmuto started running back to second base, which allowed the Dodgers to easily throw in that direction and tag him out, ending the game.

So, how weird does all this rate?

title: Where do you think Carolina Football will finish in the ACC?
description: It could be Heaven, or it could be Hell.
fullText:
It’s “talkin’ season,” as Steve Spurrier used to call it, and to riff on an old NASCAR adage, “if you ain’t provokin’, you ain’t talkin’.” In the wake of the ACC’s media event last week, pundits start rolling out various predictions and hot takes. Al on Monday highlighted ESPN’s most aggressive agent provocateur, Paul Finebaum, calling Belichick an embarrassment and suggesting he retire. USA Today predicted UNC would finish 14th in the conference. Preseason rankings (talkin’ starters) for head coach, probable starting QB, likely place of finish: UNC consistently appears somewhere in the bottom five teams in the ACC.

David Hale, who collaborated with Andrea Adelson to publish this startling piece heading into the TCU game last season, also dabbles in the editorial side of things. He took the forecasts one step further and had this to say on his Substack about UNC’s prospects next season.

16. Boston College Eagles

17. North Carolina Tar Heels

BC’s roster just looks pretty bleak. And UNC… I think if Travis Burgess can win the QB job and develop quickly under Bobby Petrino, there’s a shot. But if Billy Edwards is your QB with the rest of that roster and the schedule they have this year… I can see it getting very bad, very quickly.

That’s dead last in the conference.

Even the computers have a hard time knowing what to make of UNC heading into next season. One model projects a good defense ranking 36th by season’s send. Another, just as reputable, sees it likely to finish as one of the worst among P4 teams at 93rd. Similarly, talkin’ season is all over the map on the Tar Heels’ prospects as well.

So, there are a number of ways to think about the upcoming season. That includes not thinking about it at all, which is the strategy currently employed by some members of my family. There are the best-case scenarios for the optimists among us who want to live in hope. There’s the worst-case for the pessimists who prefer to expect disaster, as a buffer to experiencing the sort of disappointment we all suffered last September. There’s the space in between where the realists like to hang out.

Frankly, it’s all vibes at this point, and after last season’s tire fire of a season, the vibes aren’t vibing for most. Most Tar Heel fans are in “show me” mode. Still, all we’ve got between now and the opening kick in Dublin are our feelings about next season’s prospects. A case can be made the program shows up against TCU unexpectedly good in the same manner they showed up last season unexpectedly bad. A case can be made that a greatly improved but young team struggles to get to 6-6 against a much tougher schedule. A case can be made the wheels fall off entirely, which is where David Hale seems to think things end up.

Where are your feelings on that? What’s your best case for next season? Worst case? Most likely result?

title: Three reasons why hiring Jürgen Klopp will work for Germany
description: The former Liverpool boss is poised to make a big splash with the DFB.
fullText:
The football world regained one of it’s finest characters as Jürgen Klopp has come out of the shadows from his role at Red Bull, to join the DFB as the senior national team coach. While Germany has struggled over recent years, here are three reasons Klopp can turn the national team around and develop towards becoming the world’s best once again.

In case anyone forgot, Klopp is one of the best coaches in the world. With Borussia Dortmund and most recently, Liverpool, he brought his clubs to the top during his time. Liverpool when he joined on and Liverpool during his time coaching were completely different states. He brought Liverpool from mediocracy to their best run of performances in recent history. At Borussia Dortmund, he took them to the top with a budget way smaller than Europe’s biggest clubs.

At both Dortmund and Liverpool, Klopp was extremely successful at developing young talent into full-blown superstars. With the change of the guard to come at the national team, Klopp is the perfect man to get the most out of Florian Wirtz, Jamal Musiala, Lennart Karl, Said El Mala, Kennet Eichhorn, and many more talents that Germany have at their disposal.

Jürgen Klopp has managed Eintracht Frankfurt’s youth team, Mainz 05, and most notably, Borussia Dortmund. Klopp was highly successful in both Mainz and Dortmund, and both teams have players that represent Germany at the senior level. Klopp is highly respected in Germany and his connections will be crucial in ensuring he has a long enough leash to make a major impact.

While Nagelsmann managed multiple German clubs as well, he didn’t have the strength of earned reputation that Klopp maintains. That will massively help relations with clubs and players as well. It was evident that Nagelsmann did not know how to handle the press without saying bad things about his players or at best, providing mixed messaging. Klopp on the other hand, is a master of player management.

Do you think Klopp can bring Germany back to the top or is it too tall of a task? Let us know in the comments!

New, improved notifications system!

Comment on articles, community posts

title: Saints DE Cam Jordan to host 3rd annual “An Evening with Cam Jordan”
description: The signature event will benefit the Cam Jordan Foundation.
fullText:
New Orleans Saints DE Cam Jordan will host his 3rd annual “An Evening with Cam Jordan” fundraising event on Monday, October 12, 2026, at Generations Hall in New Orleans.

The foundation’s signature fundraising event will celebrate Jordan’s remarkable 16-year NFL career while launching the next chapter of his lifelong commitment to serving the New Orleans community.

The 3rd annual event will bring together Jordan’s current and former teammates, business leaders, corporate partners, community champions, and supporters from across Louisiana for an unforgettable celebration of gratitude, impact, and the future of the Cam Jordan Foundation.

As Jordan enters his final NFL season, the evening will mark more than the end of an extraordinary football career, it will celebrate a legacy built through service, leadership, and an unwavering commitment to the city that he has called home since 2011.

An event with Cam Jordan will serve as the centerpiece of the Foundation’s season-long “Thank You NOLA” campaign, a 16-week community initiative honoring the teachers, students, first responders, nonprofit leaders, lifelong Saints fans, and families who have supported Jordan throughout his career.

“For the last 16 years, New Orleans has been more than a city to me, it has become family,” said Jordan. “This final season is about saying thank you. Football gave me a platform, but the people of New Orleans gave me a purpose. I want to celebrate this community while continuing to invest in its future.”

16 seasons. One incredible legacy.⚜️💪 Join us on October 12 for the Third Annual An Evening with Cam Jordan, as we celebrate @camjordan94’s impact on and off the field while investing in the future of New Orleans. Be part of an unforgettable evening of gratitude, purpose, and… pic.twitter.com/xKNoHhQPVi

— Cam Jordan Foundation (@camjfoundation) July 21, 2026

Throughout the 2026 season, the Cam Jordan Foundation will expand its impact through 16 for 16, a weekly giveback initiative featuring school visits, surprise community activations, mentorship opportunities, neighborhood service projects, and special fan experiences celebrating Jordan’s lasting impact on New Orleans.

Proceeds from the annual dinner will directly support the foundation’s growing community initiatives, including:

●        The Legacy Fund, a long-term endowment supporting scholarships, mentorship, youth development, and community investments for generations to come.

●        The Legacy Scholarship Program, providing up to $12,500 annually in tuition assistance, mentorship, professional development, and career opportunities for Louisiana students pursuing higher education.

●        Sacks for Scholars, a season-long fundraising campaign where corporate partners and supporters pledge donations tied to Cam’s sack milestones, directly funding scholarships and educational programming.

●        16 for 16, a season-long initiative celebrating Jordan’s 16 years in New Orleans through meaningful community engagement and service.

The evening will also recognize the Foundation’s 2026 Community MVP, honoring an individual whose commitment to serving others reflects the values of leadership, generosity, and community impact that have defined Jordan’s career both on and off the field.

To learn more about the Cam Jordan Foundation, please visit Home | Cam Jordan Foundation

title: Upgrade or Downgrade at Offensive Line for the Gophers?
description: Will the Gopher’s offensive line be an improvement?
fullText:
We’ve covered Quarterback and Running Back in our Upgrade/Downgrade series, and both position groups seem to be a likely upgrade. It helps when you return your best player at that position, but now things start to get interesting for the offense. Aireonte Ersaey was a 2nd-round draft pick, Phillip Daniels left for NIL at Ohio State and then you lost 60 combined starts between Quinn Carrol and Tyler Cooper.

As per usual, the offensive line unit improved throughout the season, but it was not a strength in 2025. So after a down year, can this year’s unit be an upgrade?

2025 Starter: Nathan Roy, Greg Johnson, Ashton Beers, Tony Nelson, Dylan Ray 
2026 Starters: Roy, Johnson, Beers, Tony Nelson, Bennett Warren
Depth: Jerome Williams, Brett Carroll, Jaden Ball, Daniel Shipp, Kaveon Lee, Mark Handy

The good news here is that four starters return, and a nice transfer piece has been added.

Nathan Roy was the starter at left tackle last year for 12 of the 13 games and he had a nice season. And if you factor in that he was just a redshirt freshman, there is reason to believe that he is going to be an excellent blind-side protector for Drake Lindsey.

Next to him, you have a senior who has made 28 starts for the Gophers. Greg Johnson saw a ton of action as a true freshman, starting twice, and then has started every game as a sophomore and junior. He could very easily be an All-Big Ten caliber player this fall. And with the new five-for-five rule, I believe he’d be eligible to return in 2027, if he chose.

Under center is Beers, who started all 13 games at center last year. He will be a redshirt senior and should be a reliable starter.

After those three on the left, the rest of the line has more question marks. But most likely you will see Nelson at right guard and the Tennessee transfer, Warren, at right tackle.

Nelson is a redshirt senior who has started a few games over the last couple of seasons and likely has the inside track at starting. He also brings versatility to the line as he can play multiple positions, inside or out. But there are others lurking to take the starting RG spot. Brett Carroll and Jerome Williams both started during the Gophers’ spring game and Jaden Ball should also be in the mix. Ball and Carroll are both redshirt sophomores and should be more ready to contribute than they were a year ago. And Williams is a senior who has yet to see meaningful playing time, but he has battled injuries.

And finally, at right tackle is the transfer, Warren. He spent his first two seasons at Tennessee, where he originally signed as a highly sought-after four-star recruit. He redshirted in 2024, saw limited action in 2025 and came to the Gophers with the intention of earning the starting right tackle position. Daniel Shipp and Kaveon Lee both redshirted last year and could find their way onto the field, most likely at one of the tackle spots.

The depth along the line, particularly at tackle, is young. The future of the OL is bright, but should we need to tap into that depth this year, it might be a little green.

There should be a lot more confidence in this offensive line than there was a year ago. The two starters on the left are guys with All-Big Ten talent and Beers at center is more than capable. You have more questions on the right side, but there are much better potential answers than we had in 2025. Who starts at RG is important, but whoever it is should be fine. Who starts at RT and how they perform is critical. Warren has the talent to be that guy, but it is yet to be realized.

I’m optimistic about this year’s line and think this will be a certain upgrade.

title: Chicago Bears 2026 Training Camp Position Breakdown: Linebacker
description: In this position-by-position series, @wiltfongjr will preview the Bears roster and make his picks for who could stick around in 2026.
fullText:
The Chicago Bears made only one* significant change to their linebacking corps this season, but this group will look much different than the group that played in 2025.

Tremaine Edmunds, who was much better than fans want to give him credit for, was cut in a cap-saving move, and the Bears signed Devin Bush to take his place. Bush got a 3-year, $30 million deal, but his cap hit in 2026 will only be $5.6M. The Bears will slide Bush into the weakside linebacker spot, and his speed and athleticism should be an upgrade. Pro Football Focus had Bush as the only linebacker in 2025 (while in Cheveland) to grade above 80 against the run and in pass coverage. The 28-year-old had a slow start to his career, but he’s really coming into his own.

The Bears gave D’Marco Jackson a new 2-year, $7.5M deal, with some playing time incentives, and my guess is they want his speed on the field more this season. Last year, he received the first starts of his pro career, and in those 5 games (4 in the regular season, 1 in the playoffs), he had 37 tackles, 2 tackles for loss, a sack, and an interception, while being named the NFC Defensive Player of the Week for his Week 15 performance. His sample size was obviously smaller (261 regular-season defensive snaps), but PFF had him with an overall grade of 83.8.

T.J. Edwards was extended last offseason, but he struggled with injuries before he fractured his fibula in the playoff win over the Packers. He was never the most athletic linebacker, instead relying on his smarts to give him an edge, so the Bears need him back at 100%. Luckily, early reports had him doing some stuff at OTAs, so he may be ready when training camp opens. Edwards, who won the Brian Piccolo award for the third straight year, is a team leader both on and off the field, and as long as he’s healthy, he’ll be starting at middle linebacker.

My guess is that in some defensive subpackages, it’ll be Bush and Jackson staying on the field while Edwards comes off, but this could be matchup reliant depending on the offense’s personnel and down and distance.

The Bears picked Keyshaun Elliott in the fifth round a year after spending a fourth on linebacker Ruben Hyppolite II, but more on Ruben in the next section. Elliot has good instincts and was an excellent blitzer in college. I can see him as the eventual heir apparent at the Mike, but as a rookie, he’ll need to excel in the third phase to cement his spot on the 53.

*The Bears did bring fan favorite Jack Sanborn back after he spent a year in Dallas, where he missed 11 games with a groin injury. The soon-to-be 26-year-old signed a one-year league minimum deal, but his experience could give him an edge.

“I love it here in Chicago and being part of what [coach] Ben Johnson and [defensive coordinator] Dennis Allen have been building here and the success that they’ve had,” Sanborn told ChicagoBears.com after re-signing with his hometown team. “It’s something I couldn’t pass up, and I’m grateful that I’m able to come back here and help in any way I can.”

Tony Fields II played in 45 games (10 starts) from 2021 to 2024 with the Browns, but was injured and waived in ’24 before landing on the Rams practice squad. He didn’t play in 2025 after being cut by the Rams, but in 2026, he made the All-UFL team as a member of the Columbus Aviators. His experience will keep him in the mix for a job.

Jon Rhattigan is another veteran special teamer with over 1,200 snaps from 2021 to 2025.

Wayne Matthews III is a 2026 UDFA from Michigan State, and at his pro day, he was the only non-lineman to hit 20 reps on the bench press.

Hyppolite only saw action in 7 games as a rookie (31 snaps on defense and 82 snaps on special teams) while dealing with a shoulder injury and not being able to crack the active game day roster when healthy. His speed and athleticism will give him an opportunity to shine in the third phase, but if he can’t prove worthy of playing defense, he might be destined for the practice squad.

The Bears carried 5 linebackers last year, so how many do you think make it this year?

title: 5 under-the-radar players who could have breakout performances during Broncos training camp in 2026
description: We already talked about the eight Denver Broncos players who I believe could have breakout performances during training camp and the preseason. That list included a lot of the expected names like Jahdae Barron, Jonah Coleman, Que Robinson, and others. However, there are five players who I left off this list who I believe could […]
fullText:
We already talked about the eight Denver Broncos players who I believe could have breakout performances during training camp and the preseason. That list included a lot of the expected names like Jahdae Barron, Jonah Coleman, Que Robinson, and others. However, there are five players who I left off this list who I believe could have breakout performances that are currently a bit under the radar.

These aren’t your big names, but players pushing for a roster spot or an elevated role on the team that could solidify their spots on the roster and with the team after a strong performance during camp and the preseason.

With all that said, here are the five under-the-radar Denver Broncos players who I believe could have breakout performances

Lohner was a 7th-round pick last season by the Broncos in 2025 and was a long-term developmental player for them. He was a former basketball player with one season of college football under his belt but had the size and athletic traits that Sean Payton and the Broncos believed they could develop.

His rookie year was pretty quiet, as expected, and he spent the entire year on the Denver Broncos practice squad. Despite that, teams attempted to pry Lohner from the Broncos’ practice squad, but the developmental rookie decided to stay with the team that drafted him. Now, he enters year two looking to push for a spot on the Broncos’ 53-man roster.

Lohner made some noise early in the offseason during the Broncos 2026 rookie minicamp after Head Coach Sean Payton went out of his way to praise the developmental second-year player.

Sean Payton out of nowhere today after rookie minicamp: “I’ll tell you who stood out — Caleb (Lohner).”

Usually doesn’t single guys out unprompted, but here’s two mins of Payton raving on #Broncos second-year TE Lohner. 

“He’s in great shape. You see his athleticism.” pic.twitter.com/EswOk20o9X

— Luca Evans (@bylucaevans) May 9, 2026

Payton was then asked what Lohner is doing better this year compared to last year. He simply said, “Everything”.

“Everything. In fairness to him, remember [he] was a basketball prospect that had limited snaps of a year. So one year into the program, and how he’s moving, what he’s doing, everything looks entirely different. That learning curve and growth curve was greater for someone who had far less experience maybe than others, if that makes sense. It’s entirely noticeable, and he’s in great shape. You see his athleticism. I would say the one thing that you don’t know is… [Pro Football Hall of Fame Coach] Bill [Parcells] used to say, ‘If they don’t bite when they’re puppies, they’re probably never going to bite.’ There’s a physical aspect to the way he plays. Even on look team last season, he’d play an outside linebacker rushing our tackles. He’s 6’7”, 265 pounds and he’s not afraid of the contact. So it’s been good to see him in Year 2. You knew there was going to be a developmental upside, but that was encouraging.”

Unfortunately for Lohner, his hype would be short-lived after he would undergo a cleanup procedure that would sideline him throughout the entirety of the Broncos’ offseason program. However, he is expected to be 100% for the start of training camp and will look to reignite the hype he had this spring and push for a roster spot.

The Broncos tight end room is a bit of a weak point on paper. They have veterans Adam Trautman and Evan Engram, and behind those two, you have Lohner, Nate Adkins, Lucas Krull, and rookies Justin Joly and Dallen Bentley. So, it will be interesting to see how everything shakes out here and where Lohner lands when everything is set and done.

Late in the spring, the Broncos signed former UFL wide receiver Hakeem Butler to a one-year deal. He is a 6-5, 227-pound wide receiver with good speed and athleticism. He was a former 4th-round pick back in 2019 by the Arizona Cardinals, but it simply never worked out in the NFL. He would join the UFL and become the best wide receiver in UFL history, which included two Offensive Player of the Year awards, and rejuvenate his football career. That performance earned him a chance with the Broncos, where he would sign on to their 90-man roster and now will look to compete for a spot on their 53-man roster or on their practice squad.

New Broncos WR Hakeem Butlerpic.twitter.com/owpBA6Q9iO

— MileHighReport (@MileHighReport) June 15, 2026

I have Butler as an under-the-radar breakout candidate simply because he is a bit of a wild card. He is 30 years old, but has great size at 6-5, 227 pounds, and has high-end athleticism to go with that size. I wouldn’t be surprised if he went out there and dominated against the Broncos’ second and third-string cornerbacks during camp and did the same in the second half of the Broncos’ preseason games.

He does seem like a bit of a long shot, but the size and athleticism are there, and we’ll see if he can turn his UFL success into success in the NFL this summer.

Devon Key was a breakout player for the Broncos on the special teams side of the ball last season. He went from a fringe roster player to becoming an All-Pro special teamer for the Broncos by the end of the season. Now, could he see a similar growth on the defensive side of the ball?

I am not saying he will turn into an All-Pro safety, but the third safety role left behind by PJ Locke, who signed with the Dallas Cowboys, is there for the taking, and Key seems like the logical in-house replacement. I believe Key, who has earned the trust of the Broncos coaches, will get the first crack at it, and if he does well, will have an elevated role on the defensive side of the ball this season.

I would expect veterans Talanoa Hufanga and Brandon Jones to get days off throughout camp, so Key and others figure to get some first-team reps this summer. If he performs well, he could be an under-the-radar breakout candidate who goes from fringe roster player to All-Pro special teamer to becoming a key defensive reserve on top of his special teams work.

Rookie Miles Scott, JL Skinner, special teamer Tycen Anderson, and undrafted rookie Parker Robertson will all be competing with Key for that role on the defensive side of the ball this summer.

One player I am higher on than most is undrafted rookie linebacker Taurean York. He is rather undersized, a big reason why he went undrafted, but he is very talented and has the instincts you want from an NFL linebacker. If he were 6-1, 235 pounds, he likely would have been a day 2 selection and talked about as one of the top linebackers in this draft class.

A big reason he could have a breakout summer is the depth at the Broncos linebacker position. The depth behind starters Alex Singleton and Justin Strnad is very young and inexperienced and offers York (and others) a road to climb up the depth chart. If York is able to survive in the NFL despite his size, I believe his talent will carry him to a spot on the Broncos’ 53-man roster.

LB Taurean York signed as an undrafted free agent with the Broncos 

Has a chance to push for a spot on the 53-man rosterpic.twitter.com/v4ekhdVCGL

— MileHighReport (@MileHighReport) April 26, 2026

The Broncos have had a lot of success with former undrafted free agent players, and I firmly believe York will be the one we are all talking about this summer. His football IQ, intelligence, leadership, and instincts will make him a coach’s favorite from the start, and his play on the field will have him rising up the depth chart. Again, size will be an issue, but it was when he played in the SEC, and he still thrived, so hopefully he does the same in the NFL with the Broncos.

We are all talking about Sai’vion Jones and rookie Tyler Onyedim, two players I mentioned in my breakout piece, but I believe the massive Eyioma Uwazurike is somehow flying under the radar.

At 6-6, 320 pounds, he is an imposing presence on the field and features a mean bull rush and has a motor that does not quit. Coming out of college, he played some edge rusher and displayed his high-end athleticism despite his size, so he does have pass rush potential. We have seen Uwazurike grow as a player on and off the field and went from being suspended for an entire season to being a key reserve on a top-ranked defense in the NFL. Now, with a role opened up by John Franklin-Myers, who signed with the Titans during free agency, could Uwazurike take over that role?

Due to his massive size and strength, Uwazurike could take on extra blockers and allow Zach Allen, Nik Bonitto, and others to thrive with one-on-one blocks. He also has the size, strength, and athleticism to disrupt the pocket and get after the quarterback.

He will be competing with Malcolm Roach, Sai’vion Jones, rookie Tyler Onyedim, and others for snaps and a role along this defensive line. While all the talk is about Jones and Onyedim, I wouldn’t rule out Uwazurike being the player who steps up and takes over that role left behind by John Franklin-Myers.

title: Every Richmond player drafted by the Boston Celtics
description: Let's take a look at every player who has been drafted by the Celtics out of Richmond.
fullText:
The Boston Celtics have built their teams in a number of ways over the decades, but few if any methods have born more fruit than through the NBA draft. The best players to suit up for the Celtics have, by and large, come to Boston either through being taken directly in the annual event, or via trades made that night.

And it is not just the top stars who have been picked up by the Celtics via the draft. Countless members of the storied ball club's alumni have been taken by the team via that annual event, and some schools are better represented than others. We can see blue blood programs and some very small schools both delivering top talent to Boston's rosters over the years, so we decided to take a look at which players came from which schools overall.

So without further ado, let's take a look at every player who has been drafted by the Celtics out of Richmond.

title: NBC earns best Open Championship audience since 2022
description: American sports fans looking for an appetizer prior to Sunday’s World Cup final seemed to get one in the 154th Open Championship. NBC’s final-round coverage of the last men’s golf major of the year averaged 4.3 million viewers, up six percent from last year’s victory from Scottie Scheffler at Royal Portrush (4.1 million viewers). Ryan…
fullText:
American sports fans looking for an appetizer prior to Sunday’s World Cup final seemed to get one in the 154th Open Championship.

NBC’s final-round coverage of the last men’s golf major of the year averaged 4.3 million viewers, up six percent from last year’s victory from Scottie Scheffler at Royal Portrush (4.1 million viewers). Ryan Fox’s dramatic win capped with a birdie on the 72nd hole peaked at 6.1 million viewers during the 1:15 p.m. ET quarter-hour.

As usual, the Open Championship was the least-watched of the four majors due to a less favorable time zone for the American audience. NBC’s final-round window began at 9 a.m. and went through 2 p.m. ET, whereas final-round coverage of the Masters, for instance, begins at 2 p.m. and typically runs until about 7 p.m. ET. Those Sunday afternoon hours are prime viewing times for the U.S. audience. Sunday morning? Not so much.

As Sports Business Journal’s Josh Carpenter lays out, June’s U.S. Open averaged 5.5 million viewers in the final round, the PGA Championship 5.7 million viewers in May, and the Masters 13.9 million viewers in April.

NBC: 4.3 million viewers for the final round of @TheOpen, its best since 2022, though with the addition of Big Data, fair to wonder if last year (4.1M) would have outdrawn it.

Major viewership this year (final rounds)

Masters: 13.9M
 PGA: 5.7M
 U.S. Open: 5.5M
 Open: 4.3M pic.twitter.com/1jvOdTBxTz

— Josh Carpenter (@JoshACarpenter) July 21, 2026

Still, holding an audience of 4.3 million viewers across a five-hour window early on Sunday is a solid result for NBC. It should be noted, this is the first Open Championship to be measured using Nielsen’s new Big Data + Panel standard, and the six percent year-over-year increase falls within the range that could solely be explained by the changes.

Saturday saw a larger year-over-year bump, however. Third-round coverage on NBC and Peacock averaged 3.7 million viewers, up 16 percent versus last year (3.2 million viewers) and good for the most-watched third round since 2018.

The post NBC earns best Open Championship audience since 2022 appeared first on Awful Announcing.

title: 3 Broncos made AP's top NFL players list (plus Sean Payton as a coach)
description: Three Broncos players (and coach Sean Payton) were named among the best at their respective positions by the Associated Press.
fullText:
With training camp right around the corner, it's officially List Season in the NFL.

The Associated Press recently ranked the top five players at each position group across the league, and three Denver Broncos players made the cut.

Notably, Pat Surtain was named top-ranked cornerback for the second consecutive year.

"Surtain received seven first-place votes from a panel of eight AP pro football writers who ranked the top five players at the position, basing selections on current status entering the 2026 season," Rob Maaddi wrote for AP.

Additionally, Quinn Meinerz was the third-ranked interior offensive lineman and Garett Bolles was the third-ranked offensive tackle. On defense, Zach Allen fell in the also received votes category at defensive tackle.

The squad is led by Sean Payton, who ranked fifth among 32 head coaches in the AP survey.

Surtain, Meinerz and Bolles all received AP All-Pro recognition following the 2025 season, so it's no surprise that they have been ranked among the best players at their respective positions going into the 2026 campaign.

title: Analyst makes bold claim about Oklahoma's Courtland Guillory
description: Courtland Guillory has the talent to become one of the SEC's top defensive backs in 2026.
fullText:
The Oklahoma Sooners enter the 2026 season with one of the most talented cornerback duos in college football. Junior Eli Bowen and sophomore Courtland Guillory both earned freshman All-American honors during their first seasons in Norman.

Guillory emerged as a key contributor last season, earning a starting role early in the year while Bowen missed time due to an injury. However, when Bowen returned, it wasn't Guillory's job he took; instead, it was on the opposite side. That was a testament to how impressive the former three-star prospect had been throughout the season.

Guillory finished the year with 41 tackles, one tackle for loss, and seven pass deflections. His performance led On3's J.D. PicKell to suggest Guillory could enter 2026 as the best defensive back in the SEC.

"The one underclassman they're going to play is Courtland Guillory, sophomore corner," PicKell said. "I've said it before, he might be the best DB in the SEC, period. Now, he has to play that way, but I have no reservations about leaving No. 4 out there on an island and letting him do his thing."

Guillory has already shown he belongs among the best young defensive backs in college football, but 2026 will provide his biggest test yet. Playing in the SEC means facing some of the best receivers in the country every week, and Guillory will have plenty of opportunities to prove that PicKell's high praise is justified.

If he continues developing alongside Bowen, Oklahoma could have one of the most difficult cornerback duos for opposing offenses to attack all season.

Contact/Follow us @SoonersWire on X (formerly known as Twitter), and like our page on Facebook to follow ongoing coverage of Oklahoma news, notes, and opinions. You can also follow Jaron on X @jaronspor.

This article originally appeared on Sooners Wire: Analyst makes bold claim about Oklahoma's Courtland Guillory

title: Ben Askren says legs, not lungs, failed him in RAF 11 farewell match
description: Ben Askren admits he didn't know how his body would perform at RAF 11 against Belal Muhammad.
fullText:
Ben Askren wasn't exactly sure what his body was capable of doing before he stepped onto the mats at RAF 11.

Askren has wrestled for most of his life; however, Saturday's match against former UFC champion Belal Muhammad was his first (and only) time competing since a miraculous recovery from critical illness and a double-lung transplant. When the match was announced, many didn't know how to feel about it, including Askren's podcast co-host, UFC Hall of Famer Daniel Cormier.

"I was nervous for you before the match," Cormier said on "Funky and the Champ." "I didn't know that I loved you going to compete after that, but I think after watching last weekend – one, you just literally showed that you're Ben Askren, and you f*cking can do just amazing things – congratulations. But also, I think you kind of showed people not to be limited by stuff, right? You can never become a victim to your circumstance."

The match took place as scheduled, and Askren said he felt fine on the day of the event. So much so that he said he felt "normal" for a little while, but he also made sure to be aware that his body is not what it used to be. Muhammad ended up winning the match, but for Askren to just get onto the mats to compete was a huge win, and perhaps an even bigger relief that nothing bad happened.

"It was amazing," Askren said. "If you were to write a script, the only way you could have written a script better would be if I was able to kind of hold him off in the third period, right? I get a lead, he's coming back, I'm battling, I held him off – and my body just didn't have that one in it. So, besides that it was great.

"The crowd was really tremendous. It was powerful. RAF did a great job with the presentation, the production. It was awesome."

Getting in shape to compete was a huge hurdle that Askren had to clear. He was on death's doorstep last July, and was nearly gone four times. A year later, he managed to build his body back up to wrestle against an active former UFC champion. It's an incredibly inspiring story of resilience, but even Askren admits he didn't know what his body was capable of handling, and told Cormier he was disappointed that his cardio level didn't meet his expectations.

"I didn't know if I could wrestle hard for six minutes," Askren said. "When I got the lead and then Belal came out in the third and started pushing me hard, I was like, sh*t, I don't have anything left. There's nothing left in the tank. I knew I would compete to the best of my abilities. I knew I would try as hard as I could, so I wasn't ever really worried about that. If you can have a lot of faith in that, then usually you're OK with it.

"... I actually misspoke in the post-fight thing, I said my lungs hurt and my legs hurt. I was so tired, I meant to say my lungs didn't hurt. My legs got so tired that I just couldn't stand up, but that was normal, because that's how most of my workouts went for the past six to eight weeks. I was trying to really push myself on the aerodine, and my legs would give out before my lungs would."

This article originally appeared on MMA Junkie: Ben Askren says legs, not lungs, failed him in RAF 11 farewell match

title: 'Playing for this club is all I've ever wanted to do' - Foden signs extension
description: Manchester City attacking midfielder Phil Foden says he "can't wait to work with Enzo Maresca again" after penning a new four-year deal to extend his already lengthy stay at Etihad Stadium.  The 26-year-old, who is a lifelong Manchester City fan, joined the club's academy set-up as a nine-year-old and made his first-team debut under Pep Guardiola as a 17-year-old.  "Committing my future to City means everything to me," Foden said.
fullText:
Manchester City attacking midfielder Phil Foden says he "can't wait to work with Enzo Maresca again" after penning a new four-year deal to extend his already lengthy stay at Etihad Stadium.

The 26-year-old, who is a lifelong Manchester City fan, joined the club's academy set-up as a nine-year-old and made his first-team debut under Pep Guardiola as a 17-year-old.

"Committing my future to City means everything to me," Foden said. "Playing for this club is all I've ever wanted to do. It is always an honour to wear this shirt.

"It isn't lost on me that I've been lucky enough to be part of a historic period, with so many titles won, but we are always looking to the future and trying to win more."

The England international has won 20 major honours during his time at City: six Premier League titles, five EFL Cups, three FA Cups, three Community Shields plus a Champions League, a Super Cup and a Club World Cup.

"I can only thank the club, the staff, my team-mates and the fans who continue to put their faith in me and trust that I give everything I can for City. I hope I'll be able to repay you for years to come.

"I can't wait to work with Enzo Maresca again. He was brilliant during our Treble season, someone all the players respected and loved working with, so to have him back here is exciting.

"I'm excited for the next phase of my career at this great club."

Director of Football Hugo Viana added: "He represents everything good about our academy. He is a unique talent and his creativity is a joy to watch.

"None of what he has achieved would have been possible without his focus, dedication and humility to work hard in every moment and absorb all of the knowledge available to him through our coaches and players.

"His continued success is one that is celebrated in every corner of our club."

title: Rams fall to dead last in new ESPN ranking
description: The Rams roster is one of the oldest in the NFL but also doesn't have too much talent under the age of 25.
fullText:
The Los Angeles Rams may have one of the best rosters on paper, but it's mainly constructed of veterans rather than long-term developmental players. As such, the Rams' roster ranks 26th-oldest heading into the 2026 season.

This isn't a bad thing, especially for a team with immediate Super Bowl aspirations spearheaded by a 38-year-old quarterback, but it means they checked in at No. 32 in ESPN's latest ranking of NFL teams' under-25 talent.

ESPN built the rankings using starts and snaps logged by players under 25, age-adjusted quality of play with extra weight for Pro Bowl and All-Pro selections, contract value and length, positional importance, draft capital added in the first three rounds of the 2026 draft, projected 2026 depth charts, and any injuries or suspensions likely to affect availability.

Unsurprisingly, the Rams don't have many of those types of players on their roster at the moment.

Perhaps you have heard that the Rams are not as fond of first-round draft picks as other teams? Certainly, trades they have made for veterans contribute to the fact that while Los Angeles has a ton of talent, very little of it is under 25 years old.

The Rams only had two players currently under 25 who started games last season: safety Kamren Kinchens, who will be 24 at the end of September, and tight end Terrance Ferguson, who is also 23. Another one of the tight ends, second-round rookie Max Klare, just turned 23 on July 8. First-round quarterback Simpson will turn 24 in December. The Rams hope Simpson doesn't get on the field for a couple of seasons because they have someone much older they would rather see under center: reigning MVP Matthew Stafford, who is 38 years old.

First-round quarterback Ty Simpson is the lone player ESPN tabs as a blue chip for the Rams' under-25 core, and even he isn't expected to see the field anytime soon behind Stafford. Compounding the shortage, Los Angeles just graduated several of its best young pieces out of eligibility: cornerback Trent McDuffie, offensive tackle Warren McClendon Jr. and wide receiver Puka Nacua all turned 25 and no longer count toward the rankings.

That combination — an aging roster built through trades and free agency, a limited draft footprint and a wave of players aging out right as they hit their prime — leaves the Rams with the league's thinnest projected young talent pool heading into 2026, according to ESPN.

But again, that won't matter if they bring home a Lombardi Trophy.

This article originally appeared on Rams Wire: Rams fall to dead last in new ESPN ranking

title: Tuli Tuipulotu makes NFL's top 100 players list due to elite season
description: Tuli Tuipulotu has become a household name in the NFL as a pass rusher with the Chargers. Can Tuli help the Bolts take the next step?
fullText:
Each season, Tuli Tuipulotu is making his name known as a top pass rusher in the NFL. The Chargers' faith in him is growing. They moved on from former top draft pick Joey Bosa and let Odafe Oweh walk. Tuli Tuipulotu is becoming the cornerstone of the Chargers' defense, and he is getting more respect as a result.

In a recent article, Bobby Kownack breaks down Tuli Tuipulotu making the NFL top 100 players list. Here is what he had to say about Tuipulotu:

"Incremental improvements through his first three years led to a certified breakout for Tuipulotu last season. The 23-year-old officially emerged from the shadow of his pass-rushing mentor Khalil Mack and lapped the rest of L.A’s edge defenders, as well, leading the team in sacks for a second straight campaign to make his first Pro Bowl. His 13 sacks doubled his career total, and his pressure percentage jumped from 10.4 across 2023-24 to 15.6. Now, the question is whether he has yet another leap in store for 2026."

Tuli Tuipulotu continues to develop his game, and this past season, he put it all together, leading to a Pro Bowl nod for him. When teams face the Chargers, #45 is the main guy they are preparing for all week long. Getting on the NFL's top 100 players list is just the beginning for Tuli. This upcoming season should bring even more dominance for the former Trojan.

Contact/Follow @TrojansWire on X (formerly Twitter) and like our page on Facebook to follow ongoing coverage of USC Trojans news, notes and opinion.

This article originally appeared on Trojans Wire: Tuli Tuipulotu makes NFL's top 100 players list due to elite season

title: Bengals lose one popular trade target idea before training camp
description: A contract extension elsewhere in the NFL means the Bengals won't be making this suggested trade.
fullText:
Every now and then, the Cincinnati Bengals popped up in Jordyn Brooks trade ideas.

The Dolphins hit Brooks with a contract extension this week, ending any speculation that the new regime in Miami would go ahead and ship away the star linebacker, continuing the major roster blow-up over there.

Brooks was a fun idea, at least. He racked up 183 total tackles last season and is still just 28 years old after being a first-round pick in 2020.

It was easy to link the Bengals and Brooks. The front office in Cincinnati has been unusually aggressive this offseason. Linebacker was the worst spot on a terrible defense last year. And of all the moves the Bengals have made this offseason, they have yet to address linebacker.

Alas, the Bengals are all-in on Demetrius Knight and Barrett Carter. That won’t stop them from making another free-agent signing or trade during training camp in the name of better depth, but they believe the second-year duo remains the way of the future at the needy spot.

This article originally appeared on Bengals Wire: Bengals lose one popular trade target idea before training camp

title: Iraola on transfer plans, Alisson's future and Szoboszlai's new deal
description: Liverpool boss Andoni Iraola has been speaking to the media in Chicago as the Reds begin their pre-season tour of the USA.  At the end if you have a fully fit, healthy Conor Bradley, we are very happy, ready to go and we would not be asking ourselves these questions.
fullText:
Liverpool boss Andoni Iraola has been speaking to the media in Chicago as the Reds begin their pre-season tour of the USA.

Here are the key lines from his news conference:

On the importance of pre-season, Iraola said: "Pe-season is always the foundation of what's going to be a long season. When you arrive to a new club it's even more important because you don't have a lot of training sessions, you don't have a lot of even friendlies. It's massive because sometimes we lack days of training properly, of training with all the group of players, so everything that we can get from every friendly we play, we have to use it properly."

Asked about his wishes for the remainder of the transfer window, he said: "I think it's difficult to say a number when the market is open, and you have to be always open to new options to improve the squad. A winger, for example – we definitely need to sign a winger. But there are other situations that we will have to analyse what the market gives you, what's the cost, how we also see the players we have. So, it will depend on a lot of things."

Iraola was asked about goalkeeper Alisson's uncertain future: "I'm very happy with the situation we have right now in the goalkeeping area. We have Alisson, we have Giorgi [Mamardashvili]. We are going to send on loan, for example, Armin [Pecsi]. Alisson is someone also in a changing room that has lost some references in terms of experience, I think he's someone that definitely will help us in this process of adaptation of the new signings, of the new players, the young players."

Is right-back an area to address?: "I think it depends on Conor's [Bradley] situation. At the end if you have a fully fit, healthy Conor Bradley, we are very happy, ready to go and we would not be asking ourselves these questions. I think it's a situation that is tricky, because we trust Conor in the long term, we know we want him to be our right-back but right now he is still, I would say, a little bit far from returning."

On Dominik Szoboszlai's new contract: "I think this is one of the probably greatest news since I arrived, his commitment to this club, his contract renewal. Also, he has come in a great shape. He has been leading by example in training, he's pushing a lot. I think young players should be looking at him, the way he is training and he competes."

Iraola said he will address the club's vice-captaincy later in pre-season, with the role vacant after the departure of Andy Robertson: "I still haven't met almost half of the squad, I still haven't seen them face-to-face. But I always say, you don't need to wear an armband to be a leader, to be a captain."

Got a question about Liverpool? Get in touch here and we'll seek answers from our experts

title: Pirates reportedly listening to offers for Oneil Cruz
description: The Bucs are reportedly thinking of dealing Cruz.
fullText:
The Pittsburgh Pirates are heading down the back half of the season in the midst of a playoff race, and they have a myriad of questions surrounding whether or not they will be buyers or sellers at the MLB Trade Deadline. Apparently, according to one source, the Bucs are listening to offers on outfielder Oneil Cruz.

Holy crap: Big time baseball insider Bob Nightengale says the Pirates will listen to offers on Oneil Cruz. First time I’ve heard a credible voice legitimize the Cruz trade rumors. pic.twitter.com/oAyPoHpcrj

— Andrew Fillipponi (@ThePoniExpress) July 20, 2026

MLB inside source Bob Nightengale had this to say about the situation:

“They do have outfield depth. Can they trade Oneil Cruz to get some bullpen help? They need bullpen help as much as any contender out there. They can use another starter too. So I think they’re going to listen to offers on Oneil Cruz to see if they get the other pieces.”

Cruz has been the topic of trade speculation before, as many people felt he wasn’t reaching his potential in Pittsburgh and could get some interest from teams chasing a pennant. The irony is that Cruz is having his best season at the plate.

Despite currently being on the 60-day injured list, Cruz is expected back sometime around the beginning of to middle August. He’s averaging .264 at the plate with 14 homers and 44 RBIs as well as 21 stolen bases. The average is the best of his career, as is his OBP of .350 and his slugging percentage of .472.

He still has some work to do in the field, with his .986 fielding percentage sitting in the 30s for centerfielders, but overall, Cruz is having his finest season as a pro, which would certainly drive his value higher. One potential issue is do we trust Ben Cherington to get the most out of a Cruz deal? He hasn’t exactly been crushing trades with the Bucs.

But with the team currently loaded on offense and the pitching staff struggling, dealing Cruz for an arm to help down the stretch run certainly isn’t the worst idea.

Tell us in the comments how you feel about a possible Cruz deal.

title: Game Changing Moves At Major League Pickleball Deadline — Impact Analysis
description: At the season's trade deadline, two Major League Pickleball teams went "all in" on the 2026 season, making huge moves to try for a title.
fullText:
The Major League Pickleball (MLP) season trade deadline has come and gone, and after weeks of speculation, both teams that pundits though had flaws in their pursuit of the league favorite St. Louis Shock made moves to try to shake up the playoffs. We’ll discuss those moves, as well as the 5th and final waiver deadline, and do some analysis as to whether they’ll change the outcome of the 2026 MLP playoffs.

Here’s links at the official MLP site for the two transaction periods.

Click here for the MLP official Trade Deadline transaction list

Click here for the MLP official Waiver period moves.

This week at the trade deadline, the following transactions were announced:

Los Angeles finally made a trade to try to upgrade its component parts, acquiring Connor Garnett from Utah in a 3-team deal.

My sources tell me Garnett will just play mixed alongside Parenteau, while Freeman will continue to play with Johns, which seems like folly to me; Garnett is a top 15 Men’s doubles player (currently ranked No. 13 on the PPA) and is a huge upgrade over Freeman (ranked 32nd). A Johns/Garnett doubles pair can absolutely go toe-to-toe with the league’s top men’s pairings, while a Garnett/Parenteau pairing puts Catherine back on the right side and should improve on their terrible 11-13 record in Mixed-2 matches this season. The bigger question is simple: is this “enough” for LA to compete?

Before the moves, here were the records of the component parts for LA this season (all the records in this story are courtesy of Picklekey.com):

Parenteau/Kawamoto Women’s: 15-12

Freeman/Parenteau Mixed-2: 11-13

So, assuming that you slot in Garnett like-for-like for Freeman, this move is a direct attempt to slightly improve on a 21-6 Men’s Doubles record, but more importantly to try to improve on a Mixed-2 partnership that has really struggled. Garnett is just 12-14 himself in Mixed this year, but with players much less accomplished than Catherine. A Garnett/Parenteau partnership puts Catherine back on the right and should improve their performance in Mixed-2 matches (which, in theory, go against the weaker Mixed pairing of the other team).

The bigger problem for LA though is this: why are Parenteau and Kawamoto “just” 15-12 together in Women’s this year? We’re talking two of the absolute best women’s doubles players in the world. Is it possible that LA was trying to solve the wrong problem here?

One last comment: DreamBreaker performance. Here’s the singles performance for all the LA connected players this season:

Johns: 24-23 (this is kind of shocking actually)

Garnett should marginally improve the LA DreamBreaker performance, which has been carried by Parenteau this season (they’re 3-1 in DBs this season, with wins over StL, Atl, and PB and a loss to Dallas last weekend in San Diego).

Conclusion? The LA strategy is simple: win both Ben Johns matches, try to steal one of the others, then grind out a DreamBreaker win where they put out three of the best singles players in the game. Will Garnett help with that strategy? Marginally; if Johns was 21-6 with Freeman, what would he have been with Garnett? In reality, LA will rise or fall with the performance of Parenteau in doubles, and if Garnett can get CP to dominate in Mixed-2 LA will be a tough out.

Side-effects of the Garnett move: Utah gets Tyler Loong back from Miami, Miami gets LA’s former bench player/singles specialist Gabe Joseph, and Utah gets (reportedly) $125k of cash from LA to make the deal happen. Loong likely goes back to Utah’s bench, since they’re now running with the Tama & Yuta show, while Joseph was, in true Miami fashion, heading out the door as soon as he was acquired.

Before we get to “big move No. 2,” let’s talk about the fall-out from the first trade.

Miami immediately flips Joseph to Bay Area for James Delgado. Delgado is a better fit as a more rounded player than Joseph, and as we’re about to see Joseph isn’t done moving on the trade deadline day.

Bay Area then flipped Joseph to Las Vegas in exchange for Brandon Jacobson. LV upgrades their singles specialist in time for the playoffs, while Jacobson moves to a team where he may get some playing time.

The final move of the Trade Deadline, which was extended a few hours to allow for player notification, was a huge one: Orlando unloaded the biggest trade asset out there in Federico Staksrud, threw in their starting female Milan Rane, and moved them to New Jersey for the 5’s two bench players Martin Emmrich and Lina Padegimaite, along with cash (presumably the max allowed cash amount in trade, or $400k for the two starter-level players).

New Jersey now has an embarrassment of riches with Howells, Khlif, and Staksrud all on the same team (the rule that bars a team from having three players ranked within the top 20 is foiled thanks to Howell’s early season injury and his lack of ranking points).

The big question will be: how will Staksrud fit into this team? The answer may become obvious once we look at the component parts of the team pre move:

Clearly the problem here is with the 2026 pairing of Will Howells & Noe Khlif. After going 13-8 together last season, they’re just a .500 team, which just is not good enough in the face of the competition. The question becomes; who do you think is “responsible” for the NJ men to be just a 500 team, and who would you line up as a replacement? Staksrud plays the left, and could replace Khlif like-for-like with Howells staying on the right, and that’s what I initially suspect will happen. New Jersey may leave well-enough alone and continue to run out Waters/Khlif, making Staksrud a Men’s doubles and DreamBreaker specialist.

Speaking of DreamBreakers, take a look at the DB performance of the NJ players:

Despite being nearly a top-10 ranked singles player on tour, Khlif has been shockingly bad in DreamBreakers this season, and Staksrud will clearly step in here. New Jersey is 2-2 when going to the DreamBreaker (wins over Orlando and Dallas, losses to StL and Texas). Khlif went 2-10 against Acevedo in the Texas DB loss, and 5-7 against Goins in the StL DB loss, and Staksrud could have made the difference.

I do not suspect Rane will get into any lineup, save for injury, as the team rides or dies with its two top females.

Conclusion: NJ’s strategy is identical to LAs: get two wins from your star, try to steal one other doubles match, but if you have to go DreamBreaker lean on your top singles players. Adding Staksrud should greatly improve their Men’s team’s performance, and should greatly improve the DreamBreaker. It may just be enough to oust St. Louis, as long as NJ can counter their isolation strategy. That 1 loss in Waters/Johnson’s 25-1 record? To St.Louis and Bright/Fahey’s icing-out strategy.

As a side-effect of the Big Trade No.2, Orlando is gutted. Jack Sock now is set to play with either Greg Dow (waiver pickup, see below) or Yates Johnson, while on the female side the loss of Rane and the likely season-ending injury to Schneemann means Orlando will be playing Padegimaite and a waiver pickup or onsite stub in the final event of the season, which they’re hosting and will have them on the show court every match. I’ll be surprised of Orlando wins another game this season. Not match, game.

Orlando currently holds 12th place/the final playoff spot, but keeping that spot seems like a pipe dream now, and the 12th spot now seems like it is California’s for the taking. There are 3-4 other teams in contention for that last playoff spot, but of them: Chicago is going young, Utah just traded their best veteran, and California is the only one of this group trying.

The Final Waiver period was held on 7/21/26 with a few picks, mostly by teams upgrading or modifying their bench players.

Miami picked up the versatile and high-energy Anderson Scarpa, dropping the more singles focused Zane Ford.

California picked up Craig Johnson, dropping Aussie Joey Wild. I’m guessing here, but Wild’s travel schedule may have proven difficult to work around.

Orlando picked up broadcaster extraordinaire Greg Dow and dropped Martin Emmrich, just acquired in trade.

California then said to themselves, “hey I like Emmrich more than Johnson” and made a second waiver claim. As Jimmy Miller likes to say, “Once a Bear, always a Bear.” Well, Craig Johnson was a Bear for roughly 30 minutes.

MLP Week 8 starts up Thursday from Chicago, with a few of these teams in action, but most of these new-look lineups will not be seen until Orlando and the regular season finale.

This article was originally published on Forbes.com

title: LeBron James press conference link apparently posted in error by Miami Heat
description: The stream, titled "LeBron James Introductory Press Conference," was momentarily available on the Miami Heat's YouTube page, set for July 27, 2026.
fullText:
A scheduled livestream that was briefly visible on the Miami Heat's official YouTube channel sparked speculation that LeBron James would be returning to the team.

The stream, titled "LeBron James Introductory Press Conference," was momentarily available on the Miami Heat's YouTube page, set for July 27, 2026, USA TODAY reported.

According to Anthony Chiang, a reporter for the Miami Herald, the video was posted in error by the Heat's social media department.

"It was a mistake by the Heat's social media department while preparing for the possibility of LeBron picking the Heat. But there is no validity to it right now," Chiang wrote in a social media post.

The video has since been removed from the Heat's YouTube channel.

It's the first time James has been an unrestricted free agent since 2018. He announced he wouldn't be returning to the Los Angeles Lakers on June 30.

The Miami Heat did not immediately respond to a request for comment by the USA TODAY Network.

Sarah Perkel is a South Florida Connect Reporter for the USA TODAY Network's Florida Connect team. You can get all of Florida’s best content directly in your inbox each weekday day by signing up for the free newsletter, Florida TODAY.

This article originally appeared on Florida Today: LeBron James press conference link apparently posted in error by Miami Heat

title: Derby County’s Jacob Widell Zetterström attracts interest from Lyon
description: Olympique Lyonnais are reportedly interested in Derby County’s Jacob Widell Zetterström (28), according to information from Foot Mercato.Having joined the Rams from Djurgårdens IF in the summer of...
fullText:
Olympique Lyonnais are reportedly interested in Derby County’s Jacob Widell Zetterström (28), according to information from Foot Mercato.

Having joined the Rams from Djurgårdens IF in the summer of 2024, the Stockholm-born player has established himself as the undisputed first-choice goalkeeper.

Making 42 appearances as first-choice goalkeeper (nine clean sheets, 51 goals conceded) during his first season in England, Widell Zetterström started a further 33 times (seven clean sheets, 47 goals conceded) throughout the 2025-26 campaign, in which he was forced to sit out for several weeks.

With a contract running with Derby County until 2027, the Swede – who started against France in the round of 16 at the 2026 World Cup (3-0) – is, however, reportedly being viewed by Lyon as an option should Dominik Greif (29) or Rémy Descamps (30) leave this summer. A possibility which, for the time being, is not on the agenda. But Widell Zetterström’s profile is appealing.

title: ‘To be honest…’ – Liverpool player admits 2025/26 was a ‘very tough and strange season’
description: One Liverpool player has admitted that he found the 2025/26 season ‘very tough and strange’ and is now firmly focused on putting it behind him.The most recent campaign is one that Reds fans would ...
fullText:
One Liverpool player has admitted that he found the 2025/26 season ‘very tough and strange’ and is now firmly focused on putting it behind him.

The most recent campaign is one that Reds fans would rather forget, with their team slumping to a fifth-place finish in the Premier League, a sharp decline from the then-champions which ultimately cost Arne Slot his job.

DOWNLOAD THE OFFICIAL EMPIRE OF THE KOP APP FOR ALL THE LATEST & BREAKING UPDATES – STRAIGHT TO YOUR PHONE! ON APPLE & GOOGLE PLAY

It was also a challenging season on a personal level for Kostas Tsimikas, who was on loan at Roma but played little more than 1,000 minutes (making just six starts in Serie A) and was criticised over some of his performances.

Now back at Liverpool and involved with the pre-season camp in Chicago, the 30-year-old is determined to atone for that difficult campaign and spoke of his delight at returning to his parent club.

The ‘Greek Scouser’ told liverpoolfc.com: “I couldn’t wait [to return to LFC], to be honest. I had a very tough and strange season last year, but I’m fully here. I trained so hard alone in the off-season.

“During the last season [at Liverpool], even if I didn’t have so much game-time for some reasons, I’m really here. I’m really happy to be back, really glad and I can’t wait to wear the jersey.”

Want more Empire of the Kop coverage? Add us as a preferred source on Google to your favourites list for news you can trust

With Andy Robertson having moved on to Tottenham Hotspur, Tsimikas is now the main challenger to Milos Kerkez for the left-back berth in the Reds’ starting XI, and both of them should receive ample game-time during the pre-season fixtures.

This next month could be vital for the Greece international in terms of making his case to new head coach Andoni Iraola, with Liverpool facing a big decision on the defender’s future now that he’s into the final year of his contract.

His frustrations at Roma coincided with a dreary, morale-sapping season for his parent club, and both will be determined to put that behind him with much improved fortunes in 2026/27.

Tsimikas will be 31 next May, and reports that the Reds have a ‘concrete plan’ to try and sign Borussia Dortmund left-back Daniel Svensson would suggest that the ‘Greek Scouser’ could plausibly be coming towards the end of his time at Anfield.

For now, hopefully he can make a positive impression on Iraola in pre-season and provide valuable cover for and competition to Kerkez for a place in the starting XI, with Liverpool facing into yet another campaign of fighting on four fronts and the squad rotation issues that inevitably brings.

title: Newcastle fan group planning poignant tribute to Kevin Keegan before Liverpool game next month
description: A prominent Newcastle United fan group is planning a poignant tribute to Kevin Keegan prior to the fixture against Liverpool at St James’ Park on 23 August.Two of the clubs with whom the late, great...
fullText:
A prominent Newcastle United fan group is planning a poignant tribute to Kevin Keegan prior to the fixture against Liverpool at St James’ Park on 23 August.

Two of the clubs with whom the late, great two-time European Footballer of the Year – who died at the age of 75 on Monday after being diagnosed with cancer a few months ago – will face each other on Tyneside on the opening weekend of the Premier League season.

His death has added an emotional element to the fixture in 32 days’ time for both clubs, and his memory will be honoured before kick-off at St James’ Park.

Wor Flags, who organise fan-funded flag displays at Newcastle’s stadium, have confirmed their plans to pay tribute to Keegan on the day of the Liverpool match on Tyneside next month.

They posted on X: ‘We’ll be paying tribute to the most important man in Newcastle United’s history at our season opener against Liverpool on Sunday 23 August’, and have invited donations from anyone who wishes to contribute towards the planned flag display in his memory.

Keegan is one of the most celebrated figures in the history of both Newcastle and Liverpool, and it feels especially poignant that those two clubs should face each other in what’ll be their first competitive fixture since his death on Monday.

He illuminated Anfield for six seasons, scoring twice in the 1974 FA Cup final victory over the Magpies and bowing out with league and European Cup glory three years later.

He spent two years at St James’ Park as a player in the 1980s before returning as manager in 1992 and transforming the club’s fortunes in the subsequent five years, including a memorable challenge for the Premier League title in 1995/96.

Liverpool’s visit to Tyneside last August was played out amid a febrile atmosphere in the midst of the Alexander Isak transfer saga – he would ultimately join the Reds seven days after that fixture.

When the two clubs reconvene at St James’ Park a month from tomorrow, both sets of supporters will unite in paying tribute to a legendary figure who has a special place in the hearts of Geordies and Kopites.

title: Napheesa Collier injury update: Is Lynx star playing tonight?
description: Collier has missed Minnesota's first 27 games due to offseason ankle surgeries
fullText:
The WNBA's best team is getting a massive boost ahead of the All-Star Game. Napheesa Collier, a four-time All-WNBA selection who led Minnesota to the Finals in 2024, is listed as probable for Wednesday's game against the Seattle Storm.

Minnesota has thrived without Collier, going 21-6 including an impressive 11-2 on the road. Olivia Miles has been the driving force. The Rookie of the Year favorite is averaging 19.6 points, six assists and 4.8 assists. Natasha Howard has also been stellar with averages of 16.1 points, 8.1 rebounds and 1.8 steals.

Collier underwent surgeries on both of her ankles during the offseason. She had a right ankle procedure on New Year's Day then got her left ankle repaired in March. Collier returned to team activities on July 1, so she's had three weeks to ramp up her conditioning.

For her career, Collier averages 18.4 points, 7.8 rebounds, 1.7 steals and 1.2 blocks. Her return makes Minnesota the runaway title favorite.

Collier is expected to suit up against Seattle. Lynx coach Cheryl Reeve told reporters earlier his month to not expect much of a minutes restriction upon Collier's return.

Collier suffered injuries to both of her ankles during the 2025 season. She missed three weeks in August with a right ankle injury before tearing three ligaments in her left ankle in the WNBA semifinals. Collier wasn't able to finish the series and the Phoenix Mercury promptly eliminated the Lynx.

This article originally appeared on For The Win: Napheesa Collier injury update: Is Lynx star playing tonight?

title: Which Philadelphia Eagles rookie has the best chance to start Week 1?
description: Makai Lemon has the clearest path among Eagles rookies to start Week 1 as Philadelphia reshapes its wide receiver room
fullText:
The Philadelphia Eagles do not need to force rookies into major roles before they are ready. That is one of the benefits of having a roster built to contend immediately, with proven veterans and young starters already established across both sides of the ball. Still, every rookie class has at least one player whose opportunity is too obvious to ignore.

For the Eagles, that player is Makai Lemon.

Lemon has the clearest path among Philadelphia’s rookies to start Week 1 because of where he was drafted, what he brings to the offense, and how the wide receiver room has changed. The Eagles traded up to select the USC wide receiver with the No. 20 overall pick in the 2026 NFL Draft, adding the Biletnikoff Award winner after a final college season in which he totaled 1,156 receiving yards and 13 scrimmage touchdowns.

DeVonta Smith is the unquestioned No. 1 receiver, and the Eagles added Hollywood Brown, Dontayvion Wicks, and Elijah Moore to give Jalen Hurts more veteran options. That creates competition, but it also creates a realistic path for Lemon. Philadelphia did not move up in the first round for a player it planned to hide, and Lemon’s explosiveness, ability after the catch, and inside-out versatility give the offense a skill set that can translate quickly.

The biggest factor is health and timing. If Lemon is fully available by the end of training camp and builds trust with Hurts, he could open the season as part of Philadelphia’s top three-receiver packages. Whether that counts as a traditional “start” may depend on personnel grouping. Still, today’s NFL, a rookie receiver playing heavy snaps in 11 personnel can function as a starter even if the offense technically opens in a different formation.

Eli Stowers is the next-best candidate because of his draft status and receiving ability at tight end. The Eagles selected him in the second round with the No. 54 overall pick, and his college production at Vanderbilt suggests he can become a useful passing-game piece. Still, Dallas Goedert remains the clear leader of the tight end room, and Stowers may need time to prove he can handle the blocking and protection responsibilities that come with the position.

Markel Bell also deserves mention because offensive linemen selected in the third round are always worth monitoring. The Eagles drafted him No. 68 overall, but his clearest path to starting early likely requires an injury or an unexpected reshuffling along the offensive line. Philadelphia values tackle depth, and Bell could become important quickly, but Week 1 feels less likely than it does for Lemon.

The answer is Lemon because opportunity meets investment. He plays a position where the Eagles are reshaping roles; he brings first-round talent, and he gives Philadelphia another explosive option in a passing game built around Hurts and Smith. If Lemon stays healthy and produces throughout August, he has the best chance of any Eagles rookie to start Week 1.

This article originally appeared on Eagles Wire: Which Philadelphia Eagles rookie has the best chance to start Week 1?

title: Post Quantum Liveness, Identity, Provenance. No Biometric Identification
description: Post-quantum trust layer that proves a live human is present — no biometric templates, no central authority. Liveness gates the keys; it never enters them. Python + Swift PoC, 850+ tests - Clockwor...
fullText:
A novel machine and method that turns standard, battle-tested classical and post-quantum
cryptographic primitives into a living, presence-bound trust substrate — keys that exist
only while you're alive and present, identity without biometrics, and no central authority.

Proof-of-concept implementation of the Atlas protocol — a post-quantum, live-presence
identity substrate: one anonymous root generates unlinkable per-context pseudonyms, a
live signal times and gates the cryptography (it never enters a key), and every
feature (messaging, vault, capture, recovery, authentication) runs under one enrolled
identity + live presence.

Liveness is not identity. The live signal proves only that a person is alive and
present — and, with a second device, that both sensors are on one body right now. It
is checked by the signals' own in-the-moment coherence and cross-device correlation; it
is never matched against a stored physiological profile of a person, and Atlas keeps
no biometric template and does no biometric identification. Physiology only
times entropy and gates operations. The who is bound by the device's own Secure
Enclave biometric (Face ID / Touch ID), whose template never leaves the enclave and which
Atlas never sees or stores. (Note: the "recognition" tunnel is a Diffie–Hellman key
agreement, not face recognition.)

Maturity has three rungs: built + tested (runs in this repo, tests pass),
device-only (needs a physical iPhone — Secure Enclave, ring, camera — so it's
exercised on hardware, not in CI), and not built.

Both the Python core and the Swift AtlasCore reach built + tested:
CI runs them on every push — the backend suite (637 tests, on the pure-Python
default and the native-Ursa path) plus the Swift core/parity suite (217 tests) on
macOS. See .github/workflows/ci.yml.

This is a proof of concept, and it is honest about what is and isn't finished. Where a
seam is a stub or a layer is designed-not-built, it says so plainly below — and because it
is open source (AGPL-3.0), those are invitations to contribute, not hidden debt.

Beyond the milestone spec — also built + tested:

Crypto posture (honest): the core is post-quantum — ML-DSA-65 + Ed25519 signatures,
SPHINCS+ root, ML-KEM-768 + X25519 KEM — and persona↔persona unlinkability is hash/HKDF-derived,
so it is post-quantum too (separate identities stay unlinkable even against a quantum adversary).
The classical (not-yet-PQ) residue is narrow: the anonymous credential's same-credential multi-show
unlinkability and the discrete-log range-proof soundness, with a STARK migration path for the
latter. Not built (designed): on-network governance, the AI tiers, and the space tier
(satellites — distinct from Spaces). The economy/token is designed and its capabilities described,
but the implementation is not included here.

The Atlas invention — its architecture, mechanisms, and design decisions — is the work of the
inventor, Aun Ali. AI tools were used for research, drafting, and implementation assistance
under the inventor's direction. The ideas and the direction originate with the inventor.

AI clients used: Claude, ChatGPT, Perplexity

It is with great hope that I reveal this design and code. This project started as a return to normal workflow after a prolonged medical disability, as a desire to help other people struggling with disability and mental illness break their bonds. Med school, healing, self discovery, and helping others deal with trauma, illness, and loss has taught me that the gates to freedom lie not in any one plane, but exist dispersed and guarded selfishly, accessible only to the few wielding connection, wealth, and safety. It is difficult to spend time in a position to help others only to feel helpless in the face of apathy, abandonment, danger, disease, and poverty, and Atlas is intended to behave as a bulwark to these ghosts of a fraught past. I invite you to contact us to help us build and fulfill this vision for a brighter future for all of us.

Aun Ali, MBBS
Founder,
Clockwork Tree Inc.
